Induction of Labor 22/10/2025 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 1 By the end of this lecture students will be able to: 1. Define what IOL is. 2. List the causes of IOL. 3. Enumerate the contraindication of IOL. 4. Outline the routes of IOL 10/22/25 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 2 Objectives What is IOL? It’s the process of artificially initiating uterine contractions, prior to their spontaneous onset , with the intention to effect progressive effacement and dilatation of the cervix and ultimately delivery of the baby. 10/22/25 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 3 Indications Maternal causes: ▪ Prolonged pregnancy ▪ PROM ▪ Maternal diseases :DM , renal diseases , autoimmune diseases, HTN • Pregnancy related conditions: • Pre-eclampsia • Intrahepatic cholestasis of pregnancy. • Recurrent APH. Fetal causes: ▪ IUGR ▪ IUFD ▪ Rh- isoimmunization 10/22/25 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 4 Contraindications ❖ Major degree placenta previa ❖ Severe IUGR & fetal compromise. ❖ Severe cephalopelvic disproportion ❖ Deteriorating maternal condition with PET or cardiac diseases. ❖ Transverse lie ❖ Breech presentation is relative contraindication to IOL. ❖ Prev. 2 and more C/S , myomectomy ❖ Prev.1 C/S is relative contraindication to IOL 10/22/25 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 5 COMPLICATIONS ▪ Increased pain and need for analgesia. ▪ Uterine hyperstimulation. ▪ Fetal distress. ▪ Intrauterine infections. ▪ Uterine rupture(rare) ▪ PPH ▪ Increased risk of operative vaginal delivery and birth trauma. ▪ Failure leading to C/S 10/22/25 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 6 Prerequisites • Establish indication clearly • Informed consent • Confirmation of gestational age • Assessment of fetal size , lie & presentation • Do pelvic assessment & assesment of Bishops score 10/22/25 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 7 Bishop score: is a clinical scoring system of the cervix to quantify the degree of ripeness of cervix prior to IOL 10/22/25 TWANA A RAHIM 8 0 1 2 3 Dilatation of cervix (cm) 0 1-2 3-4 5 or more Consistency of cervix Firm Medium Soft Length of cervical canal (cm) >2 2-1 1-0.5 <0.5 Position of cervix Posterior Central Anterior Station(cm above ischial ) -3 -2 -1 or 0 Below the spine High scores >6 indicate a favorable cervix: easier , shorter induction process that results mostly in successful vaginal delivery. Low scores <6 indicate an unfavorable cervix :longer time with higher risk of failure Such women need IOL by drugs that induce cervical ripening and uterine contractions at the same time (like PGs) 10/22/25 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 9 Methods 1.Natural: • Sexual intercourse • Acupuncture/acupressure • Membrane sweeping 2.Mechanical: • Ballon catheters. • Lamineria tents. • Synthetic osmotic dilators. • Amniotomy 3.chemical: A: Non-hormonal • Herbs: evening primrose oil • Enemas • Castor oil 10/22/25 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 10 B.Hormonal: • Prostaglanin E2 ( dinoprostone) • Misoprostol (synthetic PGE1 analog) • Oxytocin • Mifepristone(anti-progesterone) • Relaxin • Nitric oxide donor 10/22/25 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 11 Conclusion • Induction of labor is a critical obstetric intervention aimed at initiating uterine contractions before spontaneous onset, to achieve vaginal delivery when the benefits of early delivery outweigh the risks of continuing pregnancy. 10/22/25 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 12 References 10/22/25 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 13 1. NICE NG207 — “Inducing labour” (published 4 November 2021). 2.Obstetrics by ten teachers 21st edition

Dr.Shayan Jalal Khalaf FIBOG College of Medicine Obstetrics Department PERINATALINFECTIONS INTRODUCTION • Important cause of still births and morbidity • Many diseases go undiagnosed • Appropriate treatment can prevent morbidity/mortality • 1971:Andres Nahmiasproposed acronym ToRCH • 1975: Harold Fuerst added Syphilis to the acronym ACRONYM:TORCHES CLAP Toxoplasmosis Other (Syphilis,Varicella Zoster, ParvovirusB- 19, HEPB) Chickenpox Lyme disease AIDS Parvovirus B19 Rubella CMV Herpes simplex Enterovirus Syphilis • Latest addition: Zika virus Toxoplasmosis • Adisease caused by an intracellular parasite Toxoplasma Gondii. • Human infection occurred by: Oocyst contaminated soil, salads, vegetables, eating raw or undercooked meat containing tissue cysts (Sheeps, pigs,….) are the most common meat sources. • Primary maternal infection in pregnancy– - Infant infection rate higher in 3rdtrimester. - Fetal death higher in 1sttrimester Signs and sympoms • Infected Pregnant women: 90% asymptomatic • Incubation period is 5–18 days following exposure. • Some may have flu‐ like illness, with lymphadenopathy and fatigue • The symptoms are usually self‐limiting • Immunocompromised pregnant women are at higher risk of developing serious complications like severe encephalitis, myocarditis, pneumonitis or hepatitis. • Similarly, infected neonates are usually asymptomatic at birth. • The classic tetrad of findings : Ø Chorioretinitis Ø Hydrocephalus Ø Intracranial calcifications Ø Convulsion Diagnosis • Serial IgG measurement (for maternal infection • Amniotic fluid PCR (for fetal infection) • Serologic testing, brain imaging, CSF analysis and ophthalmologic evaluation (for neonatal infection) • PCR testing of various body fluids or tissues Treatment In PREGNANT WOMEN with an established recent infection, SPIRAMYCIN (3g daily in divided doses) should be given. In neonates : Pyrimethamine: 50mg twice daily for 2 days then 50mg daily. PLUS Sulfadiazine: 75mg/kg/daily in two divided doses for 2 days then 50mg/kg/twice daily PLUS FolinicAcid: 10-20mg daily Prevention- counselling • Avoid raw/undercooked meat • Wash hands after gardening • Wash raw vegetables • Minimise contact with young kittens and their litter RUBELLA: • It is caused by rubella virus,Rubivirus genus and familyTogaviridae. • Intrauterine infection with rubella virus is referred to as congenital rubella infection (CRI) or syndrome. • Infection with rubella earlier in pregnancy (1st trimester ) cause worse prognosis and neonatal complications. • The virus can be transmitted to the fetus through the placenta and is capable of causing serious congenital defects, abortions, and stillbirth In the Mother • Rubella is a mild disease • There may be a mild prodromal illness involving a low‐grade fever, malaise, coryza and mild conjunctivitis • Lymphadenopathy may precede the rash and usually involves post‐auricular and sub‐ occipital glands • The rash is usually transitory, erythema tous and mostly located behind the ears and on the face and neck. Clinical diagnosis is unreliable • Transmission to the fetus occurs via maternal hematogenous spread to the placenta. • It typically occurs 5-7 days after maternal inoculation. • After the virus invades the placental barrier, it spreads throughout the fetus via their vascular system. • The congenital defects that result from infection is secondary to the cytopathical damage ensued to the blood vessels. • This in turn results in ischemia of the affected organs Fetal Manifestations: Congenital rubella syndrome (CRS) presents with one or more of the following: ●Cataracts and other eye defects ●Deafness ●Cardiac abnormalities ●Microcephaly ●Retardation of intrauterine growth ●Inflammatory lesions of brain, liver, lungs and bone marrow. □The risk of maternal-fetal transmission is the greatest in the first 10 days after gestation □ cardiac and eye defects typically resulting when maternal infection occurs prior to 8 week. □ Hearing loss is typically obse

Postpartum Haemorrhage Maryam Bakir Assistant Professor OBGYN Post partum hemorrhage (PPH) is most common cause of maternal mortality and accounts to one-quarters of all maternal death worldwide. • Definition: There is no single satisfactory definition of PPH. In general term its defined as excessive blood loss from female genital tract after delivery sufficient to affect maternal physiology (Bp, PR) excessive mean more than 500 ml after vaginal delivery and more than 1000 ml after caesarean section. Loading… Types: PPH is classified as • Primary: this means loss of blood following, but with first 24 hours, of delivery of the baby. • Secondary: this means loss occurs between 24 hours and 12 weeks post partum. primary PPH The traditional definition of primary PPH is the loss of 500 ml after normal delivery or more of blood from the genital tract within 24 hours of the birth of a baby. Loading… Primary postpartum haemorrhage (PPH) is either: •Minor – 500 ml to 1000 ml •Major – more than 1000 ml; however, this can be sub-classified into: • Moderate (1000–2000 ml) • Severe (more than 2000 ml). There is regional variation in the reported incidence of PPH. Postpartum haemorrhage, based on a definition of blood loss exceeding 500 ml, complicates up to 18% of all deliveries. Major haemorrhage, defined as blood loss in excess of 1000 ml, is estimated to complicate 1% to 5% of all deliveries Secondary PPH Secondary postpartum haemorrhage is defined as excessive vaginal bleeding from 24 hours after delivery up to 12 weeks postpartum. The reported overall incidence of secondary postpartum haemorrhage varies from 0.5% to 1.5%. Causes of PPH Causes of Primary PPH The causes of PPH can be thought of under the headings of the 4 Ts: • Tone, • Trauma • Tissue • Thrombin Causes of Secondary PPH Causes of Secondary PPH include: •Endometritis •Retained placental tissue •Sub-involution of placental site •Pseudo-aneurysms and arteriovenous malformations (rare). Add/Edit reflecndgo Pathophysiology: Myometrial contraction is the main driving force for placental separation and constriction of the blood vessels. This haemostatic mechanism is known as physiological sutures. When the placenta separates, bleeding occurs from the placental bed. Uterine atony results in a failure of the living ligature to stop the bleeding. Loading… Young and healthy women can compensate for considerable blood loss over long period, but when 40% or more of the blood volume is lost, sever shock occurs. Prevention • Risk factors for PPH may present antenatally or intrapartum and care plans should be modified accordingly. • When risk factors are identified in antenatal period women should be counselled that the safest place to give birth is in a unit with a blood bank on site. Minimising risk in antenatal period • screening for anaemia in pregnancy is recommended. • If haemoglobin is less than 11g/l at 12 weeks or less than 10,5g/l at 28 weeks, anaemia should be investigated and iron supplementation offered if required. • Parenteral iron may be considered for women who do not respond to oral iron. • All women should have their placental site determined at a mid- pregnancy anomaly scan (18+0 to 20+6). Minimising risk intrapartum • As the majority of PPH cases occur in the absence of known risk factors, routine preventative measures are important for reducing morbidity and mortality from PPH. • The following are recommended: • Active management OF 3RD STAGE OF LABOUR • should be offered to all women in the management of the third stage as this significantly reduces the risk of PPH. • Active management of 3rd stage of labor. • This consists of intervention designed to facilitate the delivery of the placenta by increasing uterine contraction and to prevent PPH by averting uterine a tony. The WHO held a technical consultation on the prevention PPH in 2006 and recommends that: 1.Active management of the third stage of labor should include administration of an uterotoni coxytocin 10 i

Prenatal diagnostic Dr. Shayan Jalal Khalaf FIBOG, MBChB College of Medicine, Sulaimani University Outline • Introduction • Indications of prenatal diagnosis • First trimester combined test • Triple test • cffDNA • Pre-Implantation diagnosis • Chorion villus sampling • Amniocentesis • Cordocentesis • Fetoskopie Introduction wide variety of screening and tests to assess health of the fetus • Using a diagnostic to: - Enable couples at risk of genetic disorders to have unaffected children - Identify potentially important intrauterine treatment if available - Plan for complications at birth - Decide to whether continue pregnancy - Discover conditions that may impact the future pregnancies Indications for prenatal diagnosis • Increased risk for chromosomal fetal abnormalities based on advanced maternal age, previous affected pregnancy • Family history of a known genetic condition or congenital abnormalities • Either of the couple affected by congenital disorder Indications • Previous history of a pregnancy affected by congenital anomalies • Maternal medical conditions, medications, congenital infections • Abnormal ultrasound findings- soft markers Methods of prenatal diagnostics Non- Invasive and invasive Non- invasive : First trimester screening Maternal serum biochemistry NIPDT(Analysis of fetal cells in maternal blood) Ultrasonography Echocardiography Doppler sonography Preeclampsia screening Pre-Implantation diagnosis Methods of prenatal diagnostics Non-Invasive and invasive • Invasive: • Chorion villus sampling • Amniocentesis • Cordocentesis • Fetoskopie First trimester combined test • A blood test : 1 pregnancy-associated plasma protein-A (PAPP- A) 2 human chorionic gonadotropin (HCG) • PAPP-A levels are lower and free beta-hCG levels are higher in trisomy 21 • PAPP-A levels and free beta-hCG levels are low in trisomy 18,13 • An ultrasound exam: Nuchal translucency(NT) frontomaxillary facial (FMF) Assessment of ductus venosus flow at 11-13 weeks' gestation Triple- Test DR:58%, FPR:3% The triple screening measures: 1 alpha-fetoprotein (AFP): high in neural tube defects, abdominal wall defects,… and low in Down syndrome. 2 human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) 3 estriol (uE3): low in Down syndrome or Edward's syndrome. Cell free fetal DNA(cffDNA) • Possible from week 9 + 0 • high sensitivity in trisomy 21 ---> •negative test ----> trisomy 21 very unlikely • Indication: a. Age>35 years old b.sonographic evidence of aneuploidy C- previous pregnancy with trisomy d- positive triple test Preimplantation genetic Diagnosis(PGD) • What is Preimplantation Genetic Diagnosis? a reproductive technology during IVF cycle . Those who might benefit most from this test couples at increased risk for chromosome abnormalities or specific genetic disorders. Chorionic villus sampling(CVS) • Chorionic villus sampling (CVS) is a prenatal test in which a sample of chorionic villi is removed from the placenta for testing. The sample can be taken through the cervix (transcervical) or the abdominal wall (transabdominal). • Implementation only after the 11 + 0 week of pregnancy, before 10 + 0 weeksà transverse fetal limb anomalies, microgenathy and microglossia • miscarriage from CVS is approximately 2%. Chorionic villus sampling(CVS) • Disadvantages: • no AFP determination in amniotic fluid possible • Mosaic formation is possible in the placenta and affects the information Amniocentesis • An amniocentesis procedure takes a sample (15–20 ml) of amniotic fluid that contains fetal cells. • Abort risk 1,9% •Implementation after the 15 + 0 weeks, before 15 + 0 weeksàincrease Foot-hand deformities And abort risk. Amniocentesis • Diagnostic: • Chromosomal analysis if the mother is >35 years old or positive family history or positive previous pregnancy • Abnormal sonomorphologic finding • To detect inherited metabolic disorders and other disorders such as muscular dystrophy. • To check the levels of Alpha feto protein to rule out neural tube defects Such as spina b

Prolonged Pregnancy and Induction of Labour 6/7/2026 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 1 • Post-term pregnancy ( prolonged pregnancy): • Is defined as a pregnancy progressing to 42 weeks (294 days) of gestation or more from the first day of the last menstrual period (WHO&FIGO definition). • The terms prolonged and post-term pregnancy are often interchanged but refers to the same condition. • Although 42weeks is the current threshold for post-term pregnancy, many clinicians currently use the term prolonged pregnancy to describe pregnancies of 41week’ gestation and beyond. • Post-date: Is a pregnancy lasting beyond the estimated date of delivery (EDD) at 40 weeks. Definitions 6/7/2026 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 2 Post-maturity syndrome -This term describe the pathologic syndrome seen in post-term fetuses in which the fetus experiences placental insufficiency and consequent IUGR. - It affect 20 % of cases of post-term pregnancy and is associated with: 1. Meconium -stained amniotic fluid. 2. Oligohydramnios. 3. Fetal distress. 4. Evidence of loss of subcutaneous fat. 5. Dry, cracked skin. All these features related to placental insufficiency. 6/7/2026 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 3 -The incidence of post-term pregnancy is about 10% depending on menstrual dating (1st day of the last menstrual period). -While use of U/S to establish gestational age lowers the incidence of post- term pregnancies from 10 % to 2-5 %. --The available evidences are strongly in support that dating by early ultrasonography alone is the most accurate method for calculating gestational age & predicting EDD. -So now it is recommended that all pregnant ladies (and certainly those who do not have regular menses), should have an ultrasound examination for gestational age determination, prior to 16 weeks gestation. Incidence 6/7/2026 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 4 Etiologic Factors • The most frequent cause is an error in dating. • When truly exists, the cause is usually unknown. • But there many risk factors like: - Anencephaly. - Placental sulphate deficiency. - Fetal adrenal hypoplasia. - Male fetus. - Previous post-term pregnancy. - Nulliparity. - White race. - Genetic predisposition. 6/7/2026 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 5 Risks of prolonged pregnancy Risks to fetus: 1. Increased risk of intra-partum fetal death (4 times ) & neonatal death (3 times) in infants born after 42 weeks compared with term fetus. 2. Oligohydramnios, meconium release and IUGR ( post-maturity syndrome). 3. Increased risk of meconium aspiration by the fetus. 4. Macrosomia (birth weight > 4kg) - Macrosomia is more common in post-term than term pregnancies . - macrosomia by itself is not bad but it affect the baby adversely by causing: -difficult labour, Shoulder dystocia(difficult delivery of shoulders) with resultant risks of orthopedic or neurologic injury. 6/7/2026 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 6 Maternal risks 1. Labour dystocia. 2. Severe perineal injury related to macrosomia. 3. Doubling in the rate of caesarean delivery. 4. A source of extreme anxiety for the pregnant woman. 6/7/2026 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 7 Are there any interventions to prevent prolonged pregnancy? -Sweeping the membranes performed as a general policy from 39 weeks onwards have been recommended to encourage spontaneous labour at term and decreased the frequency of prolonged pregnancy and the need for induction of labour. -This procedure involve passing a finger through the cervical os, sweeping it around the internal surface of the cervix and gently pushing the membrane surface away from the cervix. - It is associated with increase circulating prostaglandins. -Increased likelihood of spontaneous labour within 48 hours and reduce the need for IOL. 6/7/2026 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 8 -Although prolonged pregnancy considered at 42weeks, most women seeks advice once she passed her EDD. Options are either delivery by induction of labour or expectant management depend on: 1. Gestational age. 2. Absence/presence of maternal risk factors and / or 3. Evidence of fetal

Prolonged Pregnancy 22/10/2025 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 1 By the end of this lecture students should be able to : 1.Define prolonged pregnancy. 2.Describe the possible causes for prolonged pregnancies. 3.Explain the risks of prolonged pregnancies. 4.Outline the management plan for prolonged pregnancy. 10/22/25 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 2 Objectives Post-term pregnancy :is a pregnancy progressing to 42 weeks (294)days of gestation or more from the first day of the last menstrual period Many clinicians use the term prolonged pregnancy to describe pregnancies of 41 weeks of gestation and beyond Post-date pregnancy:is a pregnancy lasting beyond the estimated date of delivery (EDD) at 40 weeks. 10/22/25 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 3 • The incidence is around 10% • Use of u/s has lowered the incidence to 2-5% • All pregnant ladies should have an ultrasound examination for gestational age determination prior to 16 weeks of gestation 10/22/25 Dr. Soma Tahir Hawrami 4 Causes • The most frequent cause is error in dating Risk factors : ▪ Anencephaly ▪ Fetal adrenal hyperplasia ▪ Previous post term pregnancy ▪ Primiparity ▪ Genetic predisposition 10/22/25 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 5 Complications Maternal : • Labor dystocia • Severe perineal injury • Doubling in the rate of C/S • Anxiety Fetal risks : • increased risk of intra-partum fetal death & neonatal death • Post-maturity syndrome • Meconium aspiration • Macrosomia>4 kg 10/22/25 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 6 Post-maturity syndrome Is a pathological syndrome seen in post-term fetuses experiencing placental insufficiency and IUGR It affects 20% of cases and is associated with: • Meconium stained amniotic fluid • Oligohydramnios • Fetal distress • Loss of subcutaneous fat • Dry, cracked skin 10/22/25 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 7 Prevention: Membrane sweeping can be performed from 39 weeks onward to encourage spontaneous labor at term, As it increases the circulating prostaglandins. 10/22/25 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 8 Management at 40-41 weeks Immediate delivery if there is : ▪ Maternal risk factors :pre-eclampsia, DM , RH- incompatability ▪ Evidence of fetal compromise: Decreased fetal movements, abnormal NST Oligohydramnios by U/S (<5cm) For healthy uncomplicated pregnancy offer follow up until 41 weeks of gestation and then IOL at 41 weeks. 10/22/25 Dr. Soma Tahir Hawrami 9 If the mother declines IOL at 41 weeks: • Daily fetal movement counts. • Twice weekly NST and AFI measurement upto 42 weeks • Once the woman reaches 42 weeks we have to deliver her even if NST and AFI are still normal 10/22/25 Dr. Soma Tahir Hawrami 10 Management during labor and delivery: • Consider amniotomy to diagnose meconium. • Continuous CTG if meconium is present • Be prepared for shoulder dystocia and neonatal resuscitation at delivery. 10/22/25 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 11 Conclusion • effective management of post-term pregnancy requires balancing the risks of continuing pregnancy against those of intervention, with the primary goal of achieving a safe outcome for both the mother and baby. 10/22/25 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 12 References 1. NICE NG207 — “Inducing labour” (published 4 November 2021). 2.Obstetrics by ten teachers 21st edition 10/22/25 Dr.Soma Tahir Hawrami 13

Shoulder Dystocia Dr.Naz AZAD Abdullah CABOG.HDOG.MBChB Naz Azad 1 9/11/2024 Objectives • 1-Identify the risk factors for shoulder dystocia (SD) • 2-Describe a systematic team based approach to managing SD • 3-Explain the appropriate maneuvers to reduce SD using the HELPER4 mnemonic Naz Azad 2 9/11/2024 Diagnosis of Shoulder Dystocia • Definition: • Shoulder dystocia is defined as a vaginal cephalic delivery that requires additional obstetric manoeuvres to deliver the fetus after the head has delivered and gentle traction has been unsuccessful in delivering the shoulders . – Head-to-body delivery time interval of ≥60 seconds – Use of any ancillary obstetric maneuvers to affect delivery Naz Azad 3 9/11/2024 Incidence – 0.1% to 3% of all deliveries – Prediction of macrosomia by ultrasonography is modest at best – More than 50% of all SDs occur in normal birth weight infants 9/11/2024 Naz Azad 4 Shoulder dystocia: prevention/risk factors/warning signs •Prevention: diagnosis and optimal control of gestational and insulin-dependent diabetics, reduction of maternal obesity. •Careful plan for mode of delivery in women with previous shoulder dystocia delivery (recurrence rate 10–15%). •Risk factors: •macrosomia, poorly controlled gestational and insulin-dependent diabetes, maternal obesity, previous shoulder dystocia, instrumental,prolonged first stage or second stage of labour. 9/11/2024 Naz Azad 5 Turtule sign •Warning signs: •failure of restitution of head following delivery of the head, retraction of the fetal head against the perineum (analogous to a turtle withdrawing into its shell) 9/11/2024 Naz Azad 6 Impacted Shoulder Naz Azad 7 9/11/2024 Brachial Plexus Palsies • Erb Palsy – C5-C6 nerve root – Loss of sensation in arm – Paralysis of deltoid, biceps, and brachialis muscles • Klumpke Palsy – C5-C6 nerve root – C8-T1 nerve root Naz Azad 8 9/11/2024 Fetal head retracts against the perineum (turtle sign) Gentle axial traction does not affect delivery Proceed to the HELPER4 mnemonic Recognition of Shoulder Dystocia Example of Turtle Sign Naz Azad 9 9/11/2024 What is the Safe Amount of Time to Deliver? • The safe amount of time to avoid fetal acidosis caused by SD is unknown – Neonatal pH level <7 is associated with asphyxia and difficult resuscitation – Increased risk of acidosis if >5 minutes have elapsed – Each intervention takes 30 to 60 seconds and provides a safe window of time to accomplish vaginal delivery Naz Azad 10 9/11/2024 •Mnemonic for Shoulder Dystocia •Management • HELPER4 •Managing Shoulder Dystocia is a Team Effort Naz Azad 11 9/11/2024 H = Call for Help Early • After recognition of SD, activate institutional protocol – Appropriate notification • State out loud, “We have a shoulder dystocia.” – Additional nursing staff – Additional backup • Extra nursing staff • Neonatal resuscitation team • Obstetric/surgical staff • Anesthesia staff Naz Azad 12 9/11/2024 E = Evaluate and Explain • Shoulder dystocia is not a soft-tissue dystocia • Explain the situation to patient • Explain to team members that a SD is present, which shoulder is anterior, and assign roles •Episiotomy Naz Azad 13 9/11/2024 L = Legs •McRoberts Maneuver Flex maternal hips at a 90-degree angle (not out to the side) so that thighs are just touching the sides of abdomen •Effect •Straightens the lumbosacral lordosis •Increases the anteroposterior diameter of pelvis •Flexes the fetal spine •Resolves 42% of SDs Naz Azad 14 9/11/2024 P = Pressure Suprapubic pressure performed by assistant •Locking hand position similar to cardiopulmonary resuscitation •Force should act to adduct the anterior shoulder •initially apply continuous pressure, but can tIhen involve a rocking motion •Attempt for 30 to 60 seconds Copyright 2020© American Academy of Family Physicians. All rights reserved. No fundal pressure McRoberts Maneuver Combine With Suprapubic Pressure Naz Azad 15 9/11/2024 E = Enter +/- Episiotomy •Consider when additional room for the clinician’s hand is ne

Approach To Dysmenorrhea And Premenstrual Tension Dr. Huda Muhaddein FICOG,CABOG Assistant professor in gynecology and obstetrics Suleimani university /collage of medicine Women care block 2025-2026 dr. Huda Muhaddein 1 October 25 dr. Huda Muhaddein 2 Objectives : • Understand the causes, investigation and treatment of dysmenorrhoea. • Describe the common effects and management of premenstrual syndrome (PMS). October 25 dr. Huda Muhaddein 3 Dysmenorrhoea: • is defined as painful menstruation. It is experienced by 45–95% of women of reproductive age. • Primary dysmenorrhoea describes painful periods since onset of menarche and is unlikely to be associated with pathology. • Secondary dysmenorrhoea describes painful periods that have developed over time and usually have a secondary cause. October 25 dr. Huda Muhaddein 4 Aetiology of secondary dysmenorrhoea • Endometriosis: is defined as endometrial tissue lying outside the uterine cavity. • Adenomyosis:presence of islands of endometrial tissue are found deep within the underlying myometrium • Pelvic inflammatory disease . • Cervical stenosis and haematometra (rarely). October 25 dr. Huda Muhaddein 5 To ascertain the actual severity of the pain, the following questions may be useful: • Do you need to take painkillers for this pain? Which tablets help? • Have you needed to take any time off work/school due to the pain? • Some primary dysmenorrhoea is associated with flushing and nausea, which may be prostaglandin related. • It is important to distinguish between menstrual pain that precedes the period (a vital clue in endometriosis) and pain that only occurs with bleeding. October 25 dr. Huda Muhaddein 6 • Other important clues about the aetiology include pain that occurs with passage of clots, in which case medication to reduce flow may be effective. • Secondary dysmenorrhoea may be associated with dyspareunia or AUB, which may point towards a pathological diagnosis. • Certain signs associated with endometriosis include a pelvic mass (if an endometrioma is present), a fixed uterus (if adhesions are present) and endometriotic nodules (palpable in the pouch of Douglas or on the uterosacral ligaments). October 25 dr. Huda Muhaddein 7 • An enlarged uterus may be found with fibroids. • Abnormal discharge and tenderness may be seen with PID. • ‘Red flags’ in the expression of dymenorrhoea lead the clinician to suspect serious pathology and include an abnormal cervix on examination, persistent PCB or IMB, which may indicate endometrial or cervical pathology, or a pelvic mass that is not obviously the uterus. October 25 dr. Huda Muhaddein 8 Investigations: • High vaginal and endocervical swabs. • TVUSS scan endometeriosis or adenomyosis. • Diagnostic laparoscopy: performed to investigate secondary dysmenorrhoea: • when the history is suggestive of endometriosis; • when swabs and ultrasound scan are normal, yet symptoms persist; • when the patient wants a definite diagnosis or wants reassurance that their pelvis is normal. • If features in the history suggest cervical stenosis, ultrasound-guided hysteroscopy can be used to investigate further (should not be routine). • Laparoscopy for primary dysmenorrhoea should not usually be performed. October 25 dr. Huda Muhaddein 9 Management: • Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs): effective in a large proportion of women. Some examples are naproxen, ibuprofen and mefenamic acid. • Hormonal contraceptives: COCP is widely used • Progestogens, either oral (desogestrol) or parenteral (medroxyprogesterone, etonogestrel) may be useful to cause anovulation and amenorrhoea. • LNG-IUS:esp endometriosis and adenomyosis. It is often used as a first-line treatment before laparoscopy. October 25 dr. Huda Muhaddein 10 • Lifestyle changes: a low fat, vegetarian diet may improve dysmenorrhoea. exercise may improve symptoms by improving blood flow to the pelvis. • Heat: there is strong evidence to prove its benefit. • GnRH analogues: this is not a first-li

Obstetrics & Gynecolo.text: Approach to normal & abnormal Puerperium and Lactation Dr.Maryam Bakir • The puerperium refers to the 6-week period following completion of the third stage of labour, when considerable adjustments occur before return to the pre- pregnant state. • During this period of physiological change, the mother is also vulnerable to psychological disturbances, which may be aggravated by adverse social circumstances. • Adequate understanding and support from her partner and family are crucial. • Difficulty in coping with the newborn infant occurs more frequently with the first baby Physiological changes Uterine involution • Involution is the process by which the postpartum uterus, weighing about 1 kg, returns to its prepregnancy state of less than 100 g. • Immediately after delivery, the uterine fundus lies about 4 cm below the umbilicus or, more accurately, 12 cm above the symphysis pubis. • However, within 2 weeks, the uterus can no longer be palpable above the symphysis. • Involution occurs by a process of autolysis, whereby muscle cells diminish in size as a result of enzymatic digestion of cytoplasm. • Involution appears to be accelerated by the release of oxytocin in women who are breastfeeding, as the uterus is smaller than in those who are bottle-feeding. • A delay in involution in the absence of any other signs or symptoms, e.g. bleeding, is of no clinical significance. Signs: causes of delayed involution • Full bladder • Loaded rectum • Uterine infection • Retained products of conception • Fibroids • Broad ligament haematoma Genital tract changes • Following delivery of the placenta, the lower segment of the uterus and the cervix appear flabby and there may be small cervical lacerations. • In the first few days, the cervix can readily admit two fingers, but by the end of the first week it should become increasingly difficult to pass more than one finger, and certainly by the end of the second week the internal os should be closed. • However, the external os can remain open permanently, giving a characteristic appearance to the parous cervix. Lochia • Lochia is the blood-stained uterine discharge that is comprised of blood and necrotic decidua. • Only the superficial layer of decidua becomes necrotic and is sloughed off. • The basal layer adjacent to the myometrium is involved in the regeneration of new endometrium and this regeneration is complete by the third week. • During the first few days after delivery, the lochia is red; this gradually changes to pink as the endometrium is formed, and then ultimately becomes serous by the second week. • Persistent red lochia offensive associated with pyrexia suggests delayed involution that is usually associated with infection or a retained piece of placental tissue. Puerperal disorders: Perineal complications • Perineal discomfort is the single major problem for mothers, 80% complain of pain in the first 3 days after delivery,25% continuing to suffer discomfort at day 10. • Discomfort is greatest in women who sustain spontaneous tears or have an episiotomy, but especially following instrumental delivery. • Local cooling (with crushed ice, witch hazel or tap water) and topical anaesthetics, 5% lignocaine gel, provide short-term symptomatic relief. • Effective analgesia following perineal trauma can be achieved with regular paracetamol, diclofenac given rectally or orally may also be added. • Codeine derivatives are not preferable, as they have a tendency to cause constipation. • Infections of the perineum are generally uncommon. • If there is a collection of pus, drainage should be encouraged by removal of any skin sutures. • Spontaneous opening of repaired perineal tears and episiotomies is usually the result of secondary infection. • Surgical repair should never be attempted in the presence of infection; (should be irrigated twice daily and healing should be allowed to occur by secondary intention). • If there is a large, gaping wound, secondary repair should only be performed when the

new_batch/main\\1. Obstetrics & Gynecolo.file: Approach To Normal &_Abnormal Development Of Genital Tract.pdf
new_batch/main\\1. Obstetrics & Gynecolo.text: dr. Huda M Muhammad 1 Approach To Normal And Abnormal Development Of Genital Tract Dr. Huda Muhaddein FICOG,CABOG Assistant professor in gynecology and obstetrics Suleimani university /collage of medicine Women care block 2025-2026 October 25 October 25 dr. Huda M Muhammad 2 LEARNING OBJECTIVES • Understand that sexual differentiation and development begin in early embryonic life. • Understand the embryonic development and the anatomy of the perineum, the vagina, cervix and uterus, the adnexa and ovary and the bladder and ureters. • Describe the structural anomalies resulting from Müllerian tract disorders. dr. Huda M Muhammad 3 October 25 Sexual differentiation of the fetus and development of sexual organs: • The chromosomal complement of the zygote determines whether the gonad becomes a testis or an ovary (bipotential). • There are four main phases of genital development: -the indifferent gonadal phase at 4-6 wks of gestation. -the gonadal differentiation phase at 7 wk gestation. - the ductal differentiation at 9-11 wks gestation. -the external genitalia differentiation at 10-12 wks. • The development of either the testis or ovary is an active gene-directed process. dr. Huda M Muhammad 4 October 25 dr. Huda M Muhammad 5 October 25 • In the male the activity of the SRY gene (sex-determining region of the Y chromosome) causes the gonad to begin development into a testis. • The fetus has two sets of structures called the Müllerian (or paramesonephric) ducts and Wolffian (or mesonephric) ducts, which have the potential to develop into female or male internal and external genitalia respectively. dr. Huda M Muhammad 6 October 25 Development of the male sexual organs: • Testis differentiates into two cell types( Sertoli and lyedigs). • The Sertoli cells produce antiMüllerian hormone (AMH) that suppresses further development of mullerian ducts. • The Leydig cells produce testosterone that stimulates the Wolffian ducts to develop into the vas deferens, epididymis and seminal vesicles. • The testosterone is converted by the enzyme 5-alpha-reductase into dihydrotestosterone (DHT) this will happen in the external genitalia in order to virilize this external genitalia. October 25 dr. Huda M Muhammad 7 • The genital tubercle becomes the penis and the labioscrotal folds fuse to form the scrotum. • The urogenital folds fuse along the ventral surface of the penis and enclose the urethra so that it opens at the tip of the penis. Development of the female sexual organs: • In the primitive ovary granulosa cells, derived from the proliferating coelomic epithelium, surround the germ cells and form primordial follicles. • Each primordial follicle consists of an oocyte within a single layer of granulosa cells, that septate from the theca cells by basal lamina. October 25 dr. Huda M Muhammad 8 • The number of primordial follicles is reached at 20 weeks’ gestation when there are 6-7 million. • Due to atresia, at birth only 1–2 million remain. • by menarche only 300,000–400,000 are present, and by menopause none. • The development of an oocyte within a primordial follicle is arrested at the prophase of its first meiotic division. It remains in that state until it undergoes atresia or enters the meiotic process preceding ovulation. October 25 dr. Huda M Muhammad 9 • The absence of testicular AMH allows the Müllerian structures to develop and the female reproductive tract develops from these paired ducts. • At 9-11 wks the proximal two-thirds of the vagina develop from the paired Müllerian ducts, which grow in a caudal and medial direction and fuse in the midline (formation of the septum). • The midline fusion of these structures produces the uterus, cervix and upper vagina, and the unfused caudal segments form the Fallopian tubes. • Cells proliferate from the upper portion of the urogenital sinus to form structures called the ‘sinovaginal bulbs’. October 25 dr. Huda M Muhammad 10 • The Müllerian tubercles and the urogenital sinus fuse to form the vagin

October 25 dr. Huda Muhaddein 1 Approach To Primary And Secondary Amenorrhea Dr. Huda Muhaddein FICOG,CABOG Assistant professor in gynecology and obstetrics Suleimani university /collage of medicine Woman care block2025-2026 October 25 dr. Huda Muhaddein 2 October 25 dr. Huda Muhaddein 3 Amenorrhoea is defined as the absence of menstruation for more than 6 months in the absence of pregnancy in a woman of fertile age. oligomenorrhoea is defined as irregular periods at intervals of more than 35 days, with only 4–9 periods a year. primary amenorrhoea : no menstruation till 16 years of age. Secondary amenorrhoea is absence of menstruation for more than six months in a normal female of reproductive age that is not due to pregnancy, lactation or the menopause. October 25 dr. Huda Muhaddein 4 • From a clinical point of view it is probably best to classify the a etiologies of primary amenorrhoea based on the presence or absence of secondary sexual characteristics. If the Secondary sexual characteristics are normal the possible causes are: • Imperforate hymen • Transverse vaginal septum • Absent vagina and functioning uterus • Absent vagina and non-functioning uterus • XY female: androgen insensitivity • Resistant ovary syndrome • Constitutional delay October 25 dr. Huda Muhaddein 5 If the Secondary sexual characteristics are absent, so the classification will be according to the patient height as below: If the patient had Normal stature 1-Hypogonadotrophic hypogonadism • Congenital( Isolated gonodotrophin-releasing hormone deficiency, Olfactogenital syndrome) • Acquired (Weight loss/anorexia,Excessive exercise,Hyperprolactinaemia) 2-Hypergonadotrophic hypogonadism • Gonadal agenesis as XX agenesis or XY agenesis, • Gonadal dysgenesis as Turner mosaic,Other X deletions or mosaics, XY enzymatic failure • Ovarian failure • Galactosaemia October 25 dr. Huda Muhaddein 6 If the patient had Short stature 1-Hypogonadotrophic hypogonadism • Congenital as Hydrocephalus • Acquired as Trauma, Empty sella syndrome, Tumours. 2-Hypergonadotrophic hypogonadism • Turner syndrome and Other X deletions or mosaics Heterosexual development • Congenital adrenal hyperplasia • Androgen-secreting tumour • 5α-Reductase deficiency • Partial androgen receptor deficiency • True hermaphrodite • Absent Müllerian inhibitor October 25 dr. Huda Muhaddein 7 Imperforate hymen • It may present in early childhood when the infant presents with a bulging hymen behind which is a mucocele, or at puberty when there is cyclical pain due to collection of blood in the vagina (hematocolpus), may be associated difficulty with micturition and defecation. • the management will be by hymenectomy . October 25 dr. Huda Muhaddein 8 Transverse vaginal septum • The vagina fails to cannulate, the upper and lower parts of the vagina are separate. • cyclical abdominal pain due to the haematocolpos • There will be abdominal mass (hematometra or hematocolpus), The hymenal remnants are often seen separately. • Transverse vaginal septum may occur at three levels, known as a lower, middle or upper third septum. • The management is very different from imperforate hymen and very careful assessment must be made before embarking on any management strategy. October 25 dr. Huda Muhaddein 9 Absent vagina and the functioning uterus • The uterine body has developed normally but there is failure of development of the cervix and upper vagina. cyclical abdominal pain, but there is no pelvic mass to be found because there is no vagina to be distended. • retrograde menstruation occurs leading to the development of endometriosis and in some patients pelvic adhesions. Absent vagina and a non-functioning uterus(Mayer–Rokitansky–Küster–Hauser (MRKH) syndrome) • 2nd most common cause of primary amenorrhoea, Secondary sexual characteristics are normal as would be expected as ovarian function is unaffected. • Examination of the genital area discloses normal female external genitalia but a blind-ending vaginal dimple w

Obstetrics & Gynecolo.text: October 25 dr Huda Muhaddein 1 Approach To Puberty , Disorders Of Sex Development And Childhood Gynecological Disorders Dr. Huda Muhaddein FICOG,CABOG Assistant professor in gynecology and obstetrics Suleimani university /collage of medicine Women care block 2025-2026 October 25 dr Huda Muhaddein 2 Objectives • Describe the normal changes of puberty and the secondary sexual differentiation that accompanies it. • Understand the classification and causes of abnormal puberty and disorders of sexual development (DSD). • Understand the common gynecological problems happening during childhood October 25 dr Huda Muhaddein 3 Normal puberty • Puberty is the process of reproductive and sexual development and maturation that changes a child into an adult. • During childhood, the HPO axis is suppressed and levels of GnRH, FSH and LH are very low. • From the age of 8–9 years GnRH is secreted in pulsations of increasing amplitude and frequency, these are initially sleep-related, but as puberty progresses, these extend throughout the day. • . October 25 dr Huda Muhaddein 4 • This stimulates secretion of FSH and LH by the pituitary glands, which in turn triggers follicular growth and steroidogenesis in the ovary. • The oestrogen produced by the ovary then initiates the physical changes of puberty. • The exact mechanism determining the onset of puberty is still unknown, but it is influenced by many factors including race, heredity, body weight and exercise. • Kisspeptin, leptin and many other puberty activating /inhibitor genes are involved in the onset of puberty October 25 dr Huda Muhaddein 5 • The physical changes occurring in puberty are 1-breast development (thelarche). 2- pubic and axillary hair growth (adrenarche). 3- growth spurt. 4-onset of menstruation (menarche). • The first physical signs of puberty are breast budding and this occurs 2–3 years before menarche. • The appearance of pubic hair is dependent on the secretion of adrenal androgens and is usually after thelarche. October 25 dr Huda Muhaddein 6 • growth hormone secretion also increases, leading to a pubertal growth spurt. • The mean age of menarche is 12.8 years and it may take over 3 years before the menstrual cycle establishes a regular pattern. • Pubertal development was described by Tanner and the stages of breast and pubic hair development are often referred to as Tanner stages 1–5 October 25 dr Huda Muhaddein 7 Stage Pubic hair scale Breast development Stage 1 No hair No glandular breast tissue palpable Stage 2 Downy hair Breast tissue palpable under the areola (the 1sr pubertal sign in female ) Stage 3 Scant terminal hair Breast tissue palpable out side the areola; no areolar development Stage 4 Terminal hair that fills the entire triangle overlying the pubic region Areola elevation above the contour of the breast, forming a double a double scope appearance. Stage 5 terminal hair that extends beyond the inguinal crease onto the thigh Areolar mound recedes into single breast contour with areolar hyper pigmentation ,papillae development and nipple protrusion. October 25 dr Huda Muhaddein 8 Tanner staging. October 25 dr Huda Muhaddein 9 Precocious puberty • This is defined as the onset of puberty before the age of 8 in a girl or 9 in a boy. • Central precocious puberty is gonadotrophin dependent. The aetiology is often unknown, although up to 25% are due to central nervous system (CNS) malformations or brain tumours. • Peripheral precocious puberty, which is gonadotrophin independent, is always pathological and can be caused by oestrogen secretion, such as exogenous ingestion or a hormone-producing tumour. October 25 dr Huda Muhaddein 10 Delayed puberty : there are no signs of secondary sexual characteristics by the age 14 years. Its either due to : 1- hypogonadotrophic- hypogonadism: • this may be central or constitutional, but other causes may be excluded. These include anorexia nervosa, excessive exercise and chronic illness, such as diabetes or renal. • Rarer causes

WRITE THE TOPIC ASSISTED VAGINAL BIRTH Maryam Bakir Assistant Professor 10/27/2025 MARYAM BAKIR 1 Objective • Understand the indications, contraindications, procedures and complications of assisted vaginal birth with vacuum or forceps. 10/27/2025 MARYAM BAKIR 2 Introduction • Assisted vaginal birth (AVB) refers to a vaginal birth with the use of any type of forceps or vacuum extractor (ventouse). • The terms instrumental delivery, operative vaginal delivery and AVB are used interchangeably. • The goal of AVB is to expedite the birth with a minimum of maternal or neonatal morbidity. 10/27/2025 MARYAM BAKIR 3 • As with other forms of intra-partum intervention, AVB should be performed only when the safety criteria have been met and when the benefits outweigh the risks. • AVBs should be conducted by obstetricians with competency in the chosen procedure or by trainees under direct supervision of an experienced trainer. When these conditions are adhered to, the outcomes of AVB are good. 10/27/2025 MARYAM BAKIR 4 Incidence: • In the UK, between 10% and 15% of deliveries are assisted with forceps or ventouse. 10/27/2025 MARYAM BAKIR 5 Indications Fetal • Suspected fetal compromise (pathological cardiotocograph, abnormal pH or lactate on fetal blood sampling, meconium) • Fetal and maternal indications for assisted vaginal birth often coexist 10/27/2025 MARYAM BAKIR 6 Indications • Maternal • Prolonged second stage - Nulliparous women: lack of continuing progress for 3 hours (total of active and passive second stage of labour) with regional anaesthesia or 2 hours without regional anaesthesia - Multiparous women: lack of continuing progress for 2 hours (total of active and passive second stage of labour) with regional anaesthesia or 1 hour without regional anaesthesia • Maternal exhaustion/distress • Medical indications to avoid prolonged pushing or Valsalva (e.g. cardiac disease, hypertensive crisis, cerebral vascular disease, uncorrected cerebral vascular malformations, myasthenia gravis, spinal cord injury) • Combined 10/27/2025 MARYAM BAKIR 7 10/27/2025 MARYAM BAKIR 8 Criteria Full abdominal and vaginal examination : 1- Head is fully engaged (zero fifths palpable) or no more than one-fifth palpable per abdomen 2- Cervix is fully dilated and the membranes ruptured 3- Station at level of ischial spines or below (0/+1/+2/+3) 4- Exact position of the head has been determined so correct placement of the instrument can be ̨achieved 5- Caput and moulding is no more than moderate (+ or ++) 6- Pelvis is deemed adequate 10/27/2025 MARYAM BAKIR 9 criteria Preparation of the mother: 1- Clear explanation given and informed consent obtained 2- Appropriate anaesthesia is in place; for mid-pelvic rotational delivery this will usually be a regional block; a pudendal block may be appropriate, in the context of urgency; a perineal block may be suffcient for low-pelvic or outlet delivery 3- Maternal bladder has been emptied recently In-dwelling catheter has been removed or balloon defated 4- Aseptic technique 10/27/2025 MARYAM BAKIR 10 criteria Preparation of staff : 1- Operator has the knowledge, experience and skill necessary 2- Adequate facilities are available (appropriate equipment, bed, lighting) and access to an operating theatre 3- Back-up plan in place in case of failure to deliver. senior obstetrician should be present if a junior obstetrician is conducting the delivery 4- Anticipation of complications that may arise (e.g. shoulder dystocia, post-partum haemorrhage) 5- Personnel present that are trained in neonatal resuscitation 10/27/2025 MARYAM BAKIR 11 • For forceps, all of the usual prerequisites for safe AVB apply, but, in addition, it is essential that the operator checks the pair of forceps to ensure that a matching pair has been provided and that the blades lock with ease (both before and after application). 10/27/2025 MARYAM BAKIR 12 ASSESSMENT • A thorough abdominal and vaginal examination should take place to confirm the fetal lie,

Caesarean section scultetus_small Maryam Bakir Assistant Professor Objectives ◼Understand the indications, procedure, complications and consequences of caesarean section. ◼Introduce the concept of risk management in relation to operative delivery. ◼A caesarean section, also known as C- section or Caesar, is a surgical procedure in which incisions are made through a mother’s abdomen (laparotomy) and uterus (hysterotomy) to deliver one or more babies, placenta and membranes. ◼It usually indicated when vaginal delivery poses a risk for either the mother or the baby. This operation has been part of human culture since ancient time. Initially it was performed to retrieve the infant from a dead or dying mother. Incidence: ◼Depending on the population and the facilities available the incidence range between 10 and 25%, this difference may be explained by the difference in the demographic and clinical characteristic like maternal age, ethnicity,prior CS, breech presentation... ◼In the UK, more than 21% of all deliveries are by C-section. ◼The WHO ranges are between 10- 15%.The Scandinavian countries have managed to hold the rate at this level with the same outcome or even better than those with higher rate. ◼More recently it has been suggested that there is maternal and neonatal benefit with caesarean section rates up to 19% Factors that may contribute to an increase in the rate of C/S: ◼In accurate dating of pregnancy. ◼Fetal monitoring. ◼Macrosomia . ◼Maternal request. Types of C/S: Emergency: immediate threat to mother and fetus. Urgent: maternal or fetal compromise but not immediately life threatening. Scheduled: need early delivery but no maternal or fetal compromise. Elective: at a time to suit the patient and the maternity team. Perimortem: carried out in extremis while the mother is undergoing active resuscitation. Post-mortem: carried out after the death of the mother in order to save the fetus. Category 1 or emergency cs ◼Ideally, CS should be done within the next 30 min. ◼examples include abruption, cord prolaps, scar rapture, scalp blood pH < 7.20, and prolonged FHR deceleration below 80. Category 2 or urgent cs ◼The delivery should be completed within 60-75 min.examples include those with FHR abnormalities of concern. Category 3 or scheduled ◼There is concern that continuation of pregnancy is likely to affect the mother or fetus in hours or days. ◼This group has a wide rang of indications. Examples include failure to progress when CS is planned in next few hours, an IUGR fetus in preterm period with absent EDF but normal CTG, or pre-eclampsia where LFT or RFT are gradually deteriorating and CS is planned within days. ◼Timing would vary but some plan should be in place to deliver before further deterioration occurs. Category 4 or elective cs ◼These are cases where there is an indication for CS but there is no urgency. Examples include PP with no active bleeding, breech presentation, previous hysterotomy or vertical incision, past history of repair of VVF. ◼Elective CS is generally done around 39 weeks as the incidence of tachypnoea of the newborn is much less after this gestation, however the medical or clinical indication determine time of CS Indications: There are many different reasons for performing a delivery by C/S; however absolute indications are very few. Whatever indications, the principle is that whenever the risk from the vaginal delivery exceeds that of C/S, operation should be done. Current pregnancy events Significant fetal disease likely to lead to poor outcome like IUGR. Maternal medical disorder like pre-eclampsia. Monoamniotic twin. Complete placenta praevia. Active primary herpes. Malpresentation like transverse lie or breech presentation. Macrosomia (baby is too large). Past obstetric history: ◼Previous classical C/S. ◼Secondary pelvic floor or anal sphincter injury. ◼Previous sever shoulder dystocia with significant neonatal injury. Intrapartum events: ◼Maternal distress. ◼Fetal distress

COAGULATION DISORDERS IN PREGNANCY Coagulation disorder in the pregnancy Dr. Massooma Mahmood Mohammed GBCOG,MB.CH.B College of medicine, Sulaimania university 2023-2024 Hematological changes in normal pregnancy • Increase WBC • Increase V,VII,VIII,IX,X,XII, fibrinogen, VWF • Antithrombin III & protein C steady or decrease • Decrease protein S,PAI(plasmenogen activator inhibiter • Platelet decrease(little change) Venous Thromboembolism in Pregnancy: Venous thromboembolism (VTE) refers to the formation of a thrombus within veins. -This can occur any where in the venous system but the clinically predominant sites are: in the vessels of the leg (giving rise to deep vein thrombosis, DVT) and in the lungs (resulting in a pulmonary embolus, PE). : Predisposing factors to VTE • Activation of blood coagulation • Venous stasis • Endothelial injury (Virchow's triad) Epidemiology • In pregnancy risk of VTE increase to 1/1000 & is greatest in puerperum Pregnancy is associated with 10 fold increase compared with the risk for non pregnant women RISK factors for VTE & PTE • 1.pre-existing • Previous VTE • Thrombophilia (antithrombin III deficiency, factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene variant, protein C or S deficiency • Anti-phospholipid syndrome • Medical disorders (nephrotic syndrome, IBD) • Sickle cell disease, gross varicose veins • Age >35,BMI >30, parity >4 RISK factors for VTE & PTE • 2-New onset or transient • Surgical procedure in pregnancy or puerperum • OHSS • Hyperemisis, severe dehydration, blood loss • Long haul travel, immobility >4days bed rest • Pre-eclampsia • Prolong labour, midcavity instrumental delivery • Cesarean section, immobility after delivery • Sever infection Factors specific to pregnancy -multiple pregnancy. -Pre-eclampsia . -Grand multiparity. -Instrument-assisted or Caesarean delivery. -Hemorrhage. -damage to the pelvic veins. -Prolonged labour. -Sepsis. Deep vein thrombosis: Presentation : -The most common symptoms are pain in the calf with varying degrees of redness or swelling. -women’s legs are often swollen in pregnancy but unilateral symptoms should give an alarm. -Signs are few except that often the calf is tender to gentle touch. -It is mandatory to ask about symptoms of PE as a woman with PE might present first with DVT. -The difficulty is that some of these symptoms may be found in normal pregnancies. -The patient may also be asymptomatic with a retrospective diagnosis being made following a PE. Investigations and diagnosis 1.D-dimers: -VTE is associated with increased levels of blood D- dimers and this is often used as a screening test in non-pregnant individuals. -However levels of D- dimers are increased in uncomplicated pregnancy and levels increased with advancing pregnancy. -A positive D- dimer screen is of no prognostic significance in VTE but a negative D- dimer in pregnancy means no VTE (exclude VTE). -This has a high sensitivity and specificity in proximal DVTs and is non-invasive. -It is unreliable for calf DVT as it has a much lower sensitivity. -If the initial ultrasound scan is negative and there is low level of clinical suspicion, anticoagulant treatment can be stopped. -If the ultrasound is negative and there is high clinical suspicion, the patient should be anti- coagulated and the ultrasound repeated in one week, or venography performed. 2.Duplex ultrasound: 3.Venography: -Venography is invasive, requiring the injection of contrast medium and the use of x-ray. -it does allow excellent visualization of veins both below and above the knee. -But there is risks of radiation, allergic reaction and 5 percent risk of causing thrombosis. Pulmonary embolism- PE - This can occur with or without preceding DVT. -Symptoms range from minimal disturbance to sudden collapse and death, depending on the size, number and site of emboli. Clinical features: -It is crucial to recognize PE, as missing the diagnosis could have fatal implication. The most common presentation is: -mild dyspnea, -inspiratory ch

Multiple pregnancy Dr. Massooma Mahmood Mohammed GBCOG,MB.CH.B College of medicine, Sulaimania university 2023-2024 In general terms, multiple pregnancies consist of two or more fetuses. Twins make up the vast majority (nearly 99 per cent) of multiple gestations. Pregnancies with three or more fetuses are referred to as ‘higher multiples’. twins currently account for approximately 1.5 per cent of all pregnancies Rates of multiple pregnancies constitute approximately 3% of live births. The incidence of monozygotic or identical twins is generally accepted to be constant at 1 in 250. It is not influenced by race, family history or parity. Definition Risk factors for multiple gestations include (dizygotic twins) Previous multiple pregnancy Family history (maternal side) Increasing maternal age Racial origin (more common in women of West African ancestry, less common in those of Japanese ancestry) Assisted conception Risk Factors ❖Dizygotic twins arise spontaneously from the release of two eggs at ovulation. This tendency to release more than one egg can be familial or racial in origin and increases with maternal age. ❖Ovulation induction treatments may also cause the release of more than one egg. ❖With assisted conception techniques such as IVF, two or more embryos fertilized in the laboratory may be replaced in the uterus, although single embryo transfer is becoming more frequent. Aetiology The classification of multiple pregnancy is based on: number of fetuses: twins, triplets, quadruplets, etc. • number of fertilized eggs: zygosity • number of placentae: chorionicity • number of amniotic cavities: amnionicity classification Non- identical Twins Non-identical or fraternal twins are dizygotic, having resulted from the fertilization of two separate eggs. they always have two functionally separate placentae (dichorionic). always have separate amniotic cavities (diamniotic) and the two cavities are separated by a thick three-layer membrane(fused amnion in the middle with chorion on either side). The fetuses can be either same-sex or different sex pairings. Identical twins are monozygotic – they arise from fertilization of a single egg and are always same-sex pairings. They may share a single placenta (monochorionic) or have one each (dichorionic). The vast majority of monochorionic twins have two amniotic cavities (diamniotic) but the dividing membrane is thin, as it consists of a single layer of amnion alone. Monochorionic twins may occasionally share a single sac (monoamniotic) Identical Twins ❖Monozygotic twins arise from a single fertilized ovum that splits into two identical structures. ❖The type of monozygotic twins depends on how long after conception the split occurs. ❖If splitting is delayed beyond day 12,the embryonic disc has also formed, and conjoined, or ‘Siamese’ twins will result. Identical Twins All the physiological changes of pregnancy (increased cardiac output, volume expansion, relative hemodilution, diaphragmatic splinting, weight gain,) are exaggerated in multiple gestations. Miscarriage and severe preterm delivery Spontaneous preterm delivery is an ever-present risk in any twin pregnancy the average gestation at delivery is 37 weeks. about half of all twins deliver preterm.  Increased risk for pre-eclampsia, hypertension, gestational diabetes and FGR, APH, PPH and thromboembolic disease. Monochorionic monoamniotic (MCMA) twins have increased risk of congenital anomalies including neural tube defects and abdominal wall and urinary tract malformations. Complications relevant to twin pregnancy Twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome (TTTS) and, more rarely, twin anaemia–polycythaemia sequence (TAPS) The death of one of a monochorionic twin pair may result in either death or handicap of the co-twin because of acute hypotension secondary to placental vascular anastomoses between the two circulations. Compared to singletons, twin pregnancies carry at least twice the risk of the birth of a baby with

Partograph Dr. Sallama Kamel - The partograph is a graphical presentation of the progress of labour, and of fetal and maternal condition during labour. It is the best tool to help you detect whether labour is progressing normally or abnormally, and to warn you as soon as possible if there are signs of fetal distress or if the mother's vital signs deviate from the normal range. - You need to monitor a labouring mother carefully. Remember that a labour that is progressing well requires your help less than a labour that is progressing abnormally. Documenting your findings on the partograph during the labour enables you to know quickly if something is going wrong, and whether you should refer the mother to the nearest higher health facility or hospital for further evaluation and intervention. - Write the name and age of the mother, her 'gravida' (number of pregnancy regardless the outcomes, the current pregnancy should be included ) and 'para' (number of births after 24. Weeks of gestation including alive or dead fetus) status, her health facility or hospital registration number, the date and time when you first attended her for the delivery, and the time the fetal membranes ruptured (her 'waters broke'). Fetal heart rate - The fetal heart rate is recorded at the top of the partograph every 30 min in the first stage of labour and every 5 minutes in the second stage. - Count every five minutes if the amniotic fluid (called liquor on the partograph) contains thick green or black meconium. - Whenever the fetal membranes rupture, you should check the heart rate because occasionally there may be cord prolapse and compression, or placental abruption as the amniotic fluid gushes out. - The normal fetal heart rate at term (37 weeks and more) is in the range of 110–160 beats/minute Each square for the FHR on the partograph represents 30 minutes. At first 30 min HR was 120 After 30 min HR became 130 After another 30 min HR was 120 Moulding • The five separate bones of the fetal skull are joined together by sutures, which are quite flexible during the birth, and there are also two larger soft areas called fontanels. Movement in the sutures and fontanels allows the skull bones to overlap each other to some extent as the head is forced down the birth canal by the contractions of the uterus. The extent of overlapping of fetal skull bones is called moulding, and it can produce a pointed or flattened shape to the baby's head when it is born • Whenever you detect moulding in the fetal skull as the baby is moving down the birth canal, you have to be more careful in evaluating the mother for possible disproportion between her pelvic opening and the size of the baby's head. • Make sure that the pelvic opening is large enough for the baby to pass through. • A small pelvis is a frequent cause of prolonged and obstructed labour •To identify moulding •First palpate the suture lines on the fetal head and appreciate whether the following conditions apply. The skull bones that are most likely to overlap are the parietal bones, which are joined by the sagittal suture, and have the anterior and posterior fontanels to the front and back. • -If the bones are separated and the sutures can be felt easily, degree 0. • -If sutures apposed: This is when adjacent skull bones are touching each other, but are not overlapping. This is called degree 1 moulding (+1). • -If sutures overlapped but reducible: This is when you feel that one skull bone is overlapping another, but when you gently push the overlapped bone it goes back easily.This is called degree 2 moulding (+2). • - If sutures overlapped and not reducible: This is when you feel that one skull bone is overlapping another, but when you try to push the overlapped bone, it does not go back. This is called degree 3 moulding (+3) the baby should be delivered at this degree. • If you find +3moulding with poor progress of labour (there is no or small head descent), this may indicate that the labour is at increased risk

PERINEAL INJURIES AND REPAIR Maryam Bakir Assistant Professor 10/27/2025 MARYAM BAKIR 1 Objective • Understand the assessment and management of perineal tears. • Understand the assessment and management of episiotomy. 10/27/2025 MARYAM BAKIR 2 INTRODUCTION • The most common form of operative intervention is suturing of a perineal tear or episiotomy. • 85% per cent of women who have a vaginal birth will have some degree of perineal trauma and 60–70% will require suturing. 10/27/2025 MARYAM BAKIR 3 INTRODUCTION • Therefore the first important step following the birth of a baby and the delivery of the placenta is to assess the woman carefully to identify and classify any perineal tearing. • A complete assessment includes both vaginal and rectal examination. 10/27/2025 MARYAM BAKIR 4 Classification of perineal tears • First-degree tears are lacerations of the skin or vaginal epithelium only. • Second-degree tears involve perineal muscles and includes episiotomy. 10/27/2025 MARYAM BAKIR 5 Classification of perineal tears 10/27/2025 MARYAM BAKIR 6 Classification of perineal tears • Third-degree tears involve any part of the anal sphincter complex (external or internal): 3a: less than 50% of the external anal sphincter is torn 3b: more than 50% of the external anal sphincter is torn 3c: the tear involves the internal anal sphincter and/or complete disruption of the external sphincter • Fourth-degree tears involve the anal sphincter complex extending into the rectal mucosa. 10/27/2025 MARYAM BAKIR 7 OASIs • Third- and fourth-degree tears are grouped together and termed obstetric anal sphincter injuries (OASIs). • OASIs are reported in approximately 3% of nulliparous and 0.5% of multiparous individuals. • In general terms, external anal sphincter incompetence causes faecal urgency, whereas internal anal sphinc- ter incompetence causes faecal incontinence. 10/27/2025 MARYAM BAKIR 8 Risk factors Perineal tears occur more commonly with • pro- longed labour, especially prolonged pushing, • big babies, • malposition such as occipito-posterior and • in association with forceps or vacuum-assisted delivery. 10/27/2025 MARYAM BAKIR 9 Preventions • Perineal massage can be performed in the ante- natal period and this may reduce the risk or extent of tearing. • Similarly, the use of warm compresses, gentle perineal support and controlled crowning of the fetal head may reduce trauma at the time of birth. 10/27/2025 MARYAM BAKIR 10 Repair • First-degree tears or minor lacerations with minimal or no bleeding may not require surgical repair and will heal spontaneously. • Second-degree tears and episiotomies require surgical repair. 10/27/2025 MARYAM BAKIR 11 SURGICAL TECHNIQUE • An explanation should be provided to the mother and verbal consent should be documented or, if a complex tear requires repair in an operating theatre, written consent should be gained. • Adequate analgesia should be provided by infltration with local anaesthetic or topping up an epidural. • The operator should recheck the extent of lacerations with a vaginal and rectal examination. 10/27/2025 MARYAM BAKIR 12 SURGICAL TECHNIQUE • Sometimes, the anatomy is distorted and assessment is improved following positioning in lithotomy with effective anaesthesia. • If a tear is more complex than initially appreciated, a more experienced operator may be required. • It may be helpful to place a pad or tampon high in the vagina to prevent blood loss from the uterus obscuring the view. 10/27/2025 MARYAM BAKIR 13 SURGICAL TECHNIQUE • Care needs to be taken that this is removed at the end of the procedure. • The vaginal mucosa is repaired first using rapidly absorbable suture material on a large, round body needle. • A knot should be tied above the apex of the cut or tear and a continuous stitch should be used to close the vaginal mucosa. • Interrupted sutures are then placed to close the muscle layer. 10/27/2025 MARYAM BAKIR 14 SURGICAL TECHNIQUE • Closure of the perineal skin follows with ei

Dr. Massooma Mahmood Mohammed GBCOG,MB.CH.B College of medicine, Sulaimania university 2023-2024 Blood group is defined in two ways: 1-ABO group, allowing four different permutations of blood group ( O, A, B , AB ). 2-Rhesus system, which consists of C, D and E antigens. 3.Also non Rhesus groups such as Kell , Duffy (Fy)and Kidd (Jk) exsist The importance of these blood group systems occur when there is a missmatch between the fetus and mother when fetal red cells pass across to maternal circulation, as they do during pregnancy . *Sensitization of the maternal immune system to these fetal ( foreign) red blood cells may occur and subsequently give rise to the haemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn (HDFN). . The Rhesus system is coded on two adjacent genes, which sit within chromosome one . One gene codes for antigen polypeptides C/c and E/e while the other codes for the D polypeptide (Rhesus antigen). Notes that the d (little d) antigen has not been identified so it may be that women who are D negative lack the antigen altogether. Antigen expression is usually dominant , whereas those who have a negative phenotype are either homozygous for the recessive allele or have a deletion of that gene. In practice, only anti-D and anti-c regularly cause HDFN ( haemolytic disease of fetus and newborn and anti-D is much more common than anti-c. Occurrence of HDFN as a result of Rhesus isoimmunization involves three key stages : 1-First, a Rhesus negative mother must conceive a baby who has inherited the rhesus positive phenotype from the father . 2-second, fetal cell must gain access to the maternal circulation in a sufficient volume to provoke a maternal antibody response . 3-finally, maternal antibody must gain transplacental access and cause immune destruction of red cells in the fetus. Fetal RBC cross to maternal circulation Maternal immune system recognizes foreign antigens if fetus Rh + and mother Rh – Antibodies are formed against fetal antigens Subsequent pregnancy with Rh+ fetus, immune system activated and large amounts of Ab formed IgG Ab cross placenta & attack fetal RBC Fetal anemia, hydrops, etc -Rhesus disease does not affect a first pregnancy. as the primary response is weak and consists of IgM antibodies that do not cross the placenta . there after IgG antibodies are produced and cross the placenta . -Rhesus antigen are well expressed by the fetus as early as 30 days gestation . so in a subsequent pregnancy, when maternal resenstization occurs ( rhesus positive red cells pass from the baby to the maternal circulation ) IgG antibodies cross from the mother to the fetal circulation. -If these antibodies are present in sufficient quantities fetal haemolysis may occur ,leading to severe anaemia that the fetus may die unless transfusion is performed. Pregnancy loss : ectopic pregnancy spontaneous abortion elective abortion fetal death (any trimester) Procedure : chorionic villous sampling amniocentesis fetal blood sampling evacuation of molar pregnancy Delivery :abdominal trauma placental abruption unexplained vaginal bleeding during pregnancy manual placental removal external cephalic version 1-The prevalence of D Rhesus negativity is 15% in the Caucasian population 2-It is very uncommon in orientals. 3-Rhesus disease is most common in countries where anti-D prophylaxis is not widespread, as in middle east and Russia 1- Polyhydraminios. 2- Enlarged fetal heart. 3- Ascites and pericardial effusion. 4- Reduced fetal movements. 5- Abnormal CTG with reduced variability, eventually a ,sinusoidal trace. 6- Hyperdynamic fetal circulation can be detected by ultrasound by measuring increased velocities in the middle cerebral artery or aorta. A hydropic neonate under extensive intensive care ❖Once a women who is D Rhesus negative has been sensitized to the D Rhesus antigen , no amount of anti-D will ever turn the clock back . ❖In a subsequent pregnancy , close surveillance is required . ❖Rhesus disease gets worse with successive pr
