Abdominal Pain in Second and Third Trimester Presented by Polan, Farzana, Sima, Muhammad, Lawand Introduction • Abdominal pain is one of the most common complaints in late pregnancy • Pain can originate from pregnancy-related, gynecological, gastrointestinal, urinary, or musculoskeletal systems • Identifying the cause is challenging because pregnancy changes can mimic disease • Important to differentiate between benign/physiological and life-threatening causes Abdominal Pain In Pregnancy Abdominal pain in pregnancy is often due to a benign cause ,while there are many harmless causes but there may be serious cause. Abdominal pain in pregnancy may be a normal part of the process as body changes as to accommodate growing baby. Physiology • Pain is a protective mechanism. It occurs whenever tissues are stretched, compressed, inflamed, or damaged. These changes activate nociceptors, which are pain receptors found in the uterus, ligaments, peritoneum, and abdominal organs. • Three types of stimuli cause pain: mechanical, such as stretching of the round ligaments; thermal; and chemical, such as prostaglandins and inflammatory mediators. • During pregnancy, mechanical stretching and chemical inflammation are the most common reasons for abdominal pain. Two types of nerve fibers carry pain: • A-delta fibers (Fast pain) - Myelinated → fast conduction - Sharp, localized pain - Example: sudden round ligament stretching • C fibers (Slow pain) - Un myelinated → slow - Dull, diffuse, aching pain - Example: uterine stretching or Gl discomfort • Pregnancy abdominal pain is mostly C-fiber mediated → dull, gradual onset, poorly localized. Obstetric Causes We classified the pain to physiological and pathological causes 1) Physiological: benign, mild, intermittent, self-limiting pain not a sign of serious complication; related to anatomical and hormonal changes during pregnancy 1 Round ligament pain 2 Uterine stretching 3 Braxton hicks contraction 4 Digestive changes 5 Pelvic pressure ligament stretch 2) Pathological cause :Pathological pain indicates a potentially serious problem and requires evaluation we can subdivide to ( obstetrical and Non obstetrical cause ) According to obstetrical cause they are pregnancy related 1- Preterm labor (most common in second trimester) (regular uterine contraction cause cervical dilation befor 37week cause back pain then radiate to lower abdomen in Supra pubic region . 2 - Placental abruption (common in both second and third trimester) (characteristic presentation of placental abrup- tion is that of painful bleeding associated with a tense rigid abdomen and uterus hard like a wooden board 3 - Uterine rupture (most common in third trimester) ( usually occur in scared uterus previous c- section it cause sudden generalized abdominal pain due to tear wall thickness of the uterine wall 4 - Pre eclampsia ( Hypertension after 20 weeks + organ dysfunction. Mechanism of pain:Liver capsule stretching from hepatic edema. 5 - HELLP syndrome ( is an acronym for hemolysis I elevated liver enzyme and low platelet present with epigastric pain nausea and vomiting associated with serious complication like acute renal failure placental abruption still birth ) 6 - Acute fatty liver in pregnancy ( cause epigastric pain and right upper quadrant pain due to liver swelling and metabolic failure ) 7 - Acute polyhydramnios ( most often occurs in second trimester present with rapid distension of the uterus, causing maternal pain and breathlessness.Mainly Upper Abdomen (Epigastric & RUQ/LUQ) 8 - Chorioamnionitis intra-amniotic infection usually due to bacterial infection and cause Lower abdomen pain and generalized uterine tenderness 9 - Pyelonephritis Kidney infection typically due to bacteria infection and cause Flank pain (R or L), may radiate to RUQ/back/lower abdomen ECTOPIC ectopic Ovarian cyst rupture Threatened miscarriage Ovarian torsion Pelvic inflammatory disease Intestinal / GIT: Appendicitis Pancreatitis Bowel obstruction Hern

Abnormal Uterine Bleeding and Endometrial Sampling Prepared By: Aro Dana, Lava Wrya, Kizhe Kawa, Rozhi Othman Objectives of This Seminar Are: 4. Understand Biopsy Techniques 2. Recognise Key Clinical Features and Diagnostic Steps 3. Understand the Indications for Endometrial Sampling 1. Define AUB & Understand its Classification What is Abnormal Uterine Bleeding (AUB)? o AUB: Is a broad term for uterine bleeding that’s abnormal in its: Frequency, Regularity, Duration, or Volume. o It replaces older terms like menorrhagia or metrorrhagia. o AUB is a Symptom and NOT a Diagnosis. o Classiﬁed by FIGO system to Structural and Non-Structural causes. o AUB aﬀects up to 30% of women in their reproductive age. Structural Causes Polyp Malignancy Leiomyoma Adenomyosis PALM Non-Structural Causes Coagulopathy Not Classified Iatrogenic Endometrial Ovulatory Dysfunction COEIN Patient Presentation • Heavy Menstrual Bleeding (HMB): Excessive menstrual blood lose which interferes with a woman’s life in Physical, Emotional, Social and Material aspects of life. • Intermenstrual Bleeding (IMB): Bleeding between expected cycles. • Post-Coital Bleeding (PCB): Bleeding after intercourse. • Post-Menopausal Bleeding (PMB): Bleeding >12 months after the last menstrual period. Clinical Evaluation - History Important Points to be asked while taking History: o Pregnancy Status: Always Exclude ! o Nature of Bleeding: Frequency, Duration, Volume, Intermenstrual or Postcoital Bleeding o Impact: How does it aﬀect her QoL? o Associated Symptoms: Pain, Pressure, Discharge, Infertility o Medical History: PCOS, Thyroid, Bleeding Disorders. o Drug History: Tamoxifen, Anticoagulants, Contraception o Cervical Screening: Is it up to date? Clinical Evaluation - Examination � Vital Signs � Inspection � Abdominal Palpation � Speculum Examination � Bimanual Examination Initial Investigations �Pregnancy Test �Full Blood Count �Coagulation Proﬁle Only if HMB started at menarche or there’s a family/personal history for clotting disorders �Do NOT do routine hormonal (FSH, LH, etc.) or thyroid tests unless there are other symptoms of thyroid disease or PCOS (e.g., irregular cycles, hirsutism). Imaging ❖ Transvaginal Ultrasound (TVUS) is the ﬁrst-line imaging for AUB if: • The uterus is palpably enlarged. • The history or exam suggests a structural abnormality (PALM). ❖ Outpatient Hysteroscopy if intrauterine pathology is suspended. ❖ MRI if ultrasound was inconclusive. What is Endometrial Sampling It is to obtain a tissue sample (histology) from the endometrial lining. To allow a pathologist to examine the cells and diagnose: • Benign ﬁnding (e.g., secretory/proliferative endometrium, polyp). • Endometrial Hyperplasia (pre-cancerous changes). • Endometrial Carcinoma (cancer). Endometrial Sampling Indications for Endometrial Sampling • Any PMB. • Persistent IMB. • HMB Unresponsive to Medical Treatment. • Suspicious TVUS Findings: Thickened Endometrium >4mm. Irregular Endometrial lining. Polypoid Mass. • High-Risk Patients, e.g. : Tamoxifen, Obesity, Nulliparity, Lynch syndrome, PCOS. Pipelle Endometrial Biopsy Procedure: • Done in an outpatient clinic, • No general anaesthesia (GA). Advantages ● High diagnostic accuracy for diﬀuse cancer (>98%). ● Quick, low-cost, avoids GA. Disadvantages ● Can be painful. ● Risk of "inadequate sample", especially in post- menopausal women. ● It's a "blind" sample - can miss focal lesions. ● Not recommended by “NICE” Dilation & Curettage (D&C) “Old method”: Dilates cervix and uses a metal curette to scrape the endometrium. D&C is still used therapeutically (e.g., removing retained products of conception), but not for diagnosing AUB. Hysteroscopy + Targeted Biopsy (Gold Standard) Procedure: A thin, lighted camera (hysteroscope) is passed through the cervix into the uterine cavity. The cavity is distended (usually with saline). Allows direct visualization of the entire endometrial lining. �"See-and-Treat”. Advantages ▪ “Gold Standard”: Can identi

Drugs In Pregnancy Prepared by: Dalia Ghareb Maulud Liya saeed Ahmad Sara Hama Abdulla Chya Nasir qadir Pregnancy is a unique and complex period in a woman’s life. During this time, many physiological changes occur in the body that can significantly affect how drugs are absorbed, distributed, metabolized, and eliminated. Introduction • Gastric PH & Gastric Motility • Maternal Plasma Volume • Hormonal changes • Glomerular Filtration Rate Physiological Changes AÎecting Drug Pharmacology The placenta functions as an organ of exchange, allowing nutrients — and unfortunately, some medications — to pass from the mother to the fetus. Drugs Cross placenta : • low in molecular weight • poorly protein-bound • highly lipophilic • low in ionization The first trimester of pregnancy (0–13 weeks) is the most critical period for embryonic and fetal development, and the time when the risk of teratogenic effects is greatest. Placental Transfer and Fetal Exposure Medication Use and Compliance in Pregnancy • 40–90% of pregnant women are exposed to medications during pregnancy. • drugs should be avoided unless absolutely necessary ( except serious or life threatening conditions. • compliance among pregnant women tends to be suboptimal( 39% noncompliance) . Drugs can affect the fetus in three main ways: 1. Direct Effects — Causing injury, abnormal development (leading to birth defects), or even fetal death. 2. Placental Effects — Some drugs constrict placental blood vessels, reducing oxygen and nutrient delivery, which can lead to an underweight or underdeveloped baby. 3. Uterine Effects — Certain drugs can stimulate uterine contractions, reducing fetal blood supply or triggering preterm labor and delivery. How Drugs AÎect the Fetus Drug Safety During Pregnancy and Breastfeeding • After birth, medications can also pass into breast milk in small amounts. Most are safe, but some — such as cytotoxic agents, lithium, radiopharmaceuticals, and retinoids — must be strictly avoided while breastfeeding. • The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) , Pregnancy and Lactation Labeling Rule (PLLR) • benefit–risk assessment must be carefully evaluated to ensure maternal therapeutic benefit while minimizing fetal risk. Teratogenicity & Drug Safety ClassiÏcation • Teratogenicity means the ability of a drug to cause structural or functional abnormalities in the developing embryo or fetus. • Teratogenic effects can lead to miscarriage, stillbirth, growth restriction, or birth defects such as limb deformities or neural-tube defects. • Drug causing this effect called Teratogen , examples : ⚬Thalidomide → limb deformities ⚬Valproic acid → neural-tube defects ⚬ACE inhibitors → fetal renal damage thalidomide tragedy in the 1950s–60s, where a morning-sickness drug caused thousands of malformed births, completely changed drug-safety regulations. • The most critical period of fetal development is the first trimester during which major organs and the basic body structure are formed. • During this time, the developing fetus is most vulnerable to teratogens that can cause structural birth defects. Critical Periods of Fetal Development Stage Time(weeks) Effect if exposed Pre-implantation 0-2 weeks ““All or non “ efect (either death or survival) Organogenesis 3-8 weeks Major malformations (heart, brain,limbs) Fetal period 9 weeks - term Growth retardation , functional deficits Critical Periods of Fetal Development FDA Pregnancy Risk Categories (A–X) But this letter system was too simple, and in 2015 it was officially replaced. New System: PLLR (Pregnancy and Lactation Labeling Rule) • since June 2015, the FDA replaced the old category system with the Pregnancy and Lactation Labeling Rule (PLLR) because the letter categories were overly simple and often misleading. • Instead of a single letter, it provides clear, evidence-based details in three sections: New System: PLLR (Pregnancy and Lactation Labeling Rule) 1. Pregnancy: risk summary, clinical considerations, labor & deliver

ENDOMETRIOSIS & ADENOMYOSIS Presented by: Besar Khalid Hazhan Muhammadamin Lara Awat Vina Khdir Mabast Ibrahim • DEFINITIONS • EPIDEMIOLOGY • ETIOLOGY & PATHOGENESIS • CLINICAL FEATURES • DIAGNOSIS • MANAGEMENT • REFERENCES OVERVIEW WHAT IS ENDOMATRIOSIS? Endometriosis is a condition where endometrial-like tissue grows outside the uterus, most commonly on pelvic structures such as the peritoneum, pouch of Douglas, uterosacral ligaments, bladder, and ovaries. When it affects the ovary, it may form a “chocolate cyst” or endometrioma. These implants respond to hormonal cycles, causing repeated bleeding, inflammation, fibrosis, and adhesions. As a result, patients often experience pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility, and severe cases may lead to a “frozen pelvis.” EPIDEMIOLOGY Strongly oestrogen-dependent symptoms improve after menopause or with treatments inducing pseudomenopause. Affects: ~10% of women of reproductive age. Seen in ≥30% of women undergoing laparoscopy for pelvic pain or infertility. Prevalence: ~1,000 per 100,000 women Incidence: remains high worldwide ETIOLOGY & PATHOGENESIS The exact cause of endometriosis is still not known, but several theories help explain how it may develop. 1. Sampson’s Implantation Theory • During menstruation, some blood can flow backwards through the Fallopian tubes into the pelvis. • This blood may contain living endometrial cells that stick to the pelvic walls and start growing. • This idea is supported by finding menstrual blood in the pelvis during laparoscopy ETIOLOGY & PATHOGENESIS 2. Coelomic Metaplasia Theory • The peritoneal lining of the pelvis comes from the same original tissue as the endometrium. • Under certain conditions—like hormonal changes or inflammation—these cells can change (metaplasia) into endometrial-like cells. ETIOLOGY & PATHOGENESIS 3. Genetic & Immune Factors • Endometriosis can run in families, suggesting a genetic link. • Some populations have higher or lower rates, which also supports a genetic component. • Problems with the immune system may allow these abnormal cells to survive and grow outside the uterus. 4. Vascular & Lymphatic Spread • Endometrial cells may travel through blood or lymphatic vessels, which explains rare cases found outside the pelvis, such as in the lungs. PATHOLOGICAL APPEARANCE 1. Peritoneal lesions: Start as small red or petechial implants on the peritoneum; with time they become dark, pigmented “powder-burn” spots. PATHOLOGICAL APPEARANCE 2. Endometrioma: Ovarian cysts filled with old, thick blood, giving the typical “chocolate cyst” appearance. PATHOLOGICAL APPEARANCE 3. Deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE): Lesions extend deeply into tissues. Commonly involve the rectovaginal septum, uterosacral ligaments, and sometimes the colon. RISK FACTORS • Family history – first-degree relatives affected • Early menarche – starting periods at a young age • Short menstrual cycles – less than ~27 days • Low body mass / lean body size • Nulliparity / low parity – never or few pregnancies CLINICAL FEATURES Infertility • Approximately one-third of patients presenting with infertility are found to have endometriosis. • In some cases, infertility may be the only symptom of the disease. Pelvic Pain • Pelvic pain in endometriosis is often severe, dull, or stabbing and typically occurs around menstruation. • Some women, however, may experience chronic, aching discomfort throughout the month. • Importantly, the severity of the pain does not always reflect the extent of the disease. CLINICAL FEATURES Women with endometriosis may experience deep pain during intercourse and sharp or cramping pain on defecation. Sometimes, colicky pelvic pain can be mistaken for irritable bowel syndrome, because mimic the irritable bowel syndrome. In rare cases, endometriosis can affect distant sites, causing symptoms such as cyclical rectal bleeding when the bowel is involved or cyclical nosebleeds if the nasal passages are affected. DIAGNOSIS • Thickening or no

Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM) Prepared by: Gailan Othman Bilal sabah Sewa kamal Sozi Ismael Aims: • To understand gestational diabetes and its epidemiology. • To understand how it presents and how it’s diagnosed. • To understand how it’s managed and have an idea on how to prevent it. • To have an idea on how it’s surveilled and followed up for early detection in future pregnancies. 2 INTRODUCTION � Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM) is a type of glucose intolerance that’s first recognized during pregnancy. � It occurs due to hormonal changes in pregnancy, leading to insulin resistance. � GDM is one of the most common pregnancy complications, affecting both maternal and fetal health. � Early detection and management are essential to prevent short- and long-term complications. EPIDEMIOLOGY � It complicates 10-15% of pregnancies, according to the diagnostic criteria used. � It is rising in incidence parallel to the rise of obesity and sedentary lifestyles. � Higher prevalence in afro-caribbean, hispanic, and middle eastern populations. PHYSIOLOGICAL CHANGES IN PREGNANCY Physiological changes happening in pregnancy put the pregnant lady in a state of hyperglycaemia. This is to ensure adequate glucose supply to the fetus — but may work to the detriment of the mother, by inducing insulin resistance and glucose intolerance. 5 Pathophysiology 6 Happens because of the inability of the pancreatic beta cells to overcome the insulin resistance. This in turn, leads to hyperglycaemia. Insulin resistance: 1. Increased production of estriol, progestron, and cortisol. 2. Placental hormones (especially Human Placental Lactogen) increase as pregnancy progresses → insulin resistance peaks in the third trimester 3. Increased destruction of insulin by the kidneys and the placenta. Clinical Presentation Usually asymptomatic. May present with: Polyuria, polydipsia Excessive fetal growth (macrosomia) Recurrent urinary tract infections Glycosuria on routine testing 7 Risk Factors 8 Maternal age >25 years BMI >30 kg/m² Family history of diabetes Previous GDM or large baby (>4.5 kg) Ethnicity: South Asian, African-Caribbean, Hispanic Polycystic ovarian syndrome Unexplained stillbirth or congenital anomaly in past pregnancy SCREENING GUIDELINES � Universal vs. selective screening (depends on local policy). � Timing: 24–28 weeks gestation. � Early screening for high-risk women. � Screening tests vary: OGTT, FBS, or HbA1c (less common in pregnancy). 9 Maternal Complications 10 Pre-eclampsia Polyhydramnios Increased risk of caesarean delivery Birth trauma Long-term risk of type 2 diabetes and metabolic syndrome Fetal & Neonatal Complications 11 Macrosomia Shoulder dystocia and birth injury Neonatal hypoglycaemia Respiratory distress syndrome Hyperbilirubinaemia Investigations OGTT: gold standard HbA1c: for baseline or postpartum follow-up Urine dipstick for glycosuria Ultrasound: fetal growth, amniotic fluid volume Fasting blood sugar 12 Diagnostic Criteria (OGTT) WHO 2013 Criteria, depending on OGTT : Fasting: ≥92 mg/dl (≥5.1 mmol/l) 1-hour: ≥180 mg/dl (≥10.0 mmol/l) 2-hour: ≥153 mg/dl (≥8.5 mmol/l.) Glycaemic Targets (per NICE) • Fasting glucose: <95 mg/dl • 1-hour postprandial: <140 mg/dl • 2-hour postprandial: <115 mg/dl 14 Management Overview Management generally aims to: 1. Maintain euglycaemia. 2. Prevent fetal and maternal complications. 3. Encourage healthy pregnancy outcomes. 15 Lifestyle Modifications Dietary changes: balanced meals, low glycaemic index carbs. Exercise: moderate physical activity (e.g. brisk walking 30 min/day). Weight control: avoid excessive gestational weight gain. Regular glucose self-monitoring. Pharmacological Management If glycaemic targets not met after 1–2 weeks of lifestyle modification: Insulin is first-line (safe in pregnancy). Metformin or glyburide may be used if insulin not feasible Antenatal Care Multidisciplinary approach: obstetrician, endocrinologist, dietitian, diabetic nurse. Frequent antenatal visits.

GESTATIONAL TROPHOBLASTIC TUMOR 16-Nov-2025 Prepared by : Rezhwan Abubakr Sahar Muhamad Raz Kamaran Outlines: 1. Introduction 2. Epidemiology 3. Physiology 4. Pathophysiology 5. Risk Factors 6. Classification 2 types of it 7. Clinical Features 8. Diagnostic Evaluation 9.Treatment 10. Prognosis 11. Complications 12. Prevention and Counseling 13. Conclusion 14. References Introduction : Gestational trophoblastic tumor primarily encompasses pathologic features that are characterized by aggressive invasion of the endometrium and myometrium by trophoblast cells. Also it is called persistent gestational trophoblastic disease which refers to a spectrum of interrelated but histologically distinct tumors originating from the placenta , GTT refers to the subset of GTD that devdops malignant sequelae . 1.Invasive mole. 2.Gestationalchoriocarcinoma. -Rare forms ; 1.placental site trophoblastic tumor. 2. Epithelioid trophoblastic tumor. Continue : Histologic categories include : -Common forms; Epidemiology : • Occur in about 1per 1,000 pregnancies worldwide. • Higher incidence in SoutheastAsia, the Middle East, and Africa compared with Western countries , Improved socioeconomic conditions and dietary changes may be partly responsible . • In developed regions, incidence is about 1 in 1,500–2,000 pregnancies. Regions with high molar pregnancy rates are often associated with diets low in carotene and animal fats. Trophoblasic physiology : After fertilization,the trophoblast forms the outer layer of the blastocyst and later develops into: Cytotrophoblast – the inner, germinative cells. Syncytiotrophoblast – the outer multinucleated layer that invades the endometrium. Implantation: Syncytiotrophoblast invades maternal decidua . Placental development: Trophoblasts remodel maternal spiral arteries → creating low-resistance, high-flow channels to support fetal growth. Hormone production: Especially β-hCG, progesterone, estrogen — essential for pregnancy maintenance. Immunological Role : The trophoblast expresses non-classical MHC molecules (HLA-G) that prevent maternal immune rejection. Vascular and Nutritional Exchange : Through the chorionic villi, the trophoblast facilitates exchange of: • Oxygen and carbon dioxide • Nutrients and waste • Antibodies (IgG) – for passive immunity Continue : Their functions include: Pathophysiology : Gestational trophoblastic neoplasia typically devdops with or follows some form of pregnancy. Most cases follow a hydatidiform mole, rarely, GTN devdops after a live birth, miscarriage, or termination. Occasionally, the antecedent gestation cannot be confirmed with certainty. Continue : GTN occurs when trophoblastic cells undergo abnormal, uncontrolled proliferation instead of following normal pregnancy pathways. Key Mechanisms : Genetic abnormalities in conception: - Complete mole: Usually androgenetic, 46XX from paternal origin → no fetal tissue → marked trophoblastic hyperplasia. - Partial mole: Triploid (69XXX/XXY) → some fetal tissue → focal trophoblastic proliferation. These abnormal conceptions predispose to malignant transformation. Continue : Invasion and metastasis Loss of normal control of trophoblastic growth : Trophoblasts become excessively invasive. They destroy maternal tissues instead of regulated implantation. High production of β-hCG, often far above normal pregnancy levels. Angiogenesis and hemorrhage : Leads to uterine bleeding and facilitating rapid hematogenous spread. Risk Factors : Maternal age<20or>40 years Previous molar pregnancy Complete mole (higher risk than partial) Very high initial β-hCG levels Uterus larger than expected for gestational age Large ovarian cysts Persistent β-hCG after evacuation History of previous GTN Possible nutritional deficiency (carotene/folate), genetic predisposition Choriocarcinoma : ~A highly malignant GTN characterized by invasive, highly vascular, and anaplastic trophoblastic tissue without villi. Has the tendency to metastasize to the lungs,vagina,CNS,live

Obstetrical tear Prepared by : Khawen Bakhtyar Mhamad Rafiq Karo Ali Ahmad Rebwar 1.Introduction. 2.Anatomy. 3.Definition. 4.Epidemiology. 5.Classifications. 6.Episiotomy. 7.Risk factors. 8.diagnosis 9.General principles of Repair. 10.Postpartum care & Follow up. 11.counseling for future pregnancy. Objectives Introduction Obstetrical (perineal) tears are soft-tissue injuries occurring during vaginal delivery. Range from minor skin tears to complete rectal mucosa involvement. Important cause of postpartum pain, incontinence, and morbidity. Proper classification and repair reduce long-term complications. Anatomy of the Perineum Perineal skin and subcutaneous tissue Perineal body Bulbospongiosus muscle Transverse perineal muscles External anal sphincter (EAS) Internal anal sphincter (IAS) Rectal mucosa Understanding anatomy is essential for accurate diagnosis and repair. Definition Perineal tears are spontaneous lacerations of tissues during childbirth. Result from overstretching by fetal head or instrumental delivery. Classified based on depth and structures involved. This can be prevented by : Perineal support at crowning Warm compresses in second stage Controlled delivery of the fetal head Antenatal perineal massage (may help in primigravida) Epidemiology 85% per cent of women who have a vaginal birth will have some degree of perineal trauma and 60–70% will require suturing. More common in primigravid women. Higher incidence with operative deliveries & The most common form of operative intervention is suturing of a perineal tear . . Classification Overview (RCOG/ACOG) 1.1st degree – perineal skin only 2.2nd degree – perineal muscles 3.3rd degree – EAS involvement 3a: <50% EAS torn 3b: >50% EAS torn 3c: EAS + IAS 4.4th degree – injury extends into rectal mucosa First-Degree Tear Involves: perineal skin only Minimal bleeding Often requires no sutures Heals quickly and rarely associated with morbidity Mucosa Anus Second-Degree Tear Involves: Perineal skin Perineal muscles Does NOT involve anal sphincter Requires suturing with absorbable material If untreated → risk of pelvic floor weakness Muscle Third-Degree Tears ( 3a, 3b, 3c) 3a: <50% of EAS torn 3b: >50% of EAS torn 3c: EAS + IAS torn Must be repaired by trained obstetrician or colorectal specialist High risk of anal incontinence if poorly repaired Anal sphincter Fourth-Degree Tear Most severe tear Involves:(Perineal skin,Perineal muscles,EAS,IAS,Rectal mucosa) Needs repair in theatre Risk of fistula formation and long-term incontinence Rectal torn OASI’s Third- and fourth-degree tears are grouped together and termed obstetric anal sphincterinjuries (OASIs). OASIs are reported in approximately 3% of nulliparous and 0.5% of multiparous individuals. In general terms, external anal sphincter incompetence causes faecal urgency, whereasinternal anal sphinc- ter incompetence causes faecal incontinence. 4th-degree 3rd-degree Episiotomy Surgical incision of perineum to aid delivery and it classified as 2 degree tear. nd Types: Mediolateral (preferred) Midline (higher risk of 3rd/4th degree tears) Should be selective, not routine Helps in instrumental deliveries and shoulder dystocia Risk Factors Primigravida Short perineal body Previous OASIS Advanced maternal age Macrosomia Occiput posterior position Prolonged 2nd stage Instrumental delivery (forceps > vacuum) Shoulder dystocia Rapid expulsion of head Maternal Fetal Labor-related Inspection under good lighting post-delivery Digital rectal examination mandatory in Suspected 3rd/4th degree tear Identify all involved structures Accurate classification should be documented Diagnosis Usually done under local anesthesia Skin closure: continuous or interrupted sutures Muscle closure: interrupted sutures Use absorbable sutures (e.g., Vicryl Rapide) Avoid excessive tension to reduce pain General Principles of Repair Done in operating theatre EAS repair techniques: End-to-end Overlap method IAS repaired separately if visible Antibiotics recom

Obstetrics & Gynecolo.text: OVARIAN CYST IN ADOLESCENCE AND ITS COMPLICATIONS Presented by Esra yadgar, Hadiya Hamasharif,Shanyar Nawzad • Introduction • Pathophysiology • Risk factor • Clinical features • Investigation • Management • complication • Follow up • conclusion • references project overview Introduction • Fluid-filled sacs that develop within or on the surface of the ovary • May be functional (physiological) or pathological • Very common in early reproductive years • Most are benign and linked to normal menstrual physiology • Adolescents have immature HPO axis - irregular ovulation . • High chance of functional cyst formation • Common soon after menarche • Pathological cysts less common but clinically significant Epidemiology of Ovarian Cysts in Adolescents • Incidence of 1–2%. • Peak prevalence at age 15: 31.3% • Children 5-9 years: 1.8% • Adolescents 10-18 years: 18.8% Types of Ovarian Cysts in Adolescents A. Functional Cysts (Most common) 1. Follicular cyst • Failure of a dominant follicle to rupture. • Usually <6 cm, asymptomatic. 2. Corpus luteum cyst • After ovulation, corpus luteum seals and fills with fluid/blood. • May cause delayed menses and unilateral pain. 3. Theca lutein cysts • Large, bilateral; associated with (pregnancy, molar pregnancy). Cont. B. Pathological Cysts 1. Benign germ cell tumors • Dermoid cyst (Mature cystic teratoma) - most common pathological cyst in adolescents. 2. Benign epithelial tumors • Serous cystadenoma • Mucinous cystadenoma 3. Endometrioma ("Chocolate cyst") • Due to endometriosis; pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea. 4. Paraovarian cysts • Not from the ovary itself; arise from remnants of Wolﬃan ducts. • Ovulatory cycle disruption prevents normal follicle maturation or regression. • Immature/imbalanced HPO axis → follicles fail to mature, rupture, or involute. • Fluid or blood accumulates inside ovarian structures → cyst formation. • Excess FSH, LH, or hCG overstimulates ovarian cells → enlarged cystic structures. • Ovaries are highly vascular, so bleeding can also create cystic spaces. • Non-hormonal growths (epithelial, germ cell, or ectopic endometrial tissue) can form persistent cysts. • Final result: a fluid-filled sac forms from abnormal growth, failed ovulation, or failed regression. General Pathophysiology of Ovarian Cysts Classification of ovarian cysts Risk Factors for Ovarian Cysts in Adolescence Clinical Features, Signs, and Symptoms • Many ovarian cysts in adolescents are asymptomatic, particularly when they are small, functional, and short-lived. • Because they do not compress surrounding structures or impair ovarian function, they commonly go unnoticed. • Most asymptomatic cysts are discovered incidentally during imaging performed for unrelated medical evaluations. Cont. When symptoms occur, their nature depends on the cyst’s size, type, and complications. 1 - Pelvic or Lower Abdominal Pain • The most common presenting symptom. • Reported in ≈60% of symptomatic adolescents. • Character of pain: ( Dull or sharp , Intermittent or constant , Unilateral or bilateral 2 - Sudden, sharp mid-cycle pain felt in the lower abdomen due to follicular rupture during ovulation 3 - Menstrual Irregularities • Irregular menstruation • Oligomenorrhea, menorrhagia • Variations in cycle length → Often related to hormonal fluctuations associated with functional cysts. 4 - As cysts enlarge, they may exert pressure on adjacent organs, resulting in: • Bloating or abdominal fullness • Pelvic pressure sensation • Increased urinary frequency due to bladder compression • Occasional nausea or vomiting • Chronic pelvic discomfort due to mass effect Cont. Investigation of Ovarian Cysts in Adolescents • Pelvic Ultrasonography — First-Line Test Ultrasound is the primary investigation because it is non-invasive, radiation-free, and evaluates the cyst's size, location, morphology, and internal contents. A transabdominal Ultrasound (with a full bladder) is preferred for non-sexually active adolescents. Transvaginal Ultrasound may b

PUERPERIUM & PEURPERAL SEPSIS Preparad By : Rosie Omar Shnyar Luqman Rozhyar Yousuf Barin Mariwan Understand the physiological changes that occur in the normal puerperium. Understand recovery after normal birth and caesarean section. Understand the physical effects of childbirth on the perineum, bladder and bowel. Be able to recognize and manage peurperal sepsis. Learning Objectives The puerperium refers to the 6-week period following completion of the third stage of labour, when considerable adjustments occur before return to the pre-pregnant state. For those with complex medical problems, the early puerperium is especially dangerous, and most maternal deaths occur during this time. During this period of physiological change, the mother is also vulnerable to psychological disturbances. INTRODUCTION PHYSIOLOGICAL CHANGES UTERINE INVOLUTION Involution is the process by which the postpartumuterus, weighing about 1 kg, returns to its prepregnancystate of less than 100 g. Immediately after delivery, the uterine fundus lies about 4 cm below the umbilicus or, more accurately, 12 cm above the symphysis pubis. By 2 weeks, the uterus can no longer bepalpable above the symphysis. Involution occurs by a process of autolysis, whereby muscle cells diminish in size as a result of enzymatic digestion of cytoplasm. Involution appears to be accelerated by the release of oxytocin in women who are breastfeeding. A delay in involution in the absence of any other signs or symptoms, e.g. bleeding, is of no clinical significance. GENITAL TRACT CHANGES Following delivery of the placenta, the lower segment of the uterus and the cervix appear flabby and there may be small cervical lacerations. In the first few days, the cervix can readily admit two fingers, but by the end of the first week it should become increasingly difficult to pass more than one finger. By the end of the second week the internal os should be closed. However, the external os can remain open permanently,giving a characteristic appearance to the parous cervix. LOCHIA Lochia is the blood-stained uterine discharge that is comprised of blood and necrotic decidua. Only the superficial layer of decidua becomes necrotic and is sloughed off. The basal layer adjacent to the myometrium is involved in the regeneration of new endometrium and this regeneration is complete by the third week. Persistent red lochia suggests delayed involution that is usually associated with infection or a retained piece of placental tissue. RECOVERY AFTER CHILDBIRTH There are very few mothers who are not physically and emotionally drained in the first few weeks after childbirth. Alongside the psychological changes ,the main physical effects are on the perineum, bladder and bowel. PERINEAL PAIN About 80% of mothers complain of pain in the first 3 days after delivery, with a quarter continuing to suffer discomfort at day 10. Discomfort is greatest in women who sustain spontaneous tears or have an episiotomy. Local cooling and topical anaesthetics, such as 5% lignocaine gel, provide short-term relief. The perineum should be kept clean with daily cleaning or showering using tap water only. BLADDER FUNCTION Voiding difficulties and overdistension of the bladder are not uncommon after childbirth, especially if regional anaesthesia (epidural/spinal) has been used. Women who have undergone a traumatic delivery, such as a difficult instrumental delivery, or who have suffered multiple/extended lacerations may find it difficult to void because of pain or periurethral oedema. To minimize the risk of overdistension of the bladder in women who have had a spinal anaesthetic a urinary catheter is left in the bladder for at least 12 hours until the woman is mobile. Any incontinence should be investigated to exclude a vesicovaginal, urethrovaginal or reterovaginal fistula. BOWEL FUNCTION Constipation is a common problem in the early puerperium as a result of an interruption in the normal diet, intra-partum dehydration and opiate use

Part 1: Foundations & The Immediate Postoperative Phase Post Operative Care Deﬁning the Postoperative care Postoperative care is the crucial period of medical and nursing management. It's a journey focused on enabling a safe, rapid, and comfortable recovery while actively preventing and managing potential complications. Immediate (PACU) The critical ﬁrst few hours post- surgery. Intermediate (Ward) Encompasses the ﬁrst 24-72 hours. Convalescent (Home) Extends for weeks following hospital discharge. Key Point: Care is meticulously tailored to each patient's unique proﬁle (age, comorbidities) and the speciﬁc surgical procedure performed (e.g., minimally invasive vs. open surgery). In another words post operative care depends on the type of surgery performed The First Critical Hour: The PACU The Post-Anesthesia Care Unit (PACU) is where immediate postoperative vigilance begins. A seamless transition of care from the operating room is paramount. Seamless Handover Detailed report from the anesthesia team covering "ABC" status, surgical speciﬁcs, ﬂuid balance, estimated blood loss, and any intraoperative issues. Continuous Monitoring Uninterrupted assessment of vital signs (blood pressure, heart rate, oxygen saturation), pain levels, and level of consciousness. Airway & Breathing Ensuring patent airway, adequate ventilation, supplemental oxygenation, and auscultation of breath sounds. Circulatory Assessment Vigilant assessment for signs of bleeding (checking dressings, internal bleeding), maintaining IV access, and active blood pressure management. Ensuring Patient Comfort: Pain and PONV Pain Manage ment - Adequate pain control improves patient mobility, reduces psychological stress. . - Patient-controlled analgesia (PCA) may be utilized for individualized pain relief. Postoperative Nausea & Vomiting - A common and distressing complication, particularly after gynecological surgeries like laparoscopy or uterine manipulation. Eﬀective management of pain and postoperative nausea and vomiting (PONV) is fundamental to patient comfort and successful recovery. Transitioning to the Next Phase Discharge from the PACU signiﬁes a patient's stabilization and readiness for transfer to a less intensive care setting. Stable Vital Signs Blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate, and oxygen saturation within acceptable limits for the patient. Awake and Oriented Patient able to follow commands and demonstrates cognitive recovery from anesthesia. Pain Control Pain managed to a tolerable level, often with oral analgesics or adjusted IV medication. Minimal Nausea/Vomiting Absence of or controlled PONV, allowing for oral intake if appropriate. No Active Bleeding Surgical sites are stable with no signs of active hemorrhage. The primary goal of the immediate postoperative phase is patient stabilization. Once these criteria are met, the patient is safely transferred to the general surgical ward. Recognizing Potential Complications in PACU While the PACU focuses on stabilization, vigilant monitoring is essential to promptly identify and manage early postoperative complications. Hemorrhage Sudden drop in blood pressure, increased heart rate, cool extremities, or fresh bleeding from surgical sites. Requires immediate intervention. Respiratory Depression Shallow breathing, low oxygen saturation, or absent breath sounds, often due to residual anesthetic eﬀects or opioid overdose. Cardiac Arrhythmias Irregular heart rhythms, palpitations, or changes on the ECG, which can be exacerbated by pain, hypoxemia, or electrolyte imbalances. Hypothermia Core body temperature below 36°C, common after prolonged surgery. Can impair coagulation and increase risk of infection. Patient-Centered Care: Individualizing Recovery Postoperative care is not a one-size-ﬁts-all approach. Individual patient factors signiﬁcantly inﬂuence the recovery trajectory and necessitate a personalized plan. Age Considerations Elderly patients may have slower recovery, increased risk of delirium,

Pre-eclampsia and eclampsia Presented by: Chapk Othman, Lava Taha, Sima Sherko, Zanyar Ali Outline Epidemiology Classification Pathophysiology Risk Factors 01 02 03 04 Outline Complications Clinical Presentation Investigations Management 05 06 07 08 Outline Eclampsia Prevention and screening Reference 09 10 11 Epidemiology About 10% of pregnancies involve hypertension, most being mild gestational hypertension. Pre-eclampsia affects ~3% of pregnancies and is a leading cause of maternal death [50,000–75,000 annually worldwide], and is often linked to fetal growth restriction and high perinatal complications. Classification Hypertensive disorders in pregnancy (HDP) < 20 weeks of gestation ≥20 weeks of gestation Chronic hypertension Preeclampsia Gestational hypertension Chronic hypertension with superimposed preeclampsia Pre-eclampsia is defined as new-onset gestational hypertension (systolic blood pressure ≥140 mmHg and/or diastolic blood pressure ≥90 mmHg) associated with new onset of at least one of the following: • Proteinuria > 300 mg/24 hours • Maternal organ dysfunction (liver, neurological, haematological or renal involvement) • Uteroplacental dysfunction at or after 20 weeks’ gestation Pre-eclampsia Pathophysiology STAGE ONE Abnormal immune response and deficient placentation STAGE TWO Maternal endothelial cell dysfunction leading to maternal syndrome Moderate risk factors ✓First pregnancy ✓Aged 40 years or older ✓Pregnancy interval more than 10 years ✓BMI of 35 or more at first visit ✓Family hx of pre eclampsia ✓Multi fetal pregnancy Major risk factors ✓Pre eclampsia in any previous pregnancy ✓chronic kidney disease ✓Autoimmune disease such as SLE or APS ✓Chronic hypertension ✓Type 1 or 2 Diabetes Risk factors for preeclampsia Clinical Presentations Visual disturbance Epigastric pain Frontal Headache Clinical Presentations Continued • The majority of pregnant women with pre-eclampsia are Asymptomatic or merely complain of general vague ‘flu –like’ symptoms. In the clinical examination : • Hypertension is usually the first sign, • Edema of the face and hands may suggest pre-eclampsia • Tenderness in epigastric region is a Warning sign • Neurological examination may reveal hyperreflexia and clonus in severe cases. • Urine testing for protein should be considered part of the clinical examination Investigations Maternal monitoring: •CBC: watch for falling plt and rising Hematocrit •Renal profile: renal function and uric acid •Liver profile •Repetitive Testing for proteinuria Fetal monitoring: • Ultrasound: Asess fetal growth and amniotic fluid volume •Doppler : evaluate uteroplacental and fetal blood flow •CTG : Asess fetal well being ; loss of variability or deceleration > hypoxia Maternal Complications Fetal Complications CNS involvement (eg. Seizure , stroke) Intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR) Disseminated intravascular coagulopathy (DIC) Placental infarct and intrauterine fetal death (IUD) Liver failure Placental abruption Renal failure Prematurity HELLP syndrome Perinatal death Antepartum and postpartum hemorrhage Future cardiovascular diseases High rate of C/S Future risk of chronic hypertension Death Complications Managements Management There is no cure for pre-eclampsia other than to end the pregnancy by delivering the baby and placenta. The principles of Mx of preeclampsia are; • Early recognition of symotomless syndrome • Awareness of the seriousness nature of the condition in its severest form • Adherence to the guideline (admission, investigation, use of antihypertensive and anticonvulsant therapy • Well timed delivery to prevent complications • Postnatal follow up and counselling Management depending on the degree of pre eclampsia • Appropriate if:BP <160 systolic and <110 diastolic and can be controlled • no or low ( ≤1+/<300mg/24h) proteinurea • asymptomatic. • Difficult to distinguish from gestational hypertension. Outpatient Management of pre eclampsia •Warn about development of symptoms •BP and urine ch

Premenstrual tension disorder Their well being depends on our understanding Presented by: Ranjdar Sardar Rawa Ahmad Soran Salh Objectives Understand PMS: Define PMS, its significance, and clinical features. Explain Pathophysiology and Diagnosis: Describe underlying mechanisms and diagnostic criteria. Approach and Management: Outline clinical evaluation and evidence- based management strategies. Premenstrual syndrome (PMS) is a common disorder in women of reproductive age. It is characterized by at least one physical, emotional, or behavioral symptom, which appears in the luteal phase of the menstrual cycle and resolves shortly after the onset of menses. Epidemiology Epidemiological studies have revealed that about 80% to 90% of women manifest at least one of the PMS signs; however, in about 2.5% to 3% of women, the syndrome is severe enough to affect their activities and social communications. • Most common in women aged 25–40. More frequent in women with existing mood disorders. • Shows a familial/genetic tendency. One of the proposed etiologies of PMS involves the cyclic hormonal fluctuations of estrogen and progesterone during the menstrual cycle, particularly during the late luteal phase as the body prepares for menstruation. Women with and without PMS have similar cyclic changes in ovarian steroid concentrations. The distinction between having or not having PMS could stem from how the body responds to the cyclic changes in ovarian hormones across the menstrual cycle. Pathophysiology Pathophysiology Role of neurotransmitters: Serotonin: During the luteal phase, women with PMS have more serotonin abnormalities than women without PMS symptoms. Serotonin is responsible for many psychological, physiologic, emotional, and behavioral responses and women with PMD express similar symptoms to individuals who have a reduced level of serotonin, posing the thought that the two could be related. gamma aminobutyric acid (GABA): present work suggests that progesterone may play a modulatory role in GABA’s inhibitory system. This hypothesis is supported by observations of improvement in PMS symptoms using agents such as benzodiazepine and alprazolam that work to increase GABA activity. In addition, metabolites of progesterone have been shown to function as positive allosteric modulators of GABA in the brain. Endorphins: evidence that shows the release of endorphins is coupled with the release of estrogen and could theoretically decline with ovulation and remain at low levels during the late luteal phase when estrogen levels decline again prior to menstruation. physical symptoms : Abdominal cramps, abdominal bloating, tenderness and pain in the breasts, pain (back, head, muscle, joints), swelling of hands and feet, weight fluctuations, constipation, and diarrhea. Psycho-emotional symptoms: mood swings, Depression, anxiety, tendency to cry, irritability, emotional lability, difficulty, concentrating, and anger. Behavioral symptoms: changes in libido and sexual desire, social withdrawal from partner, family and friends, sensitivity, increased appetite, decreased interest in activities, and restlessness. symptoms Risk Factors for Premenstrual Syndrome (PMS) Family history of PMS or mood disorders, suggesting a genetic or hereditary component. Psychiatric comorbidities, including a history of depression or anxiety, increase susceptibility to premenstrual symptom exacerbation. Stress and psychosocial factors, such as high perceived stress or early childhood adversities. Lifestyle factors, including poor sleep, inadequate physical activity, and high caffeine intake. Younger reproductive age, with symptoms often more prominent in women in their late teens to early 30s. Differentiation from PMDD Premenstrual Syndrome (PMS) and Premenstrual Dysphoric Disorder (PMDD) represent distinct points on the spectrum of premenstrual symptomatology but differ markedly in severity, diagnostic requirements, and associated functional impairment. PMDD is a formall

Recurrent Pregnancy loss Name: Payam Sabah Shano Karim Tara Nawaf OUTLINE �What is miscarriage & how it is diâerent from recurrent miscarriage �Categories of recurrent miscarriage �Etiology of recurrent miscarriage �How can evaluate and manage each etiology Deﬁnition of Miscarriage & RPL Miscarriage: spontaneous loss of pregnancy before fetal viability (≤24 weeks) Recurrent Pregnancy Loss (RPL) / Habitual abortion: Defined as three or more consecutive miscarriages before 24 weeks. Epidemiology Incidence • Recurrent miscarriage affects ~1% of women. • If RM is defined as two losses, incidence doubles (~1.9%). • up to 50% remain unexplained o emotionally challenging for patients, families, and physicians Key Determinants ✔ Maternal age is the strongest factor — incidence rises with age. ✔ Number of prior miscarriages affects future live birth chance. New Graded Care Approach: ❑ After one miscarriage: give information & support. ❑ After two: offer initial investigations & reassurance scans. ❑ After three: full evidence-based evaluation per guideline. Secondary RPL Categories of RPL pregnancy loss in women who have never had a live birth pregnancy loss in women with at least one previous live birth Primary RPL Etiology 01 04 02 05 03 06 Genetic Factors Thrombophilia Anatomical factors Endocrine factor Immune Factors Idiopathic Factors Clinical Evaluation General Rule History Although each cause has its own tests and management, a thorough history and physical examination are the foundation of evaluation. Number & timing of miscarriages Previous pregnancies, complications Family history, medical conditions, lifestyle General exam - BMI, thyroid, signs of systemic disease Pelvic exam - uterine size, tenderness, masses Speculum - cervix, infection, discharge Physical Examination Ask about Fetal Chromosomal Anomalies Genetic factor • Most common cause of recurrent miscarriage. • Types (in descending frequency) o Trisomy o Polyploidy o Monosomy o Others • Incidence of parental chromosomal rearrangements is significantly associated with recurrent miscarriage. • Risk of miscarriage depends on the type of rearrangement: o Reciprocal translocations o Robertsonian translocations o Inversions o Other chromosomal anomalies Parental Chromosomal Rearrangements Investigation • Alongside routine investigations, cytogenetic analysis of pregnancy tissue provides a diagnosis in >90% of couples. • Parental peripheral blood karyotyping is recommended: o When pregnancy tissue testing is unsuccessful. o When no tissue is available for testing. Management of Genetic Factors • VF/ICSI + PGT-SR (Preimplantation Genetic Testing for Structural Rearrangements) o Screens embryos for chromosomal abnormalities o Transfers only healthy embryos → ↓ miscarriage risk • Gamete Donation o Option if severe parental chromosomal abnormality Thrombophilia Laboratory criteria: • •Lupus anticoagulant positivity • •Anticardiolipin antibody(aCL) of IgG or IgM isotype at moderate/high titer • •Anti-B2 glycoprotein I antibody of IgG or IgM isotype. • Diagnosis Requires at least one clinical criterion and one laboratory criterion, confirmed on two occasions at least 12 weeks apart. Clinical criteria: 1. Vascular thrombosis 2. Pregnancy morbidity includes: • ≥3 miscarriages < 10 weeks •≥1 unexplained normal fetal loss >10 weeks • ≥1 preterm birth <34 weeks due to eclampsia, severe pre- eclampsia or placental insufficiency, or Acquired — APS Management • APS: Aspirin 75 mg + LMWH/UFH from positive test → 34 week • Inherited thrombophilia: Routine anticoagulation not recommended Inherited Thrombophilia Factor V Leiden, Protein C/S deficiency, Antithrombin deficiency, Prothrombin mutation Cause systemic thrombosis Acquired Uterine Factors Anatomical Factors • Myomas: Submucosal, Intramural/Subserosal • Endometrial polyps • Intrauterine adhesions (Asherman’s syndrome) • Adenomyosis • Septate uterus is the most common cause related to Recurrent miscarriage Congenital Uterine Anomalies
