Approach to Movement Disorders Dr. Sarwer Jamal Al-Bajalan FRCP, FAAN, E.B.N, F.I.B.M.S (Neurology) Consultant Neurologist & Head of MS Clinic – Shar Teaching Hospital Ass. Prof. of Neurology - College of Medicine - Sulaimani University 29th Oct. 2024 10/29/2024 Ass. Prof. Sarwer Al-Bajalan 1 Outlines ◼Hyperkinetic vs Hypokinetic movement disorders. ◼Rhythmic vs non-rhythmic movement disorder. ◼Tremor ◼Chorea ◼Athetosis ◼Ballismus ◼Dystonia ◼Myoclonus ◼Tics ◼Video presentations of clinical patterns. 10/29/2024 Ass. Prof. Sarwer Al-Bajalan 2 Objectives The students must be able to: ◼Recognize and diagnose various patents of presentation of movement disorders. ◼Learn the academic definition, the causes and how to investigate for various patents of presentation of movement disorders. 10/29/2024 Ass. Prof. Sarwer Al-Bajalan 3 Movement disorders ◼Disorders of movement lead to either: • Extra, unwanted movement (hyperkinetic disorders: e.g: Hantington’s Chorea) or • Too little movement (hypokinetic disorders: e.g: Parkinson’s D) ◼Much of the skill in diagnosing movement disorders lies in pattern recognition. ◼Abnormal movements usually imply a disorder in the basal ganglia, in which there is disinhibition of intrinsic rhythm generators or a disorder of postural control. 10/29/2024 Ass. Prof. Sarwer Al-Bajalan 4 Movement disorders ◼Movement disorders present with a wide range of symptoms. ◼They may be genetic or acquired, and the most important is Parkinson’s disease. ◼Most movement disorders are categorised clinically, with few confirmatory investigations available other than for those with a known gene abnormality. 10/29/2024 Ass. Prof. Sarwer Al-Bajalan 5 Anatomy of the Basal Ganglia The main components are: ◼ Caudate nucleus ◼ Putamen ◼ Olfactory tubercle ◼ Globus pallidus ◼ Subthalamic nucleus ◼ Substantia nigra 10/29/2024 Ass. Prof. Sarwer Al-Bajalan 6 10/29/2024 Ass. Prof. Sarwer Al-Bajalan 7 Tremor rhythmic movement disorder Tremor is caused by alternating agonist/antagonist muscle contractions and produces a rhythmical oscillation of the body part affected. The many causes of tremor have different characteristics: ◼Parkinsonian: ‘Pill-rolling’, asymmetrical and present at rest. ◼Physiological: Exaggerated by anxiety, emotion, drugs , & thyrotoxicosis. ◼Essential: Slower than physiological tremor; often familial and responsive to propranolol. ◼Dystonic: Affects head, arms and legs; jerky, associated with dystonias. ◼Functional: Variable pattern; disappears on distraction. 10/29/2024 Ass. Prof. Sarwer Al-Bajalan 8 10/29/2024 Ass. Prof. Sarwer Al-Bajalan 9 Other hyperkinetic syndromes non-rhythmic movement disorder Chorea: o Jerky, brief, purposeless involuntary movements, appearing as fidgety; they suggest disease in the caudate nucleus. Causes of Chorea include: ◼ Hereditary: (Huntington’s disease, Wilson’s disease). ◼ Drugs: (levodopa, antipsychotics, anticonvulsants*, oral contraceptives). ◼ Autoimmune: Sydenham’s chorea, antiphospholipid syndrome, SLE. ◼ Endocrine: pregnancy, thyrotoxicosis, hypoglycemia & hypoparathyroidism. ◼ Structural lesions: vascular, demyelination, brain tumors. 10/29/2024 Ass. Prof. Sarwer Al-Bajalan 10 Huntington’s Chorea 10/29/2024 Ass. Prof. Sarwer Al-Bajalan 11 Athetosis: Slower writhing movements of the limbs. These are often combined with chorea and have similar causes. Ballism: ◼ Flinging movements of the limbs more dramatic than chorea. ◼ Usually occur unilaterally ( hemiballismus ) in vascular lesions of the subthalamic nucleus. 10/29/2024 Ass. Prof. Sarwer Al-Bajalan 12 Dystonia: ◼ A movement disorder in which a limb (or the head) involuntarily takes up an abnormal posture. ◼ This may be generalized in basal ganglia disease, or may be focal or segmental, as in spasmodic torticollis when the head involuntarily turns to one side. ◼ Other segmental dystonias may cause abnormal disabling postures during specific actions (e.g. writer’s cramp) and can be treated with botulinum toxin to the resp

Parkinson’s Disease & Other Movement Disorders Dr. Sarwer Jamal Al-Bajalan FRCP, FAAN, E.B.N, F.I.B.M.S (Neurology) Consultant Neurologist & Head of MS Clinic – Shar Teaching Hospital Ass. Prof. of Neurology - College of Medicine - Sulaimani University 29th Oct. 2024 10/29/2024 Ass. Prof. Sarwer Al-Bajalan 1 Outlines • Parkinsonism • Idiopathic Parkinson’s disease. • Parkinson Plus syndromes. • MSA • PSP • CBD • LBD • HD • Wilson's disease • Essential Tremor • Hemifacial spasm • Video presentation of clinical patterns. 10/29/2024 Ass. Prof. Sarwer Al-Bajalan 2 Objectives The student must be able to: • Diagnose Idiopathic Parkinson’s disease & differentiate it from other Parkinson plus syndromes. • Diagnose the main movement disorder patterns through video teaching. • Treat idiopathic Parkinson’s disease & some of the other common movement disorder like essential tremor. • Understand the mechanisms of action of anti-Parkinsonian medications. • Recognize the main side effects of anti-Parkinsonian medications. 10/29/2024 Ass. Prof. Sarwer Al-Bajalan 3 Movement disorders • Movement disorders present with a wide range of symptoms. • They may be genetic or acquired, & the most important is Parkinson’s disease. • Most movement disorders are categorized clinically, with few confirmatory investigations available other than for those with a known gene abnormality. 10/29/2024 Ass. Prof. Sarwer Al-Bajalan 4 Parkinsonism • Parkinsonism is a clinical syndrome characterised by: • Bradykinesia (slowing of Movement) • Rigidity (increased tone through-out movement), • Tremor & • Loss of postural reflexes. • More than 80% of cases are due to Idiopathic Parkinson’s disease (PD). • It may be caused by: • drugs, • degenerative disease (e.g. Alzheimer’s), • anoxic or other injuries, or • genetic conditions (e.g. Huntington’s disease), 10/29/2024 Ass. Prof. Sarwer Al-Bajalan 5 Causes of Parkinsonism • Idiopathic Parkinson’s disease (at least 80% of parkinsonism) • Cerebrovascular disease • Anoxic brain injury • Drugs & toxins • Antipsychotic (older & ‘atypical’) • Metoclopramide • Prochlorperazine • Tetrabenazine • Sodium valproate • Lithium • Manganese • MPTP • Other degenerative diseases • Dementia with Lewy bodies • Progressive supranuclear palsy • Multiple system atrophy • Corticobasal degeneration • Alzheimer’s disease • Genetic • Huntington’s disease • Fragile X tremor ataxia syndrome** • Dopa-Responsive Dystonia • Spinocerebellar Ataxias (particularly SCA 3) • Wilson’s disease 10/29/2024 Ass. Prof. Sarwer Al-Bajalan 6 Idiopathic Parkinson’s disease • PD is a progressive & incurable condition, with a variable prognosis. • Whilst motor symptoms are the usual presenting features, cognitive impairment, depression & anxiety become increasingly common as the disease progresses, & significantly reduce quality of life. • PD has an annual incidence of 18/100 000 in the UK & a prevalence of 180/100 000. • Average age of onset is ~60 yrs; < 5% present under 40. • Single genes causing PD have been identified in a small proportion of cases. • Having a first-degree relative with PD increases the risk by 2–3 times. 10/29/2024 Ass. Prof. Sarwer Al-Bajalan 7 Clinical features: Motor features • The presentation is usually asymmetrical, e.g. a resting tremor in an upper limb. • Typical features of an established case include: Bradykinesia: Slowness in initiating or repeating movements, impaired fine movements causing small handwriting (micrographia) & expressionless face (hypomemia). Slow to start walking, reduced arm swing, small steps & tendency to run (festination). Tremor: rest (3-4 Hz), less on action; starts in fingers/thumb & may affect arms, legs, feet, jaw & tongue. Rigidity: Cogwheel type mostly ULs limbs; plastic (lead pipe) type mostly affects LLs. 10/29/2024 Ass. Prof. Sarwer Al-Bajalan 8 Physical signs in Parkinson’s disease 10/29/2024 Ass. Prof. Sarwer Al-Bajalan 9 IPD Video 10/29/2024 Ass. Prof. Sarwer Al-Bajalan 10 10/29/2024 Ass. Prof. Sarwer A

Lumbar Spine Disorders Dr. Khalid Shokor Mahmood Neurosurgery Contents • Low Back Pain • Lumbar Disc Herniation • Cauda Equina Syndrome • Lumbar Spinal Stenosis • Spondylolisthesis Low Back Pain • The world is divided into three groups of people: • those who are having back pain • those who have had back pain • those who will have back pain! Low Back Pain (LBP) • Low back pain (LBP) is one of the most common reasons people seek medical help or miss work. • Back pain is the leading cause of disability worldwide. • After the common cold, it is the number two cause for loss of time at work. • Chronic LBP is defined as pain lasting longer than 3 months. • At any given moment, an estimated 12% of the global population suffers from LBP. • The prevalence of LBP reaches 38% annually, and lifetime prevalence has been reported to be as high as 80%. • Although the vast majority of the population will suffer from at least one episode of LBP, the clinical course is typically benign; more than 90% will achieve full functional recovery within 3 months of symptom onset. • Your responsibility as junior doctors is to make initial assessment to detect “red flags” (indicating potentially serious pathology). “Red flags” for patients with low back pain Condition Red flag Cancer or infection 1. age > 50 or < 20 y 2. history of cancer 3. unexplained weight loss 4. immunosuppression 5. UTI, IV drug abuse, fever, or chills 6. back pain not improved with rest Spinal fracture 1. history of significant trauma 2. prolonged use of steroids 3. age > 70 y cauda equina syndrome or severe neurologic compromise 1. acute onset of urinary retention or overflow incontinence 2. fecal incontinence or loss of anal sphincter tone 3. saddle anesthesia 4. global or progressive weakness in the LEs Lumbar Disc Herniation Lumbar Disc Herniation and Radiculopathy Pathophysiology • Intervertebral discs may undergo degenerative changes, this includes desiccation and fibrosis, which further leads to fissuring and tearing, which in turn increases the risk of herniation of disc material outside the normal confines of the disc space. • Central and paramedial disc herniations • The posterior longitudinal ligament is strongest in the midline. This may explain why most posteriorly herniated lumbar discs (HLD) occur slightly off to one side (paramedian). • In the lumbar spine this characteristically compresses the nerve root that is passing downward (that is, the nerve in the lateral recess just before it exits through the neural foramen of the level below; e.g., at L4–5 this would typically involve the L5 nerve root). Clinical Findings, Symptoms • Symptoms may start off with back pain, which after days or weeks gradually or sometimes suddenly yields to radiculopathy often with reduction of the back pain. • Radiculopathy is dysfunction of a nerve root; signs and symptoms may include: • Pain radiating down in LL in the distribution of that nerve root • dermatomal sensory disturbances • weakness of muscles innervated by that nerve root • hypoactive muscle stretch reflexes of the same muscles • Pain relief upon flexing the knee and thigh • Patients generally avoid excessive movements; • Increased pain with coughing, sneezing, or straining at the stool • Bladder symptoms: the incidence of voiding dysfunction is 5-10%. Most common: difficulty voiding, straining, or urinary retention. • Back pain per se is usually a minor component (only 1% of patients with acute low back pain have sciatica), and when it is the only presenting symptom, other causes should be sought. Clinical Findings, Signs • Lasègue’s sign, or positive straight leg raising test (SLRT). While patient supine, raise afflicted limb by the ankle and pain is elicited at less than 60°. It is positive in lower lumbar disc herniations (L4-5 and L5-S1). • Crossed straight leg-raising test. SLRT on the painless leg causes contralateral limb pain. • Tenderness over the sciatic notch • Femoral stretch test. While patient prone, examine

CNS Developmental Abnormalities Dr. Ali Omar Saadoon Neurosurgeon 2024-2025 LEARNING OBJECTIVES To understand: • Most common CNS congenital abnormality and there managements • some ideas about the infrequent congenital abnormality 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar 2 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar 3 Spinal dysraphism (spina bifida) Neural Tube Defect NTD. Failure of the neural tube to close completely will produce one of many pattern of neurospinal dysraphism ( spina bifida). 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar 4 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar 5 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar 6 Embryology The neural plate appears on the 17th day of gestation as a thickening of the embryonic ectoderm over the notochord. This neuroectoderm gives rise to the central nervous system. On day 18, the neural plate invaginates along the midline, forming the neural groove with the neural folds on either side. By the end of the third gestational week, the neural folds fuse forming the neural tube. Fusion begins at the hindbrain-cervical junction and the hindbrain- forebrain junction and proceeds rostrally and caudally from these two points. Then, the anterior and posterior ends (neuropores) close, completing the process. The cranial end of the neural tube closes by 24 days and the caudal by 25-26 days. Then, the neural tube is covered dorsally by mesenchyme that forms the vertebral arches and skull. Closure of the vertebral arches is completed at 11 weeks of gestation. Defective closure of the neural tube results in neural tube defects (NTDs). Depending on the point of the defect NTDs may affect the brain (anencephaly, encephalocele) or spinal cord (spina bifida). 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar 7 spina bifida 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar 8 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar 9 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar 10 spina bifida occulta Epidemiology: it is found in 10%-20% of population, most common at L5 or S1 Definition : Congenital absence of spinous process and variable amount of lamina (posterior vertebral arch),no visible exposure or herniation of meninges or neural tissue. Etiology : failure of fusion of the posterior neural arch. Clinical features: no obvious clinical signs, presence of lumbosacral cutaneous abnormalities( dimple, sinus, port-wine stain, or hair tuft) should increase suspicion of an underlying anomaly(lipoma, dermoid, diastomatomyelia) . the defect may be palpable. Investigation: Plain film( absence of the spinous process along with minor amount of the neural arch , U/S,MRI to exclude spinal anomalies Treatment : requires no treatment , if not associated with underlying anomaly. 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar 11 hairy patch, an area of pigmentation, fatty lump or the dermal sinus).often an incidental finding 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar 12 usually of no clinical importance when it occurs alone However it may be occasionally associated with diastematomyelia,tethered cord , lipoma, or dermoid , epidermoid tumors with thicking of the filum terminale. 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar 13 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar 14 Spina Bifida Aperta Meningocele Definition: Herniation of meningeal tissue and CSF through a defect in the spine, without associated herniation of neural tissue Etiology: Primary failure of neural tube closure. Clinical features: Most common in lumbosacral area, usually no disability, low incidence of associated anomalies and hydrocephalus. Investigation: Plain films, CT,MRI, U/S, echo, genitourinary investigation. Treatment: Surgical excision and tissue repair (excellent result) myelomeningocele • Definition: herniation of meningeal and CNS tissue through a defect in the spine • It is the most common significant birth defect involving the spine, 1-2/1000 live births • Etiology: same as meningocele ……? -history of affected pregnancy should take 4 mg of folic acid/day -All women of childbearing age who have not had a fetus or infant with a neural tube defect should consume at least 400 microgram(0.4 milligrams)/day of folate -Embryologically the abnormality manifested between 3-4 weeks of gestation • Hydrocephalus (HCP) devel

CNS Infection Dr. Ali Omar Neurosurgeon 2024-2025 LEARNING OBJECTIVES To understand: •Management of brain abscess and other CNS infection that has relation with surgery or managed by surgery •to take an introduction about Stereotactic surgery •To understand the brain death 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar Saadoon 2 Brain abscess • Localized collection of pus surrounded by a pyogenic membrane, inside the brain called brain abscess. • 8% of all intracranial space-occupying lesions • %2 (most industrialized nations) 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar Saadoon 3 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar Saadoon 4 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar Saadoon 5 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar Saadoon 6 Risk factors 1.pulmonary abscess 2. AV fistulas, 3. congenital cyanotic heart disease, 4.immune compromise, 5.chronic sinusitis/otitis, 6.dental procedures 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar Saadoon 7 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar Saadoon 8 The microorganism may reach the brain by different mechanisms: 1. Hematogenous spread from remote reservoir of infection( most common) • Adults; chest is the most common (lung abscess, bronchiectasis, empyema) • children; congenital cyanotic heart disease with R to L shunt • Immunosuppression (AIDS-toxoplasmosis) 2. Contiguous site of infection (infected sinus, naso/oropharynx) e.g. otitis media, mastoiditis, sinusitis, osteomyelitis, dental abscess. May gain access to the venous drainage of the brain via valve-less emissary veins that drain these region 3. Direct inoculation from penetrating trauma or during neurosurgical procedures.(trauma, iatrogenic…post operative, congenital defect….dermal sinus • In 20% of patients, no cause can be identified 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar Saadoon 9 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar Saadoon 10 The responsible microorganism •Otitis media : anaerobic streptococci, Haemophilus and Gram-negative anaerobic •Sinusitis: streptococcus milleri and staphylococcus •Traumatic: staphylococcus •Hematogenous spread : various organism are involved 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar Saadoon 11 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar Saadoon 12 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar Saadoon 13 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar Saadoon 14 Investigation • CBC : leucocytosis, • raised ESR, and C-reactive protein ( may initially be normal) • Blood culture : positive in 30% but should always be obtained . • Culture specimen : from any other suspected focus of infection should also be collected . • CT and MRI: may show ring enhancing mass lesion (differ with the stage of the abscess) 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar Saadoon 15 Treatment • identify the bacterial organism • IV antibiotics(6-8 wks) • surgery (drain or excision) • treat the cause • anticonvulsant therapy 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar Saadoon 16 Surgical treatment • Needle aspiration : may be performed under local anesthesia if necessary ,may be the only surgical treatment required but some time must be followed with excision, recommended with multiple or deep lesion, or with thin walled • Surgical excision: abscess is removed as any well- encapsulated tumor; the length of time on antibiotics can be shortened. 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar Saadoon 17 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar Saadoon 18 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar Saadoon 19 complications •With abscess rupture : ventriculitis, meningitis, venous sinus thrombosis •Long term neurologic sequelae in up to 50% of patients ( hemiparesis ,seizures) •CSF obstruction •Recurrence of abscess •Transtentorial herniation due to increased ICP prognosis 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar Saadoon 21 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar Saadoon 22 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar Saadoon 23 subdural empyemas • uncommon • sinusitis or mastoiditis • these patients are systemically unwell • with rapidly progressive neurological signs • a depressed level of consciousness and hemiparesis • Epilepsy 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar Saadoon 24 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar Saadoon 25 Treatment Like intracranial abscess, complications include refractory status epilepticus and cortical venous sinus thrombosis 10/29/2024 Dr. Ali Omar Saadoon 26 Posttraumatic meningitis • Occurs in 1-20% of patients with moderate to

Evaluation of Headache Dr. Mohammed Tahir Kurmanji Neurologist FICMS University of Suleimania Department of Neurology ⚫Headache is ache or pain in the head arising from the pain-sensitive intracranial or extracranial structures or referred from the surrounding structures. ⚫Headache arrange from mild to severe probably serious type. ⚫Headache may divided into primary, secondary or referral types. Pain Sensitivity of Cranial Structures ⚫Cranial venous sinuses with afferent veins ⚫Arteries at base of brain and their major branches ⚫Arteries of the dura ⚫Dura near base of brain and large arteries ⚫Dural, Cranial and extracranial nerves ⚫All extracranial structures ⚫Brain parenchyma ⚫Ependyma ⚫Choroid ⚫Pia ⚫Arachnoid ⚫Dura over convexity ⚫Skull Pain-Sensitive Pain-Insensitive subarachnoid space ventricles General Mechanisms of Headache ⚫Traction on major intracranial vessels ⚫Distention, dilation of intracranial arteries ⚫Inflammation near pain sensitive structures ⚫Direct pressure on cranial or cervical nerves ⚫Sustained contraction of scalp or neck muscles ⚫Stimulation from disease of eye, ear, nose and sinuses (referred pain) Epidemiology ⚫60-75% of adults have at least one headache/year ⚫5-10% will seek physician evaluation ⚫Less than 10% of ED patients with chief complaint of headache will have emergent secondary cause Epidemiology Tension Migraine Cold Stimulus APPROACH TO DEMENTIA Dr. Mohammed Kurmanji MBChB, FEBN, FIBMS(Neurologist) Ass. Prof. of Neurology / Department of Medicine / University of Sulaimani Consultant Neurologist 1 Outline: Definition Dementia vs Delirium Subtypes of Dementia Cortical vs Subcortical Dementia Mixed Dementia Workup of Dementia 2 Dementia: An acquired syndrome of intellectual impairment that interferes with social & occupational function Associated with impairment in at least one or more of the following cognitive domains: ✓learning and memory, ✓language, ✓executive function, ✓complex attention, ✓perceptual-motor, or ✓social cognition 3 Mild cognitive impairment (MCI): MCI is an intermediate clinical state between normal cognition and dementia. While specific subtle changes in cognition can occur in normal aging, MCI can also be a precursor to dementia. MCI may also represent a reversible condition in the setting of depression, as a complication of certain medications, or during the recovery from an acute illness. 4 Dementia Vs Delirium Delirium: characterized by a disturbance of consciousness and a change in cognition that develop over a short period of time Delirium will develop in about: 10-15% of surgical inpatients. 15-25% of medical inpatients. 30% of ICU patients. 5 Dementia Vs Delirium 6 Dementia Delirium Chronic insidious Acute Recall problem Registration problem No fluctuation Wax &Wane Not common Hallucination common Not present Agitation present Not found Systemic manifestations present Reversible DEMENTIA: D= Drugs, Delirium, Depression (3 Ds) E= Endocrine Disorders* M= Metabolic Disturbances* E= Eye & Ear Impairments N= Nutritional Disorders T= Tumors, Toxicity*, Trauma to Head (3 Ts) I= Infectious Disorders A= Alcohol, Arteriosclerosis 7 Irreversible DEMENTIA: Alzheimer’s Lewy Body Dementia Frontotemporal Dementia (Pick’s Disease) Parkinson’s disease (& Other Parkinsonism's) Heady Injury Huntington’s Disease Cruzefeldt-Jacob Disease 8 Anatomical substrates of memory in Dementia? Medial temporal lobes & hippocampus (also fornix, mamillary bodies of limbic system) . ====➔ Medial thalamus - especially dorsal median nucleus . =====➔ Nucleus basalis of Meynert =====➔ Bilateral damage is needed to have memory loss 9 Dementia Generally Classified to Five Subgroups: Pure memory loss Cortical dementias Subcortical dementias Mixed Dementias Reversible / Treatable Dementias 10 Pure memory loss: causes Herpes encephalitis sequelae - bilateral medial temporal lobe lesions Korsakoffs amnesia - bilateral dorsal median nuclei and mammillary body damage due to chronic thiamine deficiency Vertebral - basilar infarcts - if result in bilateral temporal lobe lesions Transient global amnesia – TIA / migrain Limbic encephalitis - damage to hippocampus/limbic pathway 11 Wernicke–Korsakoff disease  A rare but important effect of chronic alcohol misuse.  This organic brain disorder results from damage to the mammillary bodies, dorsomedial nuclei of the thalamus and adjacent areas of periventricular grey matter; it is caused by a deficiency of thiamine (vitamin B1), most commonly from longstanding heavy drinking and an inadequate diet.  It can also arise from malabsorption or even protracted vomiting.  Without prompt treatment, the acute presentation of Wernicke’s encephalopathy (nystagmus, ophthalmoplegia, ataxia and confusion) can progress to the irreversible deficits of Korsakoff’s syndrome (severe short-term memory deficits and confabulation, and also reduced red blood cell transketolase).  It must be considered in any confused patient; if in doubt, treat anyway.  Prevention of the Wernicke–Korsakoff syndrome requires the immediate use of high doses of thiamine, which is initially given parenterally and then orally. 12 Two subtypes of Cortical dementias Alzheimer’s disease Frontotemporal Dementia (Pick’s disease) 13 Fronto-temporal dementia Fronto-temporal dementia This term encompasses a number of different syndromes,

ALZHEIMER’S DISEASE Dr. Mohammed Kurmanji MBChB, FEBN, FIBMS(Neurologist) Ass. Prof. of Neurology / Department of Medicine / University of Sulaimani Consultant Neurologist Outlines • What is Alzheimer disease? • Introduction • Clinical features • Relative risk factors • Histopathology & pathophysiology • Genetics • Diagnosis • Neuroimaging • Treatment of dementia • Take home massage • AD is a neurodegenerative disorder marked by cognitive and behavioral impairment that significantly interferes with social and occupational functioning. • It is an incurable disease with a long preclinical period and slowly progressive course. Alzheimer’s Disease (AD) Introduction Epidemiology • AD is the most common dementia (65% of all dementias). • The incidence increases exponentially with age over 65 years (5%). • Late-onset AD affects up to 20% of those >80 y. • Early-onset AD (onset < 65 y) is unusual, and in some but not all cases is familial. • Familial early-onset AD makes up < 1% of cases and often follows an autosomal-dominant pattern of inheritance. • The average life expectancy after diagnosis has been reported to be 8 -10 y but may range from 3 - 20 y. Deficits occur in the following domains: o Memory: forgetfulness o Disorientation: confusion about the location of familiar places o Speed of performance: taking longer to accomplish normal, daily tasks o Problem solving: trouble handling money and paying bills o Language: problems with reading, writing, working with numbers end with aphasia. o Perceptual-motor problems: such as trouble getting out of a chair or setting the table o Judgment: compromised judgment, often leading to bad decisions Clinical Features of AD • Cognitive deficits in AD appear & progress insidiously. • Memory impairment, mainly recent events, is usually its first manifestation. • Deficits in other cognitive domains may appear with or after the development of memory impairment. • Executive dysfunction and impaired visuospatial skills tend to be affected relatively in early stage , while deficits in language function and behavioral symptoms often manifest later in the disease course. • Neuropsychiatric & behavioral symptoms are common in middle & late stages of AD, but may occur early in the course of the illness in some patients. • Non-cognitive neurologic deficits (pyramidal & extrapyramidal motor signs, myoclonus, & seizures) can occur in late stages of AD but are uncommon in early & middle stages. Atypical AD • Atypical presentations of AD have been described. These include: » Visual variant (posterior cortical atrophy), » Progressive aphasia, & » Progressive executive dysfunction as the predominant symptom. • Atypical presentations are more common in younger people with AD. • Late-onset AD should be suspected in any older adult with insidious onset, progressive decline in memory, and at least one other cognitive domain leading to impaired functioning. RELATIVE RISK FACTORS • Family history of: – Dementia – Down syndrome – Parkinson’s disease • Maternal age > 40 years • History of: – Head trauma (with LOC) – History of depression – History of hypothyroidism – History of severe headache Histopathology of AD • Generalized cortical neuronal loss (Brain Atrophy) >>> • Senile plaques & neurofibrillary tangles (with amyloid composed of -A4 protein), are precipitated leading to granulo-vacuolar degeneration >>> • Neuronal loss in nucleus basalis of Meynert >>> Pathophysiology Abnormalities & loss of neurons in hippocampus, cerebral cortex & basal forebrain. • Marked decrease in cortical choline acetyltransferase (ChAT) and acetylcholine (ACh) in the cerebral cortex and nucleus basalis of Meynert (nbM) • Degree of cholinergic deficit correlates with degree of dementia. A) APP sticks through the neuron's membrane, partly inside and partly outside the cell. B) Enzymes act on the APP and cut it into fragments of protein, one of which is called beta-amyloid. C) The beta-amyloid fragments begin coming together into clum

Dr. Hawar Adnan Mykhan MRCP ( UK) Neurology, F.I.B.M.S. ( Neurology) Infections of the nervous system can be caused by viruses, bacteria, fungi or protozoa. They may affect the lining of the brain, CSF, brain parenchyma, spinal cord, nerve roots, peripheral nerve or muscle. Infections of the CNS may be divided into meningitis, encephalitis, focal suppuration or inflammation. Bacterial meningitis Bacterial meningitis is caused by a primary infection within the subarachnoid space that causes acute inflammation of the meninges (pia and arachnoid mater). The incidence of bacterial meningitis is 2–6/100,000 per annum with peaks in infancy, adolescence and the elderly. It may be caused by a variety of organisms which vary in frequency according to the age of the host and other risk factors. Bacterial infection reaches the CNS either by direct invasion, haematogenous spread or embolisation of infected thrombi. Also infection may be iatrogenic (e.g. following ventriculo- peritoneal shunt, intracranial pressure monitor or surgery). Common causes of bacterial meningitis by age and risk factors Neonates (<3 months) Escherichia coli Group B streptococcus Listeria monocytogenes Infant and child (>3 months) Streptococcus pneumoniae (pneumococcus) Neisseria meningitidis (meningococcus) Haemophilus influenzae Adults <50 (healthy and immunocompetent) Streptococcus pneumoniae Neisseria meningitidis Adult >50 Streptococcus pneumoniae Neisseria meningitidis Listeria monocytogenes Skull fracture/post neurosurgery Staphylococcus epidermidis, Staph. Aureus, Gram-negatives (Klebsiella, Proteus, Pseudomonas, E. coli, Serratia, Acinetobacter) Pregnancy Listeria monocytogenes Immunosuppressed (e.g. malignancy, alcohol, diabetes mellitus, septicaemia, UTI) Listeria monocytogenes Gram-negatives Streptococcus pneumoniae Pseudomonas aeruginosa Group B streptococcus Staph. aureus Rarer causes Salmonella, Shigella, Clostridium, Neisseria gonorrhoea In community-acquired meningitis the transmission of pathogenic bacteria occurs by respiratory droplets spread or close contact with a carrier. Bacteria initially colonize the nasopharynx by attaching to epithelial cells using outer adhesive pili and membrane proteins. The risk of colonization of the respiratory epithelium is increased by damage caused by irritants such as a preceding viral illness or cigarette smoke. Bacteria then enter the bloodstream and gain access to the CSF via the choroid plexus epithelium or cerebral capillaries. Clinical presentation Meningitis is characterized by the presence of fever, headache and neck stiffness. There is usually associated nausea, vomiting, photophobia and progressive lethargy, stupor or coma, and epileptic activity may develop. Seizures may be focal or generalized and may occur either at presentation or at any time during the course of bacterial meningitis in up to 40% of patients. Meningitis may lead to vascular occlusion and subsequent infarction of the brain or spinal cord, progressive cranial nerve lesions and hydrocephalus secondary to impaired CSF absorption and obstruction. Investigation Bacterial meningitis is accompanied by an elevated white cell count and raised inflammatory markers. In the presence of septicaemia, platelet consumption may occur with loss of clotting factors. There may be associated hypocalcaemia, hyponatraemia and impaired renal function with metabolic acidosis. Imaging should be undertaken urgently to exclude mass lesions, hydrocephalus or cerebral edema which are a contraindication to lumbar puncture. The diagnosis of meningitis is made by blood culture, CSF analysis, staining and culture. Comparison of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) findings in different forms of meningitis Normal Acute bacterial meningitis Viral meningitis TB meningitis Appearance Clear colorless Turbid Clear/opalescent Clear/opalescent Pressure Normal Increased Normal or increased Increased Cells 0–5/mm3 100– 60,000/mm3 5–1000/mm3 5–1000/mm3 Polymorphs N

Epilepsy ABAS N. SIDIQ MBChB; FKHCMS (Neurology) Lecturer of Neurology College of Medicine, UoS 3/8/2025 ABAS N. SIDIQ 1 Learning objectives • By the end of epilepsy lecture the student should be able to: • Knows the definition epilepsy • Knows some of the pathophysiology of epilepsy and seizures. • Knows the different types of epilepsy. • Knows different pharmacological and non pharmacological treatment of epilepsy. • Knows the definition of status epilepticus . • Knows how to manage status epilepticus. 3/8/2025 ABAS N. SIDIQ 2 Definition • Seizure: is defined as a sudden, abnormal attack of movements (usually violent) or a sudden abnormal behavior. • Seizure is Latin: sacire which means to take possession of the state of having. Seizures are of two types: epileptic seizures and non-epileptic (psychogenic) seizures. Epilepsy; It is a sudden, abnormal, excessive and synchronous cerebral cortical neuronal discharge manifested clinically as motor, sensory, autonomic or psychic symptoms with or without loss of consciousness. 3/8/2025 ABAS N. SIDIQ 3 Epidemiology of Seizures and Epilepsy Seizures •Incidence: 80/100,000 per year Epilepsy •Incidence: 45/100,000 per year •prevalence: 0.5-1% 3/8/2025 ABAS N. SIDIQ 4 • Epilepsy; is considered to be a disease of the brain defined by any of the following conditions: • 1. At least two unprovoked (or reflex) epileptic seizures occurring more than 24 hours apart. • 2. One unprovoked (or reflex) epileptic seizure and a probability of further unprovoked epileptic seizures similar to the general recurrence risk (at least 60%) after two unprovoked epileptic seizures, occurring over the next 10 years. 3/8/2025 ABAS N. SIDIQ 5 3/8/2025 ABAS N. SIDIQ 6 Remote symptomatic seizure Todd’s paralysis 3/8/2025 ABAS N. SIDIQ 7 • 3. Diagnosis of an epilepsy syndrome: • Epilepsy syndrome is defined as epileptic seizures that have a group of features occurring together like age of onset, type of epileptic seizure, part of the brain involved, genetic information, EEG features, course of the disease, treatment, and prognosis. 3/8/2025 ABAS N. SIDIQ 8 3/8/2025 ABAS N. SIDIQ 9 Provoked epileptic seizures (acute symptomatic epileptic seizures): • Provoked epileptic seizures (acute symptomatic epileptic seizures) are epileptic seizures that occur within one week from a recognizable cause • may recur whenever the precipitating situation is present, but do not happen at other times in absence of the provocative factor and thus do not constitute epilepsy. 3/8/2025 ABAS N. SIDIQ 10 Medication ➔ Aminophylline, bupivacaine, bupropion, butyrophenones Recreational drugs➔ Amphetamines, cocaine, methylphenidate, psilocybin, psilocin Alcohol consumption/ withdrawal Metabolic ➔ low glucose, low Na, low calcium, low Mg, 3/8/2025 ABAS N. SIDIQ 11 Unprovoked epileptic seizures: • occur without a clear recognizable or occur longer than one week from a recognizable cause (this is called a remote symptomatic epileptic seizure); • so unprovoked epileptic seizures include two categories: epileptic seizures without any recognizable cause and remote symptomatic epileptic seizures. 3/8/2025 ABAS N. SIDIQ 12 Evaluation of a First Seizure ⬧History, physical ⬧Blood tests: CBC, electrolytes, glucose, calcium, magnesium, phosphate, hepatic and renal function ⬧Lumbar puncture (only if meningitis or encephalitis suspected and potential for brain herniation is excluded) ⬧Blood or urine screen for drugs ⬧Electroencephalogram (EEG) ⬧CT or MR brain scan 3/8/2025 ABAS N. new_batch/main\\4. Psychiatry & Neurolog.text: Diseases Of The Peripheral Nervous System ABAS N. SIDIQ MBChB; FKHCMS (Neurology) Lecturer of Neurology College of Medicine, UoS 10/29/24 ABAS N. SIDIQ 1 Learning objectives 10/29/24 ABAS N. SIDIQ 2 Different mechanisms of damage to Peripheral Nerves. Definitions of different types of neuropathy. Clinical presentations of different and important neuropathy. Clinical presentation and management of AIDP. • Numerous inherited and acquired pathological processes may affect the nerve roots (radiculopathy), the nerve plexuses (plexopathy) and/or the individual nerves (neuropathy). • Cranial nerves 3-12 share the same tissue characteristics as peripheral nerves elsewhere and are subject to the same range of diseases. • Nerve fibers of different types (motor, sensory or autonomic) and of different sizes may be variably involved. • Disorders may be primarily directed at the axon, the myelin sheath (Schwann cells) or both. ABAS N. SIDIQ 3 10/29/24 Mechanisms Of Damage To Peripheral Nerves • Peripheral nerves consist of two principal cellular structures - the axon and its anterior horn cell, and the myelin sheath, produced by Schwann cells between each node of Ranvier. • Six principal mechanisms, some coexisting, cause nerve malfunction: • Demyelination: When the Schwann cell is damaged, the myelin sheath is disrupted. This causes marked slowing of conduction, seen for example in Guillain-Barré syndrome, post-diphtheritic neuropathy and many hereditary sensorimotor neuropathies ABAS N. SIDIQ 4 10/29/24 • Axonal degeneration: The primary damage is in the axon, which dies back from the periphery. Conduction velocity tends to remain normal. Axonal degeneration occurs typically in toxic neuropathies. • Wallerian degeneration: This describes the changes following nerve section. Both axon and distal myelin sheath degenerate over several weeks. • Compression: Focal demyelination at the point of compression. The myelin sheath is disrupted. This occurs typically in entrapment neuropathies e.g. carpal tunnel syndrome. ABAS N. SIDIQ 5 10/29/24 • Infarction: Microinfarction of vasa nervorum occurs in arteritis, e.g. polyarteritis nodosa, and in diabetes mellitus. Wallerian degeneration occurs distal to the ischaemic zone. • Infiltration: Peripheral nerves are infiltrated by inflammatory cells in leprosy, by malignant cells or granulomas in sarcoidosis. ABAS N. SIDIQ 6 10/29/24 Nerve Regeneration • Regeneration occurs either by remyelination, when recovering Schwann cells spin new myelin sheaths around the axon, or by axonal growth down the nerve sheath and sprouting from the axonal stump. • Axonal growth takes place at a rate of up to 1 mm daily. ABAS N. SIDIQ 7 10/29/24 Definitions • Neuropathy means a pathological process affecting a peripheral nerve or nerves. • Mononeuritis Simplex means a process affecting a single nerve. • Mononeuritis multiplex means a process affecting several or multiple nerves usually sequentially. • Polyneuropathy describes diffuse, symmetrical disease, usually beginning peripherally. The course is either acute, chronic, static, progressive, relapsing or towards recovery. Polyneuropathies are motor, sensory, sensorimotor (i.e. mixed) and autonomic. They are classified broadly into demyelinating and axonal types, depending upon which principal pathological process predominates. ABAS N. SIDIQ 8 10/29/24 • Radiculopathy means disease affecting nerve roots. • Plexopathy means a disease affecting the nerve plexuses • Diagnosis is made by clinical pattern, nerve conduction studies, nerve biopsy (usually sural or radial nerve) and identification of systemic or genetic disease. ABAS N. SIDIQ 9 10/29/24 Focal Neuropathy (Mononeuropathy) Entrapment neuropathy è is the cause. Pressure è damages the myelin sheath, and neurophysiology will show slowing of conduction over the relevant site. Sustained or severe pressure è axons, demonstrable as loss of the sensory action potential distal to the site of compression. è single nerve lesion

Neuromuscular Junction and Muscle Diseases ABAS N. SIDIQ MBChB; FKHCMS (Neurology) Lecturer of Neurology College of Medicine, UoS 10/29/24 ABAS N. SIDIQ 1 Learning objectives Definition of myopathy, myositis , muscular dystrophy, myasthenia, myotonia. Classifications of muscle diseases. Proper investigations of muscles diseases. Different presentation and clinical features of different muscles diseases. Proper management of different muscles diseases. 10/29/24 ABAS N. SIDIQ 2 Definitions • Myopathy describes diseases of voluntary muscle generally. • Myositis indicates inflammation. • Muscular dystrophy describes inherited disorders with progressive weakness. • Myasthenia means fatigable (worse on exercise) weakness. • Myotonia is sustained contraction/slow relaxation seen in myotonias. Weakness without reflex loss is the predominant feature of muscle disease. 10/29/24 ABAS N. SIDIQ 3 Classification of muscle disease • Myasthenic disorders: 1. Myasthenia gravis 2. Lambert-Eaton myasthenic-myopathic syndrome (LEMS) • Inflammatory myopathy: 1. Polymyositis, dermatomyositis, and inclusion body myositis: alone or with collagen disease or with malignancy. 2. Viral, bacterial and parasitic infection. 3. Sarcoidosis 10/29/24 ABAS N. SIDIQ 4 • Metabolic and endocrine myopathy 1. Corticosteroids/Cushing's syndrome. 2. Thyroid disease. 3. Calcium metabolism disorders. 4. Hypokalaemia. 5. Ethanol. 6. Drugs. 10/29/24 ABAS N. SIDIQ 5 • Muscular dystrophies 1. Duchenne muscular dystrophy 2. Facioscapulohumeral dystrophy 3. Limb girdle dystrophy • Myotonias 1. Dystrophia myotonica 2. Myotonia congenita 10/29/24 ABAS N. SIDIQ 6 Investigations • Diagnosis is often made by recognition of the distribution of weakness, wasting or hypertrophy, and muscle consistency . • Genetic probes in patients and family members are helpful in the diagnosis of muscular dystrophies. 10/29/24 ABAS N. SIDIQ 7 10/29/24 ABAS N. SIDIQ 8 10/29/24 ABAS N. SIDIQ • Serum creatine phosphokinase (and aldolase) is greatly elevated in many dystrophies (e.g. Duchenne dystrophy) and in inflammatory muscle disorders, e.g. polymyositis. • These enzymes remain normal in myasthenia gravis and LEMS, and usually remain normal in myotonias 9 10/29/24 ABAS N. SIDIQ 10 10/29/24 ABAS N. SIDIQ 11 ABAS N. SIDIQ 10/29/24 Transient myasthenia gravis is sometimes caused by d- penicillamine treatment. myasthenia gravis and thyroid disease, rheumatoid disease, pernicious anaemia and SLE In 10% è thymic tumor , the incidence increasing with age. Thymic hyperplasia in 70% è below the age of 40. 12 Myasthenia gravis • Acquired condition • weakness and fatigability of proximal limb, ocular and bulbar muscles. • The heart is not affected • women twice men è peak age incidence around 30. • The cause is unknown, IgG antibodies to acetylcholine receptor protein are found. • Immune complexes (IgG and complement) are deposited at the postsynaptic membranes, causing interference with and later destruction of the acetylcholine receptor. 10/29/24 ABAS N. SIDIQ 13 10/29/24 ABAS N. SIDIQ 14 Clinical features • bimodal pattern of onset; it occurs most commonly in women in the 2nd & 3rd decade but there is a second peak of incidence in the 6th and 7th decades with men being predominantly affected. • fatigable weakness in the ocular, cranial nerve, limb, truncal or respiratory musculature. • The initial manifestation is often fatigable visual blurring, diplopia and/or unilateral or asymmetrical ptosis which worsens towards the end of the day. • Weakness remains localized to the ocular muscles in 15% of patients. 10/29/24 ABAS N. SIDIQ 15 10/29/24 ABAS N. SIDIQ 16 • The jaw, facial muscles, speech and/or swallow, neck, respiratory muscles and proximal limbs are all commonly affected. • On examination, fatigable weakness can be demonstrated in any affected muscle by repetitive or sustained activity. • Cooling of the eye with an ice pack may lead to improvement in ptosis but its sensitivity and specificity in myast

Neuro-medical and Mental Health Care’s Course 1 Neuro-medical and Mental Health Care’s Course This course comprises three, relatively thematic, related blocks: Mental Health Care, Neuromedical Care and Neuro-surgical Care. Students will encounter patients with Psychiatric, Neuromedical and Neuro-surgical problems in a number of settings. This placement aims to provide time for learners to address important outcomes linked to theses specialties as well as to reflect back to the Phase 1 teaching in the Social and Behavioural Sciences module in Year 1, Musculoskeletal System class in Year 2 and Head and Neck and Nervous System classes in Year 3 in addition to integration with other classes in Phase 1 like Mechanism of Diseases, Pharmacotherapies, Infections, etc… in addition to patient-Centered approach of integration with other courses in Phase 2 especially with Year 4 courses of Medical Care, Surgical Care and Primary care. All three blocks’ components will consist of a mix of clinical sessions, out-patient teaching and structured teaching, taking in consideration Patient-Centered Problem-Based Style of learning. Each individual component of this course will deliver a number of Core Clinical Problems and attempt to solve these problems in an integrated, both horizontal and longitudinal, style of teaching with the CARE for PATIENT as a Gold Standard. Outline of the Course Duration 6 weeks Class-based structural teaching 60 hours Small Group Problem Based Learning 30 hours Seminars 10 to 15 hours Bedside Teaching 90 hours Total Credits 9 units Module Leader Zamdar H Rasul Karim; FIBMSPsych, Lecturer of Psychiatry and Behavioral Sciences Email: zamdar.karim@univsul.edu.iq Tel: 07701482555 Locations University Campus Shahid Dr. Hemin Hospital Shar Hospital Neuro-medical and Mental Health Care’s Course 2 Faculties Twana Abdulrahman Rahim Professor of Psychiatry Rebwar Gharib Hama Assistant professor of Psychiatry Saman Anwar Faraj Assistant professor of Psychiatry Daniel Saadi Hamid Assistant professor of Psychiatry Zamdar H Rasul Karim Lecturer at Psychiatry Sarwar Jamal Ismael Professor of Neuro-medicine Zana Abdulrahman Muhammed Assistant Professor of Neuro-medicine Muhammed Tahir Muhammed Salih Assistant Professor of Neuro-medicine Hawar Adnan Maykhan Abdulla Lecturer of Neuro-medicine Khalid Shukur Mahmoud Assistant Professor of Neurosurgery Ali Omer Saadun Lecturer of Neurosurgery External Lecturers Abbas Nariman Sideeq Lecturer of Neuro-medicine Yadgar Natiq Abbas Lecturer of Psychiatry Jaafer Omer Ahmed Lecturer of Psychiatry Fahmi Ali Aziz Lecturer of Psychiatry Neuro-medical and Mental Health Care’s Course 3 Mental Health Care Block Key Person Zamdar H Rasul Karim Email: zamdar.karim@univsul.edu.iq Tel: 07701482555 Aims: Main aims of this course are to provide an opportunity to develop knowledge of common and serious mental disorders, their presentation and management, and of the structure and function of modern mental health services. Development of skills in interviewing, obtaining detailed psychiatry histories, examining mental states, and assessing risk as well as reducing the associated stigma to psychiatric disorders. Learning Objectives: By the end of the mental health block, students should be able to: • Use empathic and practical communication skills to elicit relevant patient information to formulate a psychiatric differential diagnosis and management plan in any clinical setting. • Undertake a psychiatric history and perform a mental state examination, including cognitive function. • Consider co-morbidity in psychiatric presentations. • Use a biopsychosocial model to holistically assess patients and to plan management. • Describe the treatment approaches used in psychiatry. Describe the common side effects of the treatments. • Assess patients’ risk to themselves and to others and use this knowledge within the management plan. • Relate a patient’s symptoms, problems and management to their social and cultural bac

new_batch/main\\5. Special Surgery\\Cour.text: University of Sulaimani College of Medicine SPECIAL SURGERY BLOCK INTRODUCTION Welcome to the SS Block. We hope you get the principal yet essential ENT, ophthalmological & dermatological knowledge as well as communication, manual and some surgical skills. Phase 2 Coordinator: Dr. Dana Ahmed Sharif / Prof. Nephrologist Block Leader:​ Dr. Mariwan Latif Fatah /Ass.prof ENT Co-leader​ ​Dr. Haval Ahmed Hama /Lecturer ENT Ophthalmology coordinator: Dr. Tara Mahmood Hassan / Assis. prof/Ophthalmology Plastic surgery coordinator:​Dr. Suzan fazil / assis.prof/ plastic surgeon Block Facilitators/ Teachers ENT Dr. Muaid Ismaeel Aziz,,,,,,,.​ Prof./ENT Dr. Sherko Saeed ​ Prof./ENT Dr. Mariwan Lateef Fattah​ Ass. prof/ENT Dr. Haval Ahmad Hamma​ Lecturer/ENT Ophthalmology Dr. Baxtear Qadir Hamasalih.​ Ass.prof/Ophthalmology Dr. Tara Mahmood Hasan​ Ass. Prof./Ophthalmology Dr. Khalid Saeed Abdulmajeed.​ Ass. Prof./Ophthalmology Dr. Deri Latif Amin.​ Ophthalmology Plastic surgery Dr Ari Raheem Qader………..........Prof./ plastic surgery Dr Jalal Ali …………………….. Ass,Prof./ plastic surgery Dr Suzan fadhil Jaber. .​ lecturer /plastic surgery Dr Dana abdulmajeed. ​ Ass.prof/plastic surgery AIMS OF THE BLOCK 1)​ Take a history and carry out an appropriate examination in a patient with an ENT, ophthalmic & dermatological problem. 2)​ Understand the impact of dysfunction or loss of a special sense for a patient and their career and the resources required to manage the disability. 3)​ Understand acute, common, and important ENT. ophthalmological and dermatological disorders, especially those that that have systemic features or appear in other parts of the MBchB course. LEARNING OUTCOMES ENT: •​ Demonstrate their ability to identify the important causes of nasal blockage, rhinitis, epistaxis, deafness, pain in the ear, pain in the throat, difficulty swallowing, swelling of the neck, facial pain, headache, &hoarseness of voice. •​ by taking an appropriate history to reach a provisional diagnosis. •​ Elicit selectively normal and common abnormal signs in the ears, nose and throat including the use of an otoscope and a tuning fork to test diagnostic hypotheses. •​ Use investigations selectively to confirm diagnostic hypotheses. •​ Formulate a simple management plan including an assessment of the need for referral. OPHTHALMOLOGY By the end of the block the student should be able to: •​ Demonstrate their ability to identify the important causes for ocular discomfort, visual disturbance, a red eye, ocular discharge, &an abnormal pupil. •​ Take an appropriate history to reach a provisional diagnosis. •​ Elicit selectively, normal and common abnormal signs in the eyes to test diagnostic hypotheses, in particular: o​ test and record visual acuity in adults and children. o​ perform the swinging flash light test for a relative afferent pupillary defect. o​ assess a patient for the presence of squint by means of the corneal reflexes and cover testing. o​ examine the anterior segment and external eye with a pen torch, examine the fundus with a direct ophthalmoloscope. o​ use mydriatic and fluorescein diagnostic drops safely. o​ examine visual fields by confrontation. o​ examine the ocular media of both adults and children by means of the red reflex. •​ Distinguish between ophthalmic complaints requiring immediate referral, those which require referral but are not urgent and those which can be managed by the newly qualified medical practitioner •​ Discuss the extent and causes of preventable blindness world-wide Plastic surgery Objectives 1.The basics of wound healing,stages, and problems that lead to delay in wound healing. 2.Reconstructive ladder and its applications in plastic surgery. 3.The concept of body angiosomes . 4.Skin grafts ,Biology,classification, indications and causes of falilure. 5.Flaps classification,indications, and surgical techniques. 6.Skin lesions (Benign & Malignant). 7.Scars (Hypertrophic & keloid) 8.Types of suturing & suture materials 9.Burn

Assist Prof Dr. Alan ABDULLAH Pediatrician and Neonatologist DCH, FIBMS-Ped. Objectives • To understand the incidence of preterm babies. • Discuss preterm coast effective management. • Describe methods for stabilizing preterm babies. • Outline problems of preterm babies. 2 10/25/2025 Dr. ALAN ABDULLAH ➢Every year, an estimated 15 million babies are born preterm ➢This number is rising. ➢An estimated 1 million babies die annually from preterm birth complications. ➢Preterm birth is the leading cause of newborn deaths. 3 10/25/2025 Dr. ALAN ABDULLAH Acute morbidity by gestational age among surviving infants 4 10/25/2025 Dr. ALAN ABDULLAH cost-effective care ➢Antenatal steroid injections. ➢Progesterone. ➢Tocolytics. ➢MgSO4. ➢kangaroo mother care. ➢ antiseptic cream for the umbilical cord. ➢Antibiotics to treat newborn infections. ➢Education and feeding. 5 10/25/2025 Dr. ALAN ABDULLAH 6 10/25/2025 Dr. ALAN ABDULLAH 7 10/25/2025 Dr. ALAN ABDULLAH Oxygen therapy in preterm infants Oxygen therapy should be provided to correct hypoxaemia. However: • excess oxygen leading to hyperoxia is damaging to the lungs and brain and other organs from excess free radicals • for neonatal resuscitation, it is now recommended to start with 21%–30% oxygen in preterm infants, avoiding oxygen saturation over 95%; in term infants air should be used • in preterm infants avoid low saturations (<91%) – increased risk of necrotizing enterocolitis and death, and avoid high saturation (>95%) – increased risk of retinopathy of prematurity 8 10/25/2025 Dr. ALAN ABDULLAH 9 10/25/2025 Dr. ALAN ABDULLAH Fluid balance ➢On the first day, about 60–90 ml/kg is usually required, which increases by 20–30 ml/kg per day to 150–180 ml/kg per day by about day 5. 10 10/25/2025 Dr. ALAN ABDULLAH Birth weight (g) Days 1-2 (mL/kg) Days 3-7 (mL/kg) Days 8-30 (mL/kg) <750 100-200 120-200 120-180 750-1000 80-150 100-150 120-180 1001-1500 60-100 80-150 120-180 >1500 60-80 100-150 120-180 Common respiratory problems in premature babies Respiratory distress syndrome Air leak syndrome Respiratory distress syndrome ➢Definition: (RDS) is an acute illness, usually of preterm infants, developing within 4–6 hours of birth, Characterized….. 50% of babies with birth wt. of 500-1500 gm or < 30 weeks gestation, incidence is inversely proportional to gestational age and birth wt. Antenatal steroid between 24-34 weeks gestation. Cardiovascular disease in children Prepared by Prof Dr.Aso Faeq Salih Pediatric cardiologist 2024 Incidence & Epidemiology : Heart disease in children is mostly congenital. • 8 per 1000 liveborn infants have significant cardiac malformations, 30% of which require intervention in the first year of life. • minor abnormalities of the cardiovascular system are present in up to 53.2 per 1000 live births, e.g. a bicuspid aortic valve • about 1 in 10 stillborn infants have a cardiac anomaly. Common CHD The nine most common anomalies account for 80% of all lesions (Box 18.1), but: • about 10%–15% have complex lesions with more than one cardiac abnormality and • about 10%–15% also have a non- cardiac abnormality. Etiology Types of presentation in different congenital heart disease ASD Atrial septal defect ❖Majority are sporadic . ❖Types : ➢Ostium primum . ➢Ostium secundum . ➢Sinus venosus . Clinical manifestations : All types present with similar symptoms and signs, but their anatomy is quite different. The secundum ASD is a defect in the centre of the atrial septum involving the foramen ovale. Partial AVSD is a defect of the atrioventricular septum and is characterized by: • an interatrial communication between the bottom end of the atrial septum and the atrioventricular valves (primum ASD) • abnormal atrioventricular valves, with a left atrioventricular valve which has three leaflets and tends to leak (regurgitant valve). Symptoms • None (commonly) • Recurrent chest infections/wheeze • Arrhythmias (fourth decade onwards). Physical Examination : Loud 1st heart sound .  wide & fixed splitting 2nd heart sound . Soft ejection systolic murmur ( grade I , II ) With a partial AVSD, an apical pansystolic murmur will be heard from atrioventricular valve regurgitation Investigation : CXR : Cardiomegaly  RA , RV . pulmonary artery is enlarged . Pulmonary vascularity is increased . ECG : Secundum ASD – partial right bundle branch block is common (but may occur in normal children, right axis deviation due to right ventricular enlargement • Partial AVSD – a ‘superior’ QRS axis (mainly negative in AVF; This occurs because there is a defect of the middle part of the heart where the atrioventricular node is. The displaced node then conducts to the ventricles superiorly, giving the abnormal axis. Echocardiography : This will delineate the anatomy and is the mainstay of diagnostic investigations. Treatment : Secundum ASDs, this is by cardiac catheterization with insertion of an occlusion device and in large defects surgery indicated Partial AVSD surgical correction is required. Sinus venosus type for surgery although trials for cath undergoing Treatment is usually undertaken at about 3 years to 5 years of age in order to prevent right heart failure and arrhythmias in later life. VSD ventricular septal defect Accounts for 30% Types of VSD : According to position  membranous , perimembraneous , inlet , muscular . According to size  small , large . Ventricular Defect Small VSD Usually Asymptomatic • Loud pan-systolic murmur at lower left sternal edge(loud murmur implies smaller defect) • Quiet pulmonary second sound(P2). Large VSD dyspnea, feeding difficulties, poor growth, profuse perspiration, recurrent pulmonary infections, and cardiac failure in early infancy. Tachypnoea, tachycardia and enlarged liver from heart failure • Active precordium • Soft pan-systolic murmur or no murmur (implying large defect) • Apical mid-diastolic murmur (from increased flow across the mitral valve after the blood has circulated through the lungs) • Loud pulmonary second sound (P2) – from raised pulmonary arterial pressure. 80% Ventricular Septal Defect (VSD) Large VSD: gross cardiomegaly with prominence of both ventricles, the left atrium. Small VSDs, the chest radiograph is usually normal ❖ Large VSD : ▪Dyspnea , feeding difficulties , poor growth , profuse perspiration , recurrent chest infection & cardiac failure in early infancy . ▪Cyanosis usually absent ,u

Pediatric Cardiovascular Disease Prof. Dr.Aso faeq salih Pediatric cardiologist Aortic stenosis a narrowing of the valve that opens to allow blood to flow from the left ventricle into the aorta and then to the body. Outflow obstruction in the well child Valvular, subvalvular or supravulvalar – 5% Clinical manifestation Depend on degree of stenosis Mild to moderate : asymptomatic • easy fatigability, exertional chest pain, syncope • In infant with severe stenosis can survive only if: • PDA permits flow to the aorta and coronary arteries Severe: Pulmonary stenosis Coarctation of Aorta Management Heart Failure ( HF ) State in which the heart cannot deliver adequate C . O to meet the metabolic needs of the body ❖The primary determinants of SV : ➢Pre load (volume work ). ➢After load ( pressure work ) . ➢Contractility (intrinsic myocardial function ) C.O = HR X SV Cardiac rhythm disorders may be caused by the following: Complete heart block , Supraventricular tachycardia , Ventricular tachycardia , Sinus node dysfunction Volume overload may be caused by the following: 1.Structural heart disease (eg, ventricular septal defect,[3] patent ductus arteriosus, aortic or mitral valve regurgitation, complex cardiac lesions) 2.Anemia 3.Sepsis Causes of cardiac failure Pressure overload may be caused by the following: Structural heart disease (eg, aortic or pulmonary stenosis, aortic coarctation) Hypertension Systolic ventricular dysfunction or failure may be caused by the following: Myocarditis , Dilated cardiomyopathy Malnutrition , Ischemia Diastolic ventricular dysfunction or failure may be caused by the following: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy , Restrictive cardiomyopathy , Pericarditis , Cardiac tamponade (pericardial effusion) Depends on the degree of cardiac reserve . Infants : Feeding difficulties & sweating . Poor weight gain . Irritability & weak cry . Respiratory distress . Clinical Manifestations : Fatigue . Effort intolerance . Anorexia , abdominal pain . Dyspnea . Cough . Orthopnea . ❖Children : Respiratory distress . Increased JVP . Hepatomegally . Edema . Basal crepitation . Cardiomegaly . Gallop rhythm . Holosystolic murmur of mitral , tricuspid insufficiency . Signs : ❖CXR  cardiac enlargement , pul. vascularity. ❖ECG : chamber hypertrophy , ischemic changes , rhythm disorders . ❖Echo : assess ventricular function . ❖Blood gas analysis : metabolic & respiratory acidosis . ❖Electrolyte disturbances : hypo Na , hypo glycemia . Investigations : Underlying cause must be removed or alleviated if possible . General measures : Adequate sleep & rest . Position : older children  semi upright position infants  infant chair . Modification of activities . Diet : increase no. of calories / feeding up to 24 cal/oz, or supplementing breast feeding . diet with (no added salt ) & abstinence from food containing high concentration of salts Treatment : Medications used in treating HF : Diuretics . Inotropic agents . After load reducing agents . What is Kawasaki Disease? It is a group of specific symptoms and physical signs. Affected children usually less than 5 years old. The risk of other children in the same family getting Kawasaki disease is very low even the second time is very rare. What Causes Kawasaki Disease? No actual cause knows May be caused by an infection poison, or toxin, or immunity system to the infection. Some individuals more likely to get this disease than others. Kawasaki Disease: Symptoms and Diagnosis All patients have a continuous fever lasting 5 days or more. 4 out of 5 of the following symptoms are necessary for the diagnosis Non pululent Red Eyes Changes in the Lips, Tongue and Mouth redness, drying with cracking of the lips, and a red strawberry-like tongue. Changes to the Hands and Feet The palms, or inside flat part of the hands, and the soles, or bottoms of the feet may turn red in colour. They may also appear swollen. About a week after the fever breaks, the skin from the fingers and toes ma

dr.Bakir R.Rahid Renal system HEMATURIA and GLOMERULONEPHRITIS BAKIR R. RASHID MBChB; FIBMS PEDITRIC College of Medicine, UoS 11/17/2025 dr.Bakir R.Rahid Objectives (learning out come) 1- to know what's hematuria , definition, classification, clinical approach. 2-to discuss glomerulonephritis 11/17/2025 dr.Bakir R.Rahid HEMATURIA -Hematuria is defined as present of at least 10 RBC per microliter of urine. -The incidence is 2% among school aged children. -Normal children can excrete more than 500.000 RBC /12hr. -RBC can be detected by dipstick confirmation should be done by microscopically examination of 10-15ml of freshly voided urine. 11/17/2025 dr.Bakir R.Rahid Causes of red urine without RBC: A-heme-positive: 1-myoglobin in urine. 2-hemoglobine in urine. B-heme-negative: 1- drugs like:ibuprofine,iron,metronidazole,rifampine,salicylate. 2-vegetable and fruits like:beets,black berries, food coloring. 3-metabolites:like melanin,porphyrine. 11/17/2025 dr.Bakir R.Rahid Causes of haematuria Upper urinary tract causes of hematuria which originate from nephron and interistitium characterized by: 1-brown,cola or tea colored urine. 2-proteinuria >100mg/dl. 3-RBC cast and deformed RBC. 11/17/2025 dr.Bakir R.Rahid CMMON UPPER URINARY TRACT CAUSES OF HEMATURIA ARE: 1-Isolated renal diseases like: IgA nephropathy,post infectious glomerulonephritis, membranous GN,membranoproliferative GN,focal segmental glomerulosclerosis. 2-Multicystem diseases: SLE,Henochschonleipurpura,HUS,polyartritis nodosa. 3-Tubulointeristitial causes:pyelonephritis,interistitial nephritis,acte tubular necrosis,papillary necrosis. 4-Vascular causes: arterial or veinus thrombosis,malformations, 5-Anatomic causes:hydronephrosis,cystic kidney diseases, tumour,trauma. 11/17/2025 dr.Bakir R.Rahid Lower urinary tract causes of hematuria originate from pelvocaliseall system, ureter, bladder and urethra. Characterized by: 1-bright red or pink urin. 2- gross hematuria. 3-blood clot. 4-normal RBC morphology. 5-minimum protein uria <100mg/dl. 6- occurs at the beginning or end of the urinary stream. 11/17/2025 dr.Bakir R.Rahid Common causes of lower urinary tract causes of hematuria: 1-cystitis,urithritis. 2-Urolithiasis. 3-Trauma. 4-Coagulopathy. 5-Bladder tumor. 6-Heavy exercise. 7-Factitious syndrome by proxy. UTI is the most common cause of haematuria 11/17/2025 dr.Bakir R.Rahid Clinical approach to heamaturia Approach to patient with haematuria involves: 1-detail history. 2- complete physical examinations. 3-labrotary investigations. 4- treatment accordingly 11/17/2025 dr.Bakir R.Rahid Investigation of haematuria All patients • Urine microscopy and culture • Urinary protein:creatinine and calcium:creatinine ratios • Kidney and urinary tract ultrasound • Plasma urea, electrolytes, creatinine, calcium, phosphate, albumin • Full blood count, coagulation screen, sickle cell screen 11/17/2025 dr.Bakir R.Rahid If suggestive of glomerular haematuria • ESR, complement levels, and anti-double stranded DNA antibodies • Throat swab and antistreptolysin O/anti-DNAse B titres • Hepatitis B and C screen • Renal biopsy if indicated • Test family for blood in urine • Hearing test (if Alport syndrome suspected) 11/17/2025 dr.Bakir R.Rahid A renal biopsy may be indicated if: • there is significant persistent proteinuria • there is recurrent macroscopic haematuria • renal function is abnormal • the complement levels are persistently abnormal. 11/17/2025 dr.Bakir R.Rahid Glomerulonephritis Definition: -Inflammation and proliferation of cells within the renal glomerulus which may occur as primary condition or associated with a systemic disorder. The majority of cases are immunologically mediated. 11/17/2025 dr.Bakir R.Rahid Pathogenesis Glomerular injury may be the result of : 1- genetic . 2-immunologic . 3- coagulation disorder. Immunologic injury is the most common cause , and results in glomerulonephritis. 11/17/2025 dr.Bakir R.Rahid Etiology Causes of glomerulonephritis: • Postinfectious

Inborn error of metabolism BAKIR R. RASHID MBChB; FIBMSPEDIA College of Medicine, UoS Objectives: -To know the causes of the inborn errors of metabolism. -To be aware of the presentation of a patient with an inborn error of metabolism. -Classifications of IEM -Summarize the treatment options for inborn errors of metabolism. -To have knowledge on some common IEM. Inborn errors of metabolism are a heterogeneous group of disorders that may be inherited or may occur as the result of spontaneous mutation. These diseases involve failure of the metabolic pathways involved in either the break-down or storage of carbohydrates, fatty acids, and proteins. Although any given inborn error of metabolism is very rare, taken as a group, inborn errors occur in 1 in 2500 births, making them quite common. -There is a wide range of presentations and presentation can occur at any time, even in adulthood. -Most important in making the diagnosis is a high index of suspicion. -Genetic diagnosis is essential for confirmation and to allow genetic counselling. -With some conditions, delay in diagnosis may result in neurological damage or death. - Successful emergency treatment depends on prompt institution of therapy aimed at metabolic stabilization Pathophysiological classifications 1- Disorders leading to toxicity due to accumulated metabolite: Aminoacidopathies, e.g. homocystinuria, Urea cycle disorders, e.g. citrullinaemia ,Organic acidaemias, e.g. isovaleric acidaemia, Carbohydrate disorders, e.g. galactosaemia ,Neurotransmitter disorders, e.g. pyridoxine-dependent seizures 2- Disorders of energy metabolism: Mitochondrial diseases, e.g. MELAS, MERRF ,Fatty acid oxidation disorders, e.g. carnitine transporter deficiency ,Glycogen storage disorders, e.g. McArdle disease 3- Disorders of complex organelles: Lysosomal storage disorders, e.g. mucopolysaccharidoses ,Peroxisomal disorders, e.g. Zellweger syndrome presentations Can present in a multitude of ways and at any age, although many present in neonate or early childhood. They should be considered in neonate or children with: - Poor feeding , vomiting , unusual odor - an unexpectedly severe presentation of an otherwise common illness - significant metabolic acidosis - an unexplained respiratory alkalosis -hypoglycemia - cardiac failure or cardiomyopathy - hepatomegaly or hepatosplenomegaly or liver dysfunction - unexpected drowsiness lethargy coma or irritability -early onset seizures - dysmorphic features - developmental regression - sudden unexplained death. In the history, specifically ask about: a family history of inborn error of metabolism; draw a family tree • a family history of sudden unexplained death(s), particularly in childhood, epilepsy, or learning difficulties • consanguinity 8 Urine Odor Inborn Error of Metabolism Sweaty feet Gultaric Acidemia Maple syrup Maple Syrup urine disease Boiled cabbage Hypermethioninemia Mousy or musty Phenylketonuria Rotten fish Trimethylaminuria INBORN ERRORS OF AMINO ACID METABOLISM ASSOSIATED WITH ABNORMAL ODOR Clinical pointers to specific IEM Clinical finding Disorder Coarse facies Lysosomal storage diseases Cataract Galactosemia, Zellweger syndrome Retinitis pigmentosa Mitochondrial disorders Cherry red spot Lipid storage disorders Hepatomegaly Storage disorders, urea cycle disorders Renal enlargement Zellweger syndrome Eczema/alopecia Biotinidase deficiency Abnormal kinky hair Menke disease Decreased pigmentation Phenylketonuria 11/19/2025 9 Neonatal screening test Newborn blood spot screening test (Guthrie test) aims to detect treatable conditions prior to their clinical presentation and allows early treatment to improve outcome. It is offered for all babies on day 5–7 of life with drops of blood from a heelprick collected onto filter paper. The conditions tested for are: cystic fibrosis, congenital hypothyroidism, some haemoglobinopathies, and six inborn errors of metabolism. 1-Phenylketonuria (PKU) 1 in 10,000 Carrier frequency 1 in 50 . 2-MCAD

dr.Bakir R.Rashid P PROTEINURIA AND NEPHROTIC SYNDROME BAKIR R. RASHID MBChB; FIBMS PEDITRIC College of Medicine, UoS 11/13/2025 dr.Bakir R.Rashid Objective of the lectures: 1-to know the causes of protein uria. 2-to discuss nephrotic syndrome the causes , clinical presentations, investigations, management , complications and the prognosis. 11/13/2025 dr.Bakir R.Rashid Proteinuria is common and can be detected in 10% of children between the 8-15 years of age on routine dipstick urine examinations. -in the majority it is a benign disorder or transient and not due to renal diseases. -the dipstick test provide a qualitative assessment of urinary protein because it react with albumin mainly and less sensitive for other protein like(low molecular protein,gamaglobulin ,Bence Jonce protein). 11/13/2025 dr.Bakir R.Rashid -visual changes of color of dipstick can provide a semi quantitative measure of urinary protein excretion. The reading of dipstick is reported as: A-negative B-trace =10-20mg/dl C-(+)=30mg/dl D-(++)=100mg/dl E-(+++)=300mg/dl F-(++++)= 1000-2000 mg/dl 11/13/2025 dr.Bakir R.Rashid 11/13/2025 dr.Bakir R.Rashid False –negative test for urinary protein occur in: 1-diluted urine specific gravity <1005. 2-when the protein loss is other protein and not albumin. False –positive test for urinary protein occur in: 1-concentrated urine. 2-alkalin urine PH>7. 3-gross hematuria. 4-contamination by antiseptics like chlorhexidine,hydrogene peroxide. 5-therapy with phenazopyridine. 11/13/2025 dr.Bakir R.Rashid Proteinuria is of two types: 1-trancient proteinuria. 2-persistant proteinuria. Dipstick detect albuminuria so in child with persistent proteinuria should have proteinuria quantitated with the more precise spot (urine Albumin/Createnin) ratio best performed on an early morning urine specimen to eliminate the possibility of postural proteinuria. -Ratio <30is regarded as normal protein excretion. -Ratio ( 30-300 mg/gm) indicate microalbumin urea. -Ratio >300 indicate macroalbumin urea . 11/13/2025 dr.Bakir R.Rashid -24hr urine collections offer more precise information regarding urinary protein excretion but practically it is not easy . -normal daily protein excretion is 150mg/24hr. --More precisely normal protein excretion in pediatric age group is <4mg/m2/hr. -value between 4-40mg/m2/hr is abnormal. -value >40mg/m2/hr is nephrotic range proteinuria. 11/13/2025 dr.Bakir R.Rashid Transient proteinuria This is the most common cause of proteinuria among school aged children causes are: 1-fever temperature >38.3c. 2-exercise. 3-dehydration. 4-cold exposure. 5-heart failure. 6-seizure. 7-stress. The proteinuria usually not exceed(1+or 2+) on dipstick. These children require no further investigations and no treatment. 11/13/2025 dr.Bakir R.Rashid Orthostatic or postural proteinuria Is the most common cause of persistent proteinuria in school aged children and adolescent. Makes 60% of all cases of persistent proteinuria. -children with this disorder are asymptomatic. Urine excretion of protein is normal while the patient is in supine position, but in upright position protein excretion increase ten fold or up to 1gm/24hr. In a child with persistent asymptomatic proteinuria the first step is to evaluate for postural proteinuria. 11/13/2025 dr.Bakir R.Rashid Diagnosis of postural proteinuria : The child must fully empty the bladder before going to bed and then collect the first voided urine immediately upon arising in the morning. With subsequent testing for any urinary abnormalities by complete urine analysis and determinations of a urine (protein:createnin) ratio. The absence of proteinuria by negative or trace dipstick and spot (albumin/ createnin) on a morning urine sample for three consecutive days with positive urine for protein while the child in standing position will confirm the diagnosis of orthostatic proteinuria. 11/13/2025 dr.Bakir R.Rashid The causes of orthostatic proteinuria are unknown may be because of impaired renal hemo

Dr.Bakir R.Rashid RENAL INJURY 11/14/20 25 BAKIR R. RASHID MBChB; FIBMS PEDITRIC College of Medicine, UoS Dr.Bakir R.Rashid LEARNING OBJECTS: 1-TO DISCUSS ACUTE RENAL INJURY , DEFINITION, ETIOLOGY, CLINICAL PRESENTATION AND MANAGEMENT 2-TO LEARN ABOUT CKD ETIOLOGY, CLASSIFICATION, FOLLOW UP AND MANAGEMENT. 11/14/20 25 Dr.Bakir R.Rashid ACUTE RENAL FAILURE Acute kidney injury (AKI) is characterized by a sudden decrease in the ability of the kidneys to maintain adequate electrolyte, acid-base, and fluid homeostasis along with a reduction in glomerular filtration rate (GFR) . Clinically, AKI is manifested by increases in nitrogenous waste products (blood urea nitrogen [BUN]) and serum creatinine (SCr) and, in many cases, a concomitant reduction in urine output (less than 0.5 to 1 mL/kg per hour) that may be refractory to diuretic therapy . AKI if managed properly it is reversible. 11/14/20 25 Dr.Bakir R.Rashid NORMAL GFR RANGS BY AGE GROUP:(Ml/min/1.73 m2) Premature newborn: 10-20 Term newborn: 30-40 Infant: 50-80 Toddler and children: 100-130 11/14/20 25 Dr.Bakir R.Rashid Incidence AKI occure in 2-3% of children admitted to intensive care unit and in 8% of neonate admitted to neonatal intensive care unit. 11/14/20 25 Dr.Bakir R.Rashid Criteria has been developed to characterize the pattern of acute kidney injury in critically ill children. The criteria were given the acronym of PRIFLE. This criteria depend on glomerular filtrations rate and urine output. 11/14/20 25 Dr.Bakir R.Rashid 11/14/20 25 CRITWRIA ESTIMATED CCl URINE OUTPUT Risk eCCl decreased by 25% <0.5ml/kg/hr for 8 hr Injury eCCl decreased by 50% <0.5ml/kg/hr for 16 hr Failure eCCl decreased by 75% or < 35ml/min/1.73 m2 <0.3ml/kg/hr for 24 hr Or anuric for 12 hr Loss Persistent failure for > 4wk. End-stage End-stage renal disease. Persistent failure for >3 months. Dr.Bakir R.Rashid The PRIFLE criteria has been replaced by KDIGO ( kidney disease improving global outcome) criteria which are the current international clinical practice guidelines and the gold standard for definition , diagnosing and managing of AKI and CKD. 11/14/20 25 Dr.Bakir R.Rashid 11/14/20 25 Dr.Bakir R.Rashid The severity of acute renal failure can be classified in to three stages on the base of rise in serum creatinine: -stage l >150% rise in serum creatinine. -stage ll >200% rise in serum creatinine. -stage lll >300% rise in serum creatinine. 11/14/20 25 Dr.Bakir R.Rashid Common causes of acute renal failure: ARF HAS BEEN CLASSIFIED IN TO 3 MAIN TYPES: 1-Prerenal ARF:is characterized by diminished effective circulating arterial volume, which leads to inadequate renal perfusion and a decreased glomerular filtration rate. Common causes are: -Dehydration. -Hemorrhage. -Sepsis. -Hypoalbuminemia. -Heart failure. 11/14/20 25 Dr.Bakir R.Rashid 2-Intrinsic ARF: characterized by renal parenchymal damage common causes are: -Post infectious glomerulonephritis. -Lupus nephritis. -Hemolytic uremic syndrome. -Henochschonline purpura. -Acute tubular necrosis. -Acute interstitial nephritis. -Tumor lysis syndrome. 11/14/20 25 Dr.Bakir R.Rashid 3-Post renal ARF: characterized by obstruction of urinary tract. Common causes are: -Posterior urethral valve. -Bilateral ureteropelvic junction obstruction. -Urolithiasis. -Tumor. -Hemorrhagic cystitis. -Neurogenic bladder. ---Obstruction must be bilateral to cause ARF.Relife of the obstruction usually results in recovery of renal function except inpatients with associated renal dysplasia or prolonged obstruction. 11/14/20 25 Dr.Bakir R.Rashid CLINICAL MANIFESTATION -Clinical presentation depend on the underlying causes, in patient with prerenal causes there are features of volume depletion. -patient with renal parenchymal causes present with features of the underlying causes with volume overload,hypertention and edema. -patient with post renal causes present with volume overload and abdominal mass. 11/14/20 25 Dr.Bakir R.Rashid LABORATORY FINDING -Elevated concentration o

Neonatal Infections Dr. Hayder Fakhir Mohammad C.A.B.P., F.I.C.M.S., D.C.H., M.B.Ch.B. Assistant Professor/ Pediatrics College of Medicine / UoS Hayder F. M. 10/17/2025 1 Learning Objectives • Neonatal infections. • Neonatal sepsis. • Early onset sepsis. • Late onset sepsis. • Risk factors, manifestations and management. • NEC (Risk factors, manifestations and management). 10/17/2025 Hayder F. M. 2 Infection The neonatal period is the time of highest risk in childhood for acquiring a serious invasive bacterial infection.
