Neonatal infections in the first few weeks of life are often categorized as early-onset and late-onset sepsis. 10/17/2025 Hayder F. M. 3 10/17/2025 Hayder F. M. 4 Neonatal sepsis Definition:- It is a clinical syndrome of systemic illness accompanied by bacteremia occurring in the first month of life. Incidence of primary sepsis is 1-10 per 1000 of live birth and as high as 13-27 per 1000 for infants weighing less than 1500 gm. 10/17/2025 Hayder F. M. 5 Early-onset infection Early-onset sepsis (<72 hours after birth) result from vertical exposure either from ascending infection or exposure to high bacterial load during birth, after rupture of the membrane. The most common organisms are group B streptococcus and Gram-negative organisms (E. Coli, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas). Listeria monocytogenes. 10/17/2025 Hayder F. M. 6 Presentation may be with respiratory distress, temperature instability and the other clinical features of sepsis. A septic screen and chest X-ray are performed. A full blood count analysis is performed to detect neutropenia, as well as blood cultures. An acute-phase reactant (C-reactive protein) is helpful but takes 12–24 hours to rise, so one normal result does not exclude infection, but two consecutive normal values are strong evidence against infection. 10/17/2025 Hayder F. M. 7 Antibiotics are started immediately without waiting for culture results. Intravenous antibiotics are given to cover group B streptococci and L. monocytogenes and other Gram-positive organisms (usually benzylpenicillin or ampicillin), combined with cover for Gram-negative organisms (usually an aminoglycoside such as gentamicin). If cultures and C-reactive protein are negative and the infant has no clinical indicators of infection, antibiotics should be stopped after 36– 48 hours. 10/17/2025 Hayder F. M. 8 Late Onset Infection In late-onset infection (>72 hours after birth), the source of infection is usually the infant’s environment, either the hospital, when it should be viewed as preventable, or community. The presentation is usually non-specific. Nosocomially acquired infections are an inherent risk in a neonatal unit, and all staff must adhere strictly to effective hand hygiene measures to prevent cross-infection. In neonatal intensive care, other sources of infection are indwelling central venous catheters for parenteral nutrition, invasive procedures that break the protective barrier of the skin, and tracheal tubes. 10/17/2025 Hayder F. M. 9 Coagulase-negative staphylococcus (CONS) is the most common pathogen, but the range of organisms is broad, and includes Gram- positive bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus and Enterococcus faecalis) and Gram-negative bacteria (Escherichia coli and Pseudomonas, Klebsiella, and Serratia species). 10/17/2025 Hayder F. M. 10 Initial therapy (e.g. with Flucloxacillin and Gentamicin) is aimed to cover most staphylococci and Gram-negative bacilli. If the organism is resistant to these antibiotics or the infant’s condition does not improve, specific antibiotics (e.g. Vancomycin for coagulase-negative staphylococci or enterococci) or broad-spectrum antibiotics (e.g. Meropenem) may be indicated. Use of prolonged or broad-spectrum antibiotics predisposes to invasive fungal infections (e.g. Candida albicans) in premature babies. Serial measurements of an acute-phase reactant (C- reactive protein) are useful to monitor response to therapy. 10/17/2025 Hayder F. M. 11 Neonatal meningitis, althoug

Viral Infections in Children Dr. Hayder Fakhir Mohammad C.A.B.P., F.I.C.M.S., D.C.H., M.B.Ch.B. Assistant Professor/ Pediatrics College of Medicine / UoS 10/20/2025 Hayder F.M. 1 Learning Objectives • Introduction. • The human herpes-viruses. -Chickenpox. -Shingles (herpes zoster). -Epstein–Barr virus: infectious mononucleosis. -Cytomegalovirus. 10/20/2025 Hayder F.M. 2 Many of the common childhood infections present with a fever and rash. Incubation periods vary from 24hours to 48 hours for viral gastroenteritis to about 2 weeks for chickenpox, but for some diseases, such as HIV, the length of time between exposure and the development of symptomatic illness may extend to many years. • The infectious period usually begins a day or two before the rash appears. 10/20/2025 Hayder F.M. 3 10/20/2025 Hayder F.M. 4 The human herpesviruses There are currently eight known HHVs that cause infections in humans: HSV-1 and HSV-2, VZV, cytomegalovirus (CMV), EBV, HHV-6, HHV-7, and HHV-8. HHV-8 is associated with Kaposi sarcoma in HIV-infected individuals. Herpes simplex virus infections HSV usually enters the body through the mucous membranes or skin, and the site of the primary infection may be associated with intense local mucosal damage. HSV-1 is usually associated with lip and skin lesions, and HSV-2 more commonly with genital lesions, but both viruses can cause both types of disease. Asymptomatic: Herpes simplex infections are very common and are mostly asymptomatic. 10/20/2025 Hayder F.M. 5 Gingivo-stomatitis This is the most common form of primary HSV illness in children. It usually occurs from 10 months to 3 years of age. There are vesicular lesions on the lips, gums, and anterior surfaces of the tongue and hard palate, which often progress to extensive, painful ulceration with bleeding. There is a high fever and the child is very miserable. The illness may persist for up to 2 weeks. Eating and drinking are painful, which may lead to dehydration. Management is symptomatic, but severe disease may necessitate intravenous fluids and Acyclovir. 10/20/2025 Hayder F.M. 6 Gingivo-stomatitis 10/20/2025 Hayder F.M. 7 Skin manifestations Mucocutaneous junctions and damaged skin are particularly prone to infection. ‘Cold sores’ are recurrent HSV lesions on the gingival/lip margin. • Eczema herpeticum – In this serious condition, widespread vesicular lesions develop on eczematous skin. This may be complicated by secondary bacterial infection, which may result in septicemia. Treatment is with intravenous Acyclovir. 10/20/2025 Hayder F.M. 8 • Herpetic whitlows – These are painful, erythematous, oedematous white pustules on the site of broken skin, typically on fingers. Spread is by autoinoculation from gingivostomatitis and infected adults kissing their children’s fingers. A less common cause is contact with genital herpetic lesions. 10/20/2025 Hayder F.M. 9 • Eye disease HSV may cause blepharitis or conjunctivitis. It may extend to involve the cornea, producing dendritic ulceration. This can lead to corneal scarring and ultimately loss of vision. Any child with herpetic lesions near or involving the eye requires urgent ophthalmic assessment involving examination of the cornea by slit lamp examination. 10/20/2025 Hayder F.M. 10 • Disseminated infection • Neonatal HSV infection: The infection may be focal, affecting the skin or eyes, or encephalitis, or may be disseminated. Its morbidity and mortality are high. • Infection in the immunocompromised host : Infection may be severe. Cutaneous lesions may spread to involve adjacent sites, e.g. esophagitis and proctitis. Pneumonia and disseminated infections involving multiple organs are serious complications. 10/20/2025 Hayder F.M. 11 Chickenpox (primary varicella zoster infection) Exposure Spread by respiratory droplets. Highly infectious during viral shedding. Incubation period: 10–23 (median 14). Period of infectivity: 2 days before illness to 6 days after starting illness. 10/20/2025 Hayder F.M. 12

Hypoxic Ischemic Encephalopathy and Neonatal Seizures Dr. Hayder Fakhir Mohammad C.A.B.P., F.I.C.M.S., D.C.H., M.B.Ch.B. Assistant Professor/ Pediatrics College of Medicine / UoS 10/17/2025 Hayder F.M. 1 Anoxia: – Complete lack of oxygen. Hypoxia: – Decreased availability of oxygen. Hypoxemia: – Decreased arterial concentration of oxygen. Ischemia: – Insufficient blood flow to cells or organ resulting in interrupted metabolism and death of the cell or organ affected. Perinatal asphyxia, neonatal asphyxia, or birth asphyxia: is the medical condition resulting from deprivation of oxygen to a newborn infant that causes physical harm, mainly to the brain. 10/17/2025 Hayder F.M. 2 Definition The Perinatal Asphyxia may be defined as hypoxic insult to the fetus severe enough to cause metabolic acidosis, neonatal encephalopathy, and multi-organ system dysfunction. Asphyxia associated with severe bradycardia or cardiac insufficiency will reduce or eliminate tissue blood flow, resulting in ischemia. The fetal and neonatal circulatory system respond to reduced oxygen availability by shunting the blood preferentially to the brain, heart and the adrenal glands away from intestine, kidney, lung and skin. 10/17/2025 Hayder F.M. 3 In perinatal asphyxia, gas exchange, either placental or pulmonary, is compromised or ceases altogether, resulting in cardiorespiratory depression. Hypoxia, hypercarbia and metabolic acidosis follow. Compromised cardiac output diminishes tissue perfusion, causing hypoxic–ischemic injury to the brain and other organs. The response to hypoxia also is characterized by: 1- Release catecholamines from the adrenal glands. 2- Transient hypertension and tachycardia followed by bradycardia and shock. 3- Production of mixture of metabolic and respiratory acidosis. 4- Hypoxemia. 10/17/2025 Hayder F.M. 4 Effect of Asphyxia: 1) C.N.S.: hypoxic –ischemic encephalopathy. IVH , cerebral edema, seizures, hypotonia, hypertonia. 2) C.V.S.: myocardial ischemia, poor contractility, hypotension. 3) R.S.: respiratory distress syndrome, persistent fetal circulation. 4) Renal system: acute tubular or cortical necrosis. 5) Adrenal: adrenal hemorrhage. 6) G.I.T.: perforation, ulceration, necrosis. 7) Metabolic: hyponatremia, hypocalcemia, hypoglycemia, syndrome of inappropriate ADH. 8) Hematology: D.I.C. 10/17/2025 Hayder F.M. 5 10/17/2025 Hayder F.M. 6 The neonatal condition is called Hypoxic– Ischemic Encephalopathy (HIE). It remains an important cause of brain damage, resulting in disability or death, and its prevention is one of the key aims of modern obstetric care. In high-income countries, approximately 0.5– 3/1000 live-born term infants develop HIE and 0.3/1000 have significant neuro-disability. The incidence is higher in low- and middle- income countries. 10/17/2025 Hayder F.M. 7 Etiologies HIE usually follows a significant hypoxic event immediately before or during labor or delivery, especially if the fetus or infant has increased susceptibility. HIE may occur if there is: • Failure of gas exchange across the placenta – excessive or prolonged uterine contractions, placental abruption, ruptured uterus. • Interruption of umbilical blood flow – cord compression including shoulder dystocia, cord prolapse. • Inadequate maternal placental perfusion – maternal hypotension or hypertension. 10/17/2025 Hayder F.M. 8 • Compromised fetus – intrauterine growth restriction, anemia. • Failure of cardiorespiratory adaptation at birth –failure to breathe. Clinical Presentations The clinical manifestations start immediately or up to 48 hours afterwards, and can be graded: • Mild (grade 1) – the infant is irritable, responds excessively to stimulation, may have staring of the eyes, and hyperventilation. 10/17/2025 Hayder F.M. 9 • Moderate (grade 2) – the infant shows marked abnormalities of movement, is hypotonic, cannot feed as cannot suck, may have brief apneas and may have seizures. • Severe (grade 3) – there are no normal spontaneous movements or

Viral Infections in Children cont.. Dr. Hayder Fakhir Mohammad C.A.B.P., F.I.C.M.S., D.C.H., M.B.Ch.B. Assistant Professor/ Pediatrics College of Medicine / UoS 11/4/2024 Hayder F. M. 1 Learning Objectives • Human herpes V. 6 & 7. • Human parvovirus B19. • Enteroviruses. • Measles. • Mumps. • Rubella. • Kawasaki disease. 11/4/2024 Hayder F. M. 2 Human herpes virus 6 and human herpes virus 7 HHV-6 and HHV-7 are closely related and have similar presentations, although HHV-6 is more prevalent. Most children are infected with HHV-6 or HHV-7 by the age of 2 years, usually from the oral secretions of a family member. They classically cause Roseola Infantum (also known as exanthema subitum), characterized by a high fever with malaise lasting a few days, followed by a generalized macular rash, which appears as the fever wanes. 11/4/2024 Hayder F. M. 3 Many children have a febrile illness without rash, and many have a subclinical infection. Frequently clinically misdiagnosed as measles or rubella, or meningitis (when associated with Febrile seizures), or allergy to drugs. Rarely, they may cause aseptic meningitis, encephalitis, hepatitis, or a mononucleosis like syndrome. 11/4/2024 Hayder F. M. 4 Human parvovirus B19 Human parvovirus B19 (HPV-B19) causes Erythema Infectiosum or fifth disease (so-named because it was the fifth disease to be described of a group of illnesses with similar rashes), also referred to as ‘slapped-cheek syndrome’. Infections can occur at any time of the year, although outbreaks are most common during the spring. Transmission is via respiratory secretions from affected patients, by vertical transmission from mother to fetus and by transfusion of infected blood products. HPV-B19 infects the erythroblastoid red cell precursors in the bone marrow. 11/4/2024 Hayder F. M. 5 HPV-B19 causes a range of clinical syndromes: • Asymptomatic infection – common; about 5% to 10% of preschool children and 65% of adults have antibodies. • Erythema infectiosum – the most common illness, with a viremic phase of fever, malaise, headache, and myalgia followed by a characteristic rash on the face (slapped cheek) a week later, progressing to a maculopapular, ‘lace’-like rash on the trunk and limbs; complications are rare in children, although arthralgia or arthritis are relatively common in adults. 11/4/2024 Hayder F. M. 6 11/4/2024 Hayder F. M. 7 • Aplastic crisis – the most serious consequence of HPV-B19 infection; it occurs in children with chronic hemolytic anemia's, where there is an increased rate of red cell turnover (e.g. sickle cell disease or thalassemia); and in immunocompromised children (e.g. with malignancy) who are unable to produce an antibody response to neutralize the infectious agent • Fetal disease – transmission of maternal HPV-B19 infection may lead to fetal hydrops and death due to severe anemia, although the majority of infected fetuses will recover. 11/4/2024 Hayder F. M. 8 Enteroviruses Human enteroviruses, of which there are many (including the coxsackie viruses, echoviruses, and polioviruses), are a common cause of childhood infection. Transmission is primarily by the fecal–oral and respiratory droplet routes. Following replication in the pharynx and gut, the virus spreads to infect other organs. Infections occur most commonly in the summer and autumn. Over 90% of infections are asymptomatic or cause a non- specific febrile illness, sometimes with a rash usually over the trunk that is blanching or consists of fine petechiae. 11/4/2024 Hayder F. M. 9 Hand, foot, and mouth disease Painful vesicular lesions on the hands, feet, mouth, and tongue, and often also on the buttocks. Systemic features are generally mild. The disease subsides within a few days. 11/4/2024 Hayder F. M. 10 Herpangina Vesicular and ulcerated lesions on the soft palate and uvula causing anorexia, pain on swallowing, and fever. Severe cases may require intravenous fluids and appropriate analgesia. 11/4/2024 Hayder F. M. 11 Other enteroviru

Pediatric gastroenterololgy Dr. Heersh HMH Raof Saeed Constipation Constipation is considered in children when hard stool is passed with difficulty every three days that persist for two weeks. Constipation Causes A. Organic • Structural: anal stenosis, anal fissure. • Neuromuscular: Hirshprung disease, spina bifida, • Metabolic: hypokalemia, hypercalcemia, hypothyroidism, diabetes mellitus. • Drugs: Narcotics, anticholenergic, lead, • Intestinal: IBD, celiac disease,CF,tumors. Constipation Causes B. Functional In an otherwise healthy child constipation may result simply from an episode of painful defecation, difficulties during the period of toilet training, inattention to the urge to defecate because of involvement in other activities or discomfort with toilet facilities in school. Constipation Clinical features A detailed history of pattern of defecation may reveal stool-withholding behavior like contracting the gluteal muscles by stiffening the legs while lying down or holding on furniture while standing, some children will squat, push and cry which may be misinterpreted as an attempt to defecate. Accompanying symptoms include abdominal pain, abdominal distention and flatulence. Constipation Clinical features Sometimes rectal bleeding, poor appetite, enuresis and history of UTI are associated. • Soiling (an overflow incontinence of liquid stool) can be present in long standing cases. Constipation Clinical features • Physical examination of the abdomen : – distention – palpable fecal masses. • Digital examination of the rectum is needed to evaluate the sphincter and ampulla Constipation Diagnosis • When no underlying cause is identified by history and physical examination a favorable response to treatment support the diagnosis of functional constipation. • In refractory cases appropriate radiographic and laboratory tests are needed to rule out the organic causes. Barium enema and anorectal manometry are most helpful. Constipation Therapy For functional constipation include: 1.patient education, a regular bowel training program including sitting on toilet for 5-10 minutes after each meal is often helpful in establishing a regular bowel habits. 2. Relief of impaction can be achieved by enema or polyethylene glycol. 3. softening of stool by lactulose, high fiber Constipation Therapy ➢ diet, or mineral oil must be continued until regular bowel pattern has been established for several months. ➢ Biofeedback training may be beneficial in difficult cases. (Biofeedback therapy is a learning strategy originating from psychological learning theories, more specifically from 'instrumental learning' and 'operant conditioning.) ➢medication : ➢ (osmotic laxatives) ➢Lactulose ➢Movicol (potassium chloride ,sodium chloride,sodium bicarbonate,macrogol 3350 ➢Stool softeners :docusate sodium (Colace) and docusate calcium (Surfak) ➢Stimulant laxatives : senna (Senokot, Ex-Lax) and bisacodyl (Correctol, Dulcolax, Dacodyl). ➢Lubricants : mineral oil Thank you

Diarrhea Dr. Heersh HMH Raof College of Medicine University of Sulaimani Definition and significance • Acute gastroenteritis is a clinical syndrome often defined by increased stool frequency that exceeds the child's usual number of daily bowel movements by two or more),or increase fluidity that is considered abnormal by the mother • The term gastroenteritis denotes infections of the gastrointestinal (GI) tract caused by bacterial, viral, or parasitic pathogens • It is the leading cause of illness and death among children in developing countries. • It is an important cause of malnutrition. FLUID MOVEMENT IN THE GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT ➢Large quantities of fluid are transported bidirectionally across epithelial barriers in the gastrointestinal tract ➢he small intestine performs most of the fluid absorption (83 percent) in the gastrointestinal tract Absorption and Secretion in Health versus Diarrheal States Healthy Diarrhea NaCl, Nutrient absorption Chloride secretion NaCl absorption Chloride Secretion Adapted from: Barrett KE: Gastrointestinal Physiology. www.accessmedicine.com Types of diarrhea: ➢Acute watery diarrhea: is diarrhea that begins acutely, lasts less than 14 days and involves passage of frequent watery stools ➢Dysentery: is diarrhea with visible blood in the feces. ➢Bacillary dysentery ➢Amoebic dysentry ➢Persistent diarrhea: is diarrhea that begins acutely but is of unusually long duration(14 days and more). The episode may begin as watery diarrhea PATHOGENESIS ➢destruction of enterocytes (cytotoxic) ➢transudation of fluid into the intestinal lumen and net loss of fluid and salt in the stool ➢decreases the ability to digest food, particularly complex carbohydrates ➢Certain enterotoxigenic pathogens cause diarrhea by stimulating fluid secretion. As examples, Vibrio Cholerae ➢n secretory diarrhea, the epithelial cells' ion transport processes are turned into a state of active secretion. ➢Diarrhea caused by hypermotility, Mechanism of diarrhea There are Two principal mechanisms:- 1. Secretory 2. Osmotic. Mechanism of diarrhea- Secretory Secretory diarrhea: is caused by abnormal secretion of water and electrolytes into the small bowel lumen. This occurs when the absorption of Sodium by the villi is impaired while the secretion of Chloride in the crypt cells continues or is increased. The net result is fluid secretion which leads to loss of water In infectious diarrhea secretory effect on the bowel mucosa can result from bacterial toxins like E.coli, and Vibrio cholerae OR viruses such as rotavirus. Mechanism of diarrhea- Osmotic The small bowel mucosa is a porous epithelium across which water and electrolytes move rapidly to maintain osmotic balance between the bowel contents and the ECF. Diarrhea can occur when a poorly absorbed osmotically active substance is ingested, if the substance is isotonic solution the water and solute will simply pass through the gut unabsorbed causing diarrhea like MgSO4(used as purgative), lactose or glucose. When the substance taken as hypertonic solution water will move from ECF into gut lumen until osmolality of intestinal contents and ECF is equalized. Mechanism of diarrhea- Motility disorder Mechanism of diarrhea- ➢ Surface area ➢ Mucosal invasion Etiology Viral 70-85% of acute gastroenteritis in developed countries ➢Rotavirus: 1/3 of all pediatric acute gatroenteritis hospitalizations. Seasonal variation: increased in winter and decreased in summer. ➢Caliciviruses, astroviruses, and enteric adenoviruses Etiology Bacterial ✓Campylobacter ✓Salmonella ✓ Shigella ✓ E. coli, ✓Yersinia ✓Clostridium difficile Etiology Parasitic Giardia, Entameba histolytica Cryptosporidium Consequences of diarrhea • Isotonic dehydration 70-80% 1. There is balanced deficit of water and sodium. 2. Serum sodium concentration is normal 130- 150meq./L. 3. Serum osmolality is normal. 4. Hypovolemia occurs as a substantial loss of ECF Dehydration • Hypertonic dehydration: 1. There is deficit of water and sodium but

new_batch/main\\𝟮. Child Care \\1B. Theo.file: 𝟮.𝟭 Developmental Problems & Child With Special Needs.pdf
new_batch/main\\𝟮. Child Care \\1B. Theo.text: Developmental problems and child with special needs Dr.Ibrahim K. Jaff Assist.prof. of pediatrics Consultant pediatrician and pediatric neurologist University of Sulaymani College of Medicine Department of clinical Science Content: • Definition • Types • Etiology • Features • Presentations • Other definitions • Clinical approach • Assessment • Examination • Investigations • Management Loading… Definition: Development is ability of child to gain milestones in expected period of age, generally divided to four domains: Gross motor Fine motor Hearing, speech, language Social, emotional, behavioral. Delay may be : global :(when two or more domains affected) Specific: when only one domain affected its called developmental disorder when developmental skills are both delayed and acquired in improper age. Features of developmental problems: May be anticipated antenataly or at birth, or postnataly Cerebral palsy is the commonest cause of motor impairment Autistic spectrum disorder and Attention deficit hyperactivity disorder usually occurs in preschool age or early school age children. Early detection of hearing and vision impairment affect the outcome of development. The medical,social,emotional and educational requirements are complex, needs multidisplinatory child developmental services. The cause may be physical, psychological. (not only neurodevelopmental) Family history, consanguinity, presence of similar condition is significant. Loading… Etiology: Central cause,e.g cerebral palsy Spinal cord ,e.g. spina bifida Neuropathy, central or peripheral disorders Myopathies, congenital or acquired Syndromes Idiopathic Developmental assessment: Observe the child from first look. Make it fun. Use toys,penciles,pictures. Ask the parents about ability of the child. Look at the child's social interaction to you. Presenting features of potential neurodevelopmental problems by age: Prenatal: positive family history e.g. affected sibling or family members e.g taysach disease more common among Jewish people. antenatal screening tests(U.S of fetus) ,DNA testing of fetal cells, and maternal blood for down syndrome, spina bifida,hydrocephalus.Amniocentesis for suspected genetic disorders Perinatal: birth asphyxia,prematurity,intraventricular hemorrhage, leukomalacia dysmorphic and neurocutanous syndromes, abnormal tone, feeding problem Infancy: global developmental delay, neurocutanous disorders, dysmorphic features, vision, hearing problems in parents. Preschool age: speech and language disorder or delay, abnormal gait, poor motor skills, poor social communication, unusual behavior like stereotype hyperactivity, inattention. School age: problems with balance and coordination, learning difficulties, hyperactivety,in attention, learning problems e.g. dyslexia,apraxia,sociality Any age: acquired brain injury e.g.meningitis,trauma.regression of millstones Conditions that cause abnormal development and learning disability: Prenatal: Genetic : like chromosomal disorders e.g. Down syndrome, fragile x-syndrome Structural brain problems: cerebral dysgenesis,microcephaly,absent corpus- -callosum, neuronal migration disorder, hydrocephalus. Cerebrovascular: stroke ;hemorrhagic or ischemic Metabolic: hypothyroidism, phenylketonuria Teratogenic : alcohol or drug abuse Congenital infections: Rubella,cytomegalovirus,toxoplasma,herpis ,syphilis, others Neurocutanous syndromes: tuberous sclerosis,neurofibromatosis,sturge weber sy. Perinatal: Extreme prematurity: intraventricular hemorrhage, periventricular leukomalacia Perinatal asphyxia: hypoxic ischemic encephalopathy Metabolic: symptomatic hypoglycemia, hyperbilirubinemia. Loading… Postnatal: Infection: meningitis, encephalitis Anoxia: suffocation, near drowning, seizures Trauma: traumatic brain injury, accidental and non accidental Metabolic: hypoglycemia, inborn error of metabolism Cerebrovascular: stroke- hemorrhagic, ischemic Nutritional deficiency: malnutrition, vitamin deficiency Others: chronic illness, ch

CEREBRAL PALSY(c.p.) Dr. Ibrahim Khasraw Jaff Consultant pediatrician and Ass.prof. of pediatrics. Lecturer in ped.neurology. Introduction :- - It is the most common form of chronic motor disability. - It is not inherited but some has genetic background . - Prevalence is 2.5/1000 life births . - It is a consequence of damage to the developing brain can result from different causes . Definition :- It is adynamic(static) disorder characterized by presence of all these 4 criteria : 1. The pathological process exclusively affects the brain , (cerebrum or cerebellum)not the other part of the C.N.S. . 2. It affects motor and posture . 3.The lesion in the brain is static(non progressive and non curable for ever),but peripheral clinical Manifestations may change by time 4. Should occur during development of the brain (fetal life , infancy , early childhood). Etiology :- 80% of the causes are antenatal , 10%during birth, . Only10% due to post-natal problems. Prenatal : - Intra uterine infection (maternal) . - Metabolic/endocrine disorder of the child. - Anoxic events to the fetus. - Genetic (syndromes) . - I.U.G.R. , cerebral malformations , cerebral infarction. Etiology (continue) :- Perinatal : - Perinatal stroke . - Intrapartum asphyxia . - Prematurity . - L.B.W. B. Etiology (continue) :- Postnatal : - Kernicterus (bilirubin encephalopathy) . - Trauma (birth injury , head injury ) . - Infection of the brain & menings . - C.V.A. . - Brain hypoxia . - Hydrocephalus . Classification :- Physiological : 1. Spastic c.p. (most common) 60% of cases . 2. Athetoid 10-20% . 3. Ataxic 5-10% . 4. Atonic (rare) . 5. Mixed type , e.g; athetoid with spastic . Classification (continue..) :- Topographic classification : 1. Monoplegic : only one limb affected . 2. Hemiplegia : unilateral upper L. & lower L. affected but upper > lower . 3. Diplegia : all 4 limbs affected but L.L.much > U.L. . 4. Quadriplegia:all of them to the same extent. 5. Triplegia : 3 limbs affected 1 spared . . . Classification (continue..): Etiologic : -Prenatal (congenital c.p.) when the event occurred during intra uterine life .80% -Natal .(during birth process).10% - Postnatal ( acquired c.p.) the event occurred after birth .10% Classification (continue..) Functional : - Class 1 : no limitation of activity . - Class 2 : slight to moderate limitation . - Class 3 : severe limitation (bed ridden or wheelchair) . - Class 4 : no useful physical activity (should be fed and cleaned)by care takers . Classification (continue..) :- According to the site of the lesion : - Pyramidal tract CP : the lesion is in motor cortex, internal capsule ± corticospinal tract, or in the midbrain, Pons, medulla. Usually there is clasped knife spasticity which exadurates during anxiety and illnesses. - Extra pyramidal CP :the lesion either in basal ganglia or any areas except of pyramid., thalamus, sub thalamic nucleus, .the child usually gets rigidity or abnormal movements.(choreoath.,dystonia, tremor. mixed) - Cerebellar type : ataxic c.p. & atonic, usually due to anoxic event happened . Clinical features :- - Usually most of them they have delayed developmental milestones . - Absence or persistence of neonatal primitive reflexes - are alarming signs , e.g; persistent morrow reflex beyond 6 months . - Signs of upper motor neuron lesion in the affected limb. - Other clinical features according to the type of c.ps appear in this way :- Clinical features (contuine..) spastic c.p: All spastic C.Ps are about 60% of cases. Most of them they have no asphyxia, but C.V.A.is the main cause. Spastic hemiplegic c.p; - Decrease active movement in the affected side . - Hand preference at an earlier age in normal side). - Gait : circumduction/tip toe walking . - Fisting of hand in affected side(after 4 months old). - Equinovarus deformity of foot, delay walking≥18months . - Associated problems : M.R. , epilepsy . Clinical feature (continue..):- Spastic diplegic C.P.(little's disease) Most common type, there is weakn

phacomatosis Dr. Ibrahim Khasraw Jaff Ass.Prof.& Consultant pediatrician Senior lecturer in pediatric neurology Neurocutaneous syndromes Definition : Neurofibromatosis NF1&NF2 Incidence 1:3000 live births, its prevalence around 1/50000 Its autosomal dominant disorder1/3 due to new mutation There's Abnormality chromosome 17& neurofibrin (NF1:Von-wrikling hausen dis.) : This type consists of 90% of cases of neurofibromatosis in general for making the diagnosis which are mostly clinical as below : Two or more of these should be present : 1.>=6 of cafe-au-lait spot>5 mm in diameter before puberty, later on >15 mm they are on the trunk and extremities not on the face. Present in 100% of cases. 2.>1 NF (nodular overgrowth of NF) ,or 1-plexiform neurofibroma. 3. Axillaries or inguinal freckles' (hyper pigmented spots) . 4.Optic glioma which cause blurring of vision (visual acuity and visual filed defect). 5. ≥2 or more Iris –lesh nodule on slit lamp exam (hamartoma 2 or more). 6. Bony lesion: sphenoid dysplasia lead to protrusion of eye balls (present with pulsating exophthalmoses) . 7.1st degree relative with NF1 . Other Features :- Skin lesions more common after puberty, may be few or multiple. NF may occur in peripheral N. & cranial N. Visual ,auditory involvement if the 2nd & 8thcranialN. involved. Hydrocephalus, learning difficulty 30% of cases, epilepsy may present in 8% of cases, ↑incidence of brain tumors, hypertension, precocious puberty, psychological disturbance. Pseudoarhrosis, scoliosis ,cardiac involvement may occur . NF2(central) neurofibromatosis: Consists of 10% of cases. Presents in one of these ways: 1) Present with bilateral aquastic neuroma and deafness. More common in adolescents. Deafness & cerebro pontine angle syndrome(facial palsy +- cerebellar ataxia ). Feature of both NF1 &NF2 may be mixed . Both may have endocrinal disorder(MEN). 2) Unilateral acoustic neuroma + parent or sibling with N.F.2. 3) Unilateral acoustic neuroma + any 2 of the following : (meningioma, schwannoma, glioma, posterior lenticular opacity). cont: Phaeochromocytoma ,pulmonary hypertension, renal artery stenosis ,glioma of the brain & sarcoma . Treatment: no radical treatment for both, apart from symptomatic measures to improve quality of life. Surg. For ,hydrocephalus and brain tumor. Drugs for Epilepsy to prevent farther attacks. Tuberous sclerosis (Epiloea): A.D, 70% due to new mutation . Prevalence 1:9000 live births . Hamartoma in many organs :brain ,eye, skin, kidney ,heart (multi system disease) . Abnormality in hamartin & tuberin in chr.9,or16. Due to mutation inTSC1 or TSC2 gen. Prenatal diagnosis available. Clinical features: Cutaneous: 1.Ash-leaf spots (depigmented)better seen by woods light& U.V Should be 3 or more present. It is earliest feature. 2.Shagreen patch (roughened patch) in area of lumber spine. 3.Adenoma sebaceium (angiofibroma) like butter fly on the bridge of nose & cheeks unusual before 5 years old . Neurological Infantile spasm + -developmental delay . Epilepsy: usually focal, salaam attack, myoclonus, atonic, grandmal. But never Absence epilepsy. Intellectual impairment (60-70% of them they have). Autistic features and other behavioral abnormality. Others : Brain glioma and hydrocephalus. Subangual fibroma. Retinal hamartoma. Cardiac tumor (Rabdomyoma,)in early weeks of life, may disappears in infancy in 50% of cases spontanously. Polycystic kidney ,renal angiomyolipoma . Investigation : CT scan show calcification(periventricular), sub ependymal nodules & tubers in 2nd year of life (but may not appear before 3-4 years of life). Woods light for ash leaf spots. DNA study, genetic testing C.X.R. for lymphangiomyoma(special pathognomonic tumor of the lungs). Echo study for cardiac tumor(which causes cardiac outlet obstruction). E.E.G. may detect the type of associated epilepsy. Fundoscopy examination to see iris-lesh nodule or hamartomas. Treatment: There is no radical treatment. Its only supportive For epilepsy

Febrile seizure and epilepsy syndromes Dr.Ibrahim K.Jaff Ass.prof.of pediatrics pediatric Neurologist Objective: To have full idea about seizures, including febrile ones and epileptic seizures How to deal with status epilepticus in either type of them Those seizures are greatly preventable and potentially treatable. Definition :- Seizure is an event due to transient abnormal neural discharge from the cerebral cortex characterized by disturbance either in tone ,posture, sensation, consciousness, or , psychological state . It may be convulsive seizure or non convulsive seizure . So convulsion is a seizure with abnormal movement . Etiology: electrolyte disturbance . like hypocalcaemia, hypomagnesaemia, hypo or hypernatremia. Fever (febrile illness):may induce epileptic seizure in spite of taking antiepileptic drug by the patient properly. inborn error of metabolisms: precipitation of toxic metabolic products in the brain ,acquired hypoglycemia. Hypoxia. ,hypercarbia. Drug induced: either as a side effects or intoxications. C.N.S. infection , C.V.A., head injury. Cardiac cause ,e.g. heart block. It should be known that in seizures there's always brain involvement but the causes may be outside of it. Classification of seizures: - Epileptic S. & non epileptic S. . - True S. & pseudo S. . - Focal S. & generalized S. . - Febrile S. & afebrile S. . Febrile seizures (convulsions): Definition : it’s a convulsion caused by sudden elevation of body temperature. in which thermo-regulation center in hypothalamus can’t adopt it. when there is no metabolic or electrolyte derangement, and the pathology is out of the CNS, the child had no unprovoked seizures before, and the body temperature above 38 C. Types : Simple F.c. , Complex F.c. or typical , atypical F.c. Differences between Simple and Complex F.c. Typical (simple F.c.) Atypical (complex F.c.) 1) Generalized (tonic-clonic s.) 1) Focal . 2) Duration < 15 min. 2) > 15 min . 3) Not reoccurs within the same 24 h . 3) Reoccurs 4)There is no post ictal state. 4)There is post- ictal state Generally both types occur between 6 m – 60m peaks at 14-18 m . Incidence 3-4 % of young children . Precipitating factors : may be in the onset of any viral infections which induce rising body temperatures . Diagnosis of F.c. :- - History , witness , type , duration , Family history of F.c , Ep. -Exam : exclude meningitis by looking at signs of meningism ,like drowsiness , bulging fontanel , neck stiffness , kernig and brudzeniski signs - six hours later not regaining full consciousness, -poisoning , trauma , abuse , neurocutanous syndrome should be excluded by: History, examination, or investigations . Investigations: - Blood : Bd sugar , S. electrolytes: S. Ca, Mg, Na , Bd culture , C.B.P+E.S.R. , C.R.P . - Urine : G.U.E. , C & S Lumber puncture( C.S.F .analysis), indications : 1) 1st attack of fever with convulsion in child less than 6 months old in (fully vaccinated)children. 2)-1st attack of fever &convulsion less than 1-year old (not fully vaccinated). 3) 1st attack below 18M. with suspicion of meningitis. 4)when meningeal signs were positive, in all aged children. 5)Focal neurological deficits.(after doing C.T. scan). 6)persistent lethargy ,not fully interactive child after 6h.of S. 7)Antibiotics used before the convulsion(as partially treated bacterial meningitis) *Radio-imaging not indicated even in atypical F.S. *E.E.G. also has no role in F.S unless you expect that this could be a first epileptic seizure induced by fever. That is Expected when there is strong family history of epilepsy. or the child presented with febrile status epilepticus. Treatment of F.s. :- Seizure control - A , B , C , D . - Give anticonvulsant ,when attack lasted >5m. then as in epilepsy Later on follow the lines of treatment as status epilepticus. -Lower body temp. gradually, best by cold sponges & antipyretics(avoid aspirin) -Search for the cause and give proper treatment. Intermittent prophylaxis: when it was complex febri

Childhood epilepsy syndromes Dr.Ibrahim K.Jaff Assist.prof. of pediatrics Pediatric Neurologist Objective: To be familial with different types of epilepsy syndromes in children How can we control their attacks and improve quality of their lives. How we choose antiepileptic drugs in correct way. Classification: According to ILAE: Idiopathic(with age related onset): Benign childhood epilepsy with centro temporal spikes Childhood epilepsy with occipital paroxysms Primary reading epilepsy Symptomatic: Chronic progressive epilepia partialis continua Syndromes characterized by seizures with specific mode of presentation Cryptogenic: presumed to be symptomatic but etiology unknown Classification: Generalized epilepsies and syndromes: Idiopathic(with age related onset, listed in order of age): Benign neonatal familial convulsions Benign neonatal convulsions Benign myoclonic epilepsy of in infancy Childhood absence epilepsy Juvenile absence epilepsy Generalized epilepsy with tonic-clonic seizures Epilepsy with seizures precipitated by specific mode of activation (Reflex epilepsy). Cryptogenic: (in order of age): West syndrome Lennox-Gastaut syndrome Epilepsy with myoclonic-astatic seizures(Doose syndrome) Epilepsy with myoclonic absence seizures Symptomatic: Early myoclonic encephalopathy Early infantile epileptic encephalopathy with burst suppression Cerebral malformations with focal or generalized epilepsy. Inborn errors of metabolism,presents with progressive myoclonic E. Undetermined E.: Severe myoclonic epilepsy of infancy(Dravet syndrome) Acquired epileptic aphasia(Landau-Klifner syndrome) Focal E.: Idiopathic: Benign infantile myoclonic epilepsy(non familial) Benign childhood epilepsy with Centro temporal spikes Early onset benign childhood occipital epilepsy(panaiotopolus syndrome) Late onset childhood occipital epilepsy(Gastaut type) Familial E.(Autosomal dominant): Benign familial neonatal seizures Benign familial infantile seizures Autosomal dominant nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy Familial temporal lobe epilepsy Symptomatic: Limbic system E. Mesial temporal lobe E. with hippocampal sclerosis Rasmussen encephalitis Hemi convulsion-hemiplegia syndrome Benign idiopathic neonatal convulsions(5th day fits) Prevalence: rare, less than 1% of childhood epilepsy Age of onset: day 1-7 (mostly around 5) Seizure types : partial clonic or subtle(e.g.apnea) EEG : asynchronous Rolandic theta rhythm(interictal) or rhythmic spike &slow wave(ictal) Etiology: idiopathic Treatment: Phenobarbitone,phenytoin,benzodiazepines,may need no treatment if not frequent. Prognosis : excellent ,when treated, it will not reoccurs. Other features : its autosomal dominant inheritance, possibly due to disorder of potassium channels. Early myoclonic encephalopathy(neonatal myoclonic encephalopathy) Prevalence: rare <1% of childhood epilepsy Age of onset : late neonatal period Seizure types : segmental myoclonia, generalized myoclonia, partial motor, after several months evolve to infantile spasm EEG : burst of spikes, fluctuating sharp and slow wave(burst suppression, later change to hyps-arrhythmia) Etiology : usually due to metabolic disorders Treatment : corticosteroids,benzodiazepines,vigabatrine, but usually not effective Prognosis : poor, all infants are neurologically abnormal,50% of them die within a year. West syndrome(infantile spasms and hypsarrhythmia) Prevalence : uncommon (1-5% of all childhood epilepsy) Age of onset : 3-13 months(peaks 5-9) Seizure types : brief tonic or clonic spasms, typically in clusters recurring many times daily. Spasms are commonly extensors(neck and arms, legs extended)or flexor(jack-knife,salaam attacks) or mixed, occasionally there's only head nods. EEG : Chaotic, large amplitude slow waves and spikes &Sharpe waves varying in site and size. Initially hyps-arrhythmia seen when the baby drowsy or in light sleep. May be atypical when there's burst suppression and preserve basal rhythm. Etiology: 70-80% of them is sympto

Neuromuscular disorders Dr. Ibrahim Khasraw Jaff Consultant pediatrician and lecturer in pediatric neurology Definition :- Neuromuscular disease defines by disorders of the motor unit characterized by weak muscles, and excludes influence on muscle function from the brain, as in hypotonic cerebral palsy. The motor unit have four components: 1. The motor neuron in the brain stem cranial nuclei or the anterior horn cell of the spinal cord. 2. The peripheral nerve. 3. The neuromuscular junction. 4. All muscle fibers, each group innervated by a single motor neuron . Spinal muscular atrophy-type-1: :(werdnig –hoffman dis.) :includes 25%, type 2(late infantile type)49%: juvenile type 25%, Adult onset type less than 1%. they Are degenerative diseases of the motor neuron that begins in fetal life and continued to be progressive in infancy and childhood. Werdnig -Hoffmann is a severe infantile form caused by a continuation of programmed cell death that was normal in embryonic life. It’s lower motor neuron disease, affects neurons in the anterior horn of the spinal cord .. Its autosomal recessive disease. Incidence about 10-15:100000 cases. The cardinal features are severe hypotonia, generalized weakness, thin muscle mass, absent tendon reflexes. It’s 2nd most common neuromuscular disease after Duchene muscular dystrophy. SA (continue) : Presents with delayed developmental milestones ,like C.P but here the reflexes are absent. *Usually the child neither achieve sitting nor walking. *They present as floppy infant , with slowly progressive hypotonia of upper &lower limbs ,facial muscles, but extra ocular muscles are intact ,the sphincters are intact also . *Muscles are atrophic and flaccid .Tendon reflexes are absent. Sensation is intact. *Fasciculation of the tongue is quite characteristic . *Heart is normal ,mentality is normal. *Prenatal history reveals diminished fetal movements during pregnancy S.M.A (continue) :- Infants soon develop respiratory distress and are unable to feed. They lie flaccid with little movement. Unable to overcome gravity, and they loss head control. More than 2/3 die by 2 years of age and many die early in infancy. Cause of death is aspiration pneumonia&/or respiratory failure.esp. when the gag reflex will loss . Diagnosis: *Genetic study : by D.N.A analysis shows gene deletion in chromosome number-5. *Muscle enzymes :may be normal ,or raised 1-2 times of normal .It increased with duration of the disease. *E.M.G.: shows fibrillation potential (denervation pattern). *N.C.V. is normal. *Muscle biopsy: shows atrophy of type-1 &type-2 muscle fibers, with absence of glycogen &lipid stores, which is helpful for differentiation from glycogen storage disease. *Sural nerve biopsy : may show sensory neuropathic changes. *Prenatal diagnosis present by DNA analysis of blood, muscle biopsy of fetus, chorionic villous biopsy. Guillain –Barrè syndrome: Its acute post infectious polyneuropathy that cause demylination in mainly motor nerves. Lymphocyte sensitized to the basic protein of myelin have been identified in this disease which support the presumed autoimmune pathogenesis. Typically 2 weeks after an infection by mycoplasma or campylobactor jejany,or immunization by vaccines of polio rabies, influenza, weakness insidiously begins to involve the distal muscles of the lower extremities and progressively involves the trunk, the upper limbs and finally the bulbar muscles. Proximal and distal muscles are involved relatively symmetrically. The onset is gradual and progresses over days or weeks. In cases with abrupt onset tenderness on palpation and pain in muscles is common in the initial stages. Affected children are irritable and bulbar involvement may occur in about half of cases. Respiratory insufficiency may result . Urinary incontinence or retention is usually transient. Tendon reflexes will be lossed. G.B.S. (continue) :- Rarely sensation is involved (cause pain and parasthesia). But autonomic disturbance is not uncommon

Common bacterial infections Dr. Jamal.M.Hussein Objective of learning: ⚫Understanding the specific pathogens . ⚫Mechanism how they produce diseases. ⚫Clinical presentation of differen pathogen . ⚫Diagnostic tests and ways of prevention and treatment. ⚫Complications and outcome. Cholera: ⚫Cholera is an acute secretory diarrheal illness caused by toxin-producing strains of the gram-negative bacterium Vibrio cholerae. ⚫Severe cholera is characterized by profound fluid and electrolyte losses in the stool and the rapid development of hypovolemic shock, often within 24 hours from the initial onset of vomiting and diarrhea. ⚫Administration of appropriate rehydration therapy reduces the mortality of severe cholera from over 10 percent to less than 0.5 percent . ETIOLOGIC AGENT V. cholerae is a diverse species and includes pathogenic and non-pathogenic variants. Only cholera toxin-producing (toxigenic) strains of V. cholerae are associated with cholera. V. cholerae is classified serologically; of over 200 serological groups identified, only 2 (V. cholerae O1 and O139) have caused cholera epidemics. EPIDEMIOLOGY: ⚫Cholera is vastly underreported, and precise measurements of the morbidity and mortality attributable to V. cholerae infection are lacking. However, there are an estimated 3 million cases of diarrheal illness and approximately 100,000 deaths worldwide caused by V. cholerae annually. ⚫Global distribution — Cholera primarily occurs in settings where there is inadequate access to clean water and sanitation. ⚫ Cholera is endemic in approximately 50 countries. ⚫Transmission — V. cholerae infection is primarily acquired by ingesting contaminated food or water . CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS: ⚫Infection with V. cholerae results in a spectrum of disease, ranging from asymptomatic intestinal colonization to severe diarrhea . ⚫The clinical manifestations of cholera caused by V. cholerae O1 versus O139 are indistinguishable. ⚫Incubation period — Cholera has a typical incubation period of one to two days. However, the incubation period of cholera varies with host susceptibility and inoculum size and can range from several hours to as long as three to five days. ⚫Diarrhea — While mild cases of V. cholerae infection may be clinically indistinguishable from other causes of diarrheal illness, the profound and rapid loss of fluid and electrolytes mark severe cholera as a clinically distinct entity. ⚫Cholera stools may contain fecal matter and bile in the early phases of disease . However, the characteristic symptom of severe cholera ("cholera gravis") is the passage of profuse "rice-water" stool, a watery stool with flecks of mucous . It typically has a fishy odor. The diarrhea is usually painless, without tenesmus. In children, the maximal rate of stool excretion in severe cholera is typically between 10 and 20 ml/kg/hour . This rate of fluid loss is not typically observed in other causes of diarrheal illness. ⚫In addition, compared with other causes of childhood diarrheal illness, stool from cholera patients contains a higher concentration of sodium, as well as significant amounts of potassium and bicarbonate ⚫In patients treated with proper rehydration, diarrhea is most severe during the first two days and ends after four to six days .The total volume loss over the course of illness may be up to 100 percent of body weight. ⚫Other gastrointestinal symptoms — Vomiting, frequently with watery emesis, is common, and may begin either before or after the onset of diarrhea. Patients may have abdominal cramping but typically do not have the frank abdominal pain classically associated with dysentery. DIAGNOSIS: ⚫Most cases of cholera are presumptively diagnosed based on clinical suspicion in patients who present with severe acute watery diarrhea. ⚫The diagnosis can be confirmed by isolation of V. cholerae from stool cultures performed on specific selective media. ⚫Rapid tests such as stool dipsticks or darkfield microscopy can support the diagnosis in set

Neonatal Cholestasis Dr. Jamal Mohammed Hussein FIBMS pediatrics Lecturer at college of medicine university of sulaimani Learning outcome • Recognize the definition, causes and clinical presentations of neonatal cholestatic jaundice. • Differentiate the causes of neonatal cholestatic jaundice. • Manage neonatal cholestatic jaundice generally. • Diagnose and manage Biliary atresia • Diagnose and manage Neonatal hepatitis syndrome Prolonged Neonatal Jaundice Many newborn infants become clinically jaundiced. About 5–10% are still jaundiced at >2 weeks of age, when it is called ‘prolonged neonatal jaundice’. This is usually an unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia, which resolves spontaneously. LO 1 LO 1 Neonatal Cholestatic Jaundice Prolonged neonatal jaundice caused by liver disease is characterized by a raised conjugated bilirubin (>20 μmol/L) and is usually accompanied by: • Pale stools • Dark urine • bleeding tendency • Failure to thrive LO 1 LO 1 Causes of prolonged neonatal jaundice LO 1 Unconjugated • Breast milk jaundice • Infection, e.g. urinary tract • Haemolytic anaemia, e.g. G6PD deficiency • Hypothyroidism • High gastrointestinal obstruction e.g. hypertrophic pyloric stenosis • Crigler–Najjar syndrome Conjugated (>20 μmol/L) Bile duct obstruction • Biliary atresia • Choledochal cyst Neonatal hepatitis syndrome • Congenital infection • Inborn errors of metabolism • α1-Antitrypsin deficiency • Galactosaemia • Tyrosinaemia (type 1) • Errors of bile acid synthesis • Progressive familial intrahepatic cholestasis (PFIC) • Cystic fibrosis • Total parenteral nutrition Intrahepatic biliary hypoplasia • Alagille syndrome. Definition of Neonatal Cholestatic Jaundice •Conjugated hyperbilirubinemia developing within the first 90 days of extrauterine life. •Conjugated bilirubin exceeds 20 mmol/L., when TSB is less than 100 mmol/L •Conjugated bilirubin generally exceeds 15% of the total bilirubin. LO 1 LO 1 Clues in the history and physical examination that can direct the approach of these infants: 1. Cholestatic jaundice in the first 24 hours of life is usually caused by infection. 2. SGA babies suggest IU infection, metabolic liver disease, and Alagille’s syndrome. 3. An impalpable spleen, BA with polyspleenia. LO 2 Clues in the history and physical examination that can direct the approach of these infants: 4. Dysmorphic features: Triosomies, Alagille, Zellweger and certain congenital infections. 5. Cardiac murmurs :Triosomies, Congenital infections and Alagille’s syndrome. 6. Hypoglycemia : metabolic liver disease, hypopituitarism and severe liver disease. 7. Neurologic abnormalities: Triosomies, Zellweger, congenital infection and metabolic disease. LO 2 LO 2 Diagnostic plan Step1: Prompt differentiation of conjugated from unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia is imperative. (Significant elevation of total bilirubin, with > 15% conjugated.) LO 2 LO 2 Diagnostic plan Step 2: Prompt recognition of any specific or treatable primary cause of cholestasis like sepsis, endocrine and metabolic causes. TORCHS screen α1antitrypsin Galactose in urine , enzyme assay? Cortisol, thyroid function Serum ferritin , GGT, karyotype Plasma a.a., cholesterol, triglyceride LO 2 LO 2 Diagnostic plan Step 3: Ultrasonography. � Abnomal bile duct like choledocal cyst and gall bladder shape and size. � Gall stones can be visualized � “ Triangular cord sign” correlates with biliary atresia. IF US is abnormal proceed to Peroperative cholangiography and Surgery. LO 2 Diagnostic plan Step 4: IF US is normal then do Liver Biopsy Biliary atresia peroperative Chlangiography and Surgery Bile duct paucity Gene study Neonatal hepatitis syndrome LO 2 LO 2 Other diagnostic options •Hepatobiliary scan(TIBIDA). •MRCP. •ERCP. LO 2 LO 2 General Management � Protein calorie Malnutrition Dietary formula and MCT formula. �Fat soluble vitamins malabsorption Vitamins AKED replacement. �Micronutrient deficiency Calcium and zinc supplement. �Retention of biliary constituents Choleretics a

VIRAL HEPATITIS Dr. Jamal.M.Hussein HEPATOTROPIC VIRUSES PATHOLOGY; ⚫The viruses produce there pathogenic effect through two mechanisms; Direct cytopathic effects Immune mediated cell damage Acute inflammation of the liver HEPATITIS A: ⦿RNA virus a member of the picornaviruses. ⦿most prevalent virus causing viral hepatitis ⦿Usually causes self limited benign hepatitis ⦿fulminant hepatic failure is rare and occurs more in adults EPIDEMIOLOGY; ⦿more common in developing countries ⦿ highly contagious disease. ⦿Transmission is almost always by feco-oral route . ⦿parenteral transmission occurs rarely. ⦿It doesn’t cause problem if it affects pregnant women. ⦿Patient is contagious 2 weeks before and 1 week after the onset of jaundice ⦿The duration of viral excretion is more prolonged in infants CLINICAL PRESENTATION ⚫Incubation Period is about 3 wks ⚫Usually symptomatic in older children and adolescents. ⚫acute febrile illness with anorexia, nausea, malaise, vomiting and jaundice, ⚫duration of the illness is 7-14 days. ⚫lymphadenopathy and splenomegly may occur but is uncommon. ⚫usually asymptomatic in infants and young children presenting as anicteric illness with symptoms undistinguishable from other forms of viral gastroenteritis DIAGNOSIS; ⚫IgM by radioimmunoassay, this becomes detectable with the onset of symptoms till 6 months. ⚫IgG antibodies are present after 8 weeks from infection and confers long term immunity. COMPLICATIONS; ⦿1- Fulminant Hepatitis; this is very rare but in endemic area HAV constitute 40% of pediatric acute liver failure. ⦿2- Cholestatic syndrome; symptoms of hepatitis wax and wanes over prolonged period of time, eventualy resolving with out sequelae. TREATMENT; ⦿Symptomatic treatment ⦿intravenous fluid in cases of excessive vomiting and dehydration. ⦿Fat soluble vitamins may be needed in prolonged cholestatic syndrome. PREVENTION; ⦿1-Vaccination; there are two inactivated highly immunogenic and safe vaccines are present. (Havrix and Vaqta). ⦿Given 2 doses at 6-12 months apart for risky groups. ⦿2-Immunoglobuline; intramuscular Ig is present for pre and post exposure prophylaxes HEPATITIS B ⦿A member of Hepadnaviridae family, a double strand DNA virus. ⦿HBsAg, HBcAg,HBeAg EPIDEMIOLOGY; ⚫world wide spread. ⚫present in high concentrations in blood, serum, serous exudate ⚫In moderate concentration in saliva, vaginal secretion and semen ⚫Transmission by; blood and blood product and Sexual contact ⚫most important risk factor is perinatal exposure to HBV Ag positive mother with greater risk in HBVe Ag positive ⚫Up to 90% of the infants become chronically infected if untreated ⚫Immunoprophylaxis protects more than 95% of neonates ⚫serological markers become positive within 1-3 months, indicating that transmission occurrs during delivery ⚫breast feeding does not confer a greater risk of hepatitis than formula feeding. ⦿Chronic HBV infection; is defined as being positive for HBV s Ag for more than 6 months ⦿Chronic HBV is inversely related to the age of acquisition of the virus PATHOLOGY; HBV Infect the hepatocyts Expression of viral Ag HBe, HBc Ag Combination with class I histocompatibity complex Hepatocyts become target for T-cell lysis • HBV is a non cytopathogenic virus that causes injury by immune-mediated processes • most complete immune response being associated with the greatest likely hood of viral clearance and most sever liver injury. CLINICAL FEATURES; ⚫Many cases in children asymptomatic ⚫symptomatic episode is similar to that of HAV may be more sever and more likely to include involvement of skin and joint. ⚫acute liver failure is more ⚫most patients recover but chronic carrier state is about 10% of adults ⚫hepatomegly and lymphadenopathy and splenomegly are common. ⚫Altered sensorium may mark the onset of encephalopathy DIAGNOSIS; HBV detection by PCR COMPLICATIONS; TREATMENT; PREVENTION; ⦿HBV vaccine. ⦿There are 2 DNA vaccines available and both are highly immunogenic ⦿The safety profile is excellen

Meningitis and Encephalitis Dr.Jamal.M.hussein FIBMS pediatrics Objectives:  Identifying the definition , causes and pathophysiology of meningitis and encephalitis.  Identifying clinical presentation in different age groups.  Identifying diagnotic tests and proper treatment .  Out come and follow up. Meningitis occurs when there is inflammation of the meninges covering the brain. Viral infections are the most common cause of meningitis, and although infants may be very unwell at presentation, most are self-resolving. Bacterial meningitis may have severe consequences. Tuberculous meningitis is rare in countries with low tuberculosis (TB) prevalence. It mainly affects children under 5 years of age. Fungal and parasitic meningitis are rare in children and predominantly affect immunocompromised individuals. Causes of non-infectious meningitis include malignancy and autoimmune diseases. Bacterial meningitis Bacterial meningitis remains a serious infection in children, although the number of cases in the UK has declined markedly. It has an overall 5% to 10% mortality, with over 10% of survivors left with long-term neurological impairment. Pathophysiology Bacterial infection of the meninges usually follows bacteremia.  Much of the damage caused by meningeal infection results from the host response to infection and not from the organism itself. The release of inflammatory mediators and activated leucocytes, together with endothelial damage, leads to cerebral oedema, raised intracranial pressure, and decreased cerebral blood flow. The inflammatory response below the meninges causes a vasculopathy resulting in cerebral cortical infarction, and fibrin deposits may block the resorption of CSF by the arachnoid villi, resulting in hydrocephalus. organism  <3 months old Group B streptococcus Escherichia coli and other coliforms Listeria monocytogenes  >3 months Neisseria meningitides Streptococcus pneumoniae Presentation: The early signs and symptoms of meningitis are non-specific, especially in infants and young children. older children describe the classical meningitis symptoms of headache, neck stiffness, and photophobia. However, neck stiffness may also be seen in some children with tonsillitis and cervical lymphadenopathy.  As children with meningitis may also have sepsis, signs of shock, such as tachycardia, tachypnoea, prolonged capillary refill time and hypotension. Purpura in a febrile child of any age should be assumed to be due to meningococcal sepsis, even if the child does not appear unduly ill at the time; meningitis may or may not be present in this situation. Initial cerebrospinal fluid findings in different types of meningitis includes: Fungal Tuberculosis Viral Partially Treated Bacterial 25-500 50-500 10-100 200-500 > 500 WBC Count Lymphotyes Lymphocytes Early neutrophils, Late Lymphocytes Lymphocytes Mostly neutrophils WBC Type < 40 < 40 > 40 variable < 40 Glucose (mg/dL) 25-500 50-500 < 100 variable 100 Protein (mg/dL) May be positive with special stain Rarely acid fast bacillus positive Negative Negative Positive Gram Stain Negative Negative Negative negative Positive Culture Management It is imperative that there is no delay in the administration of antibiotics and supportive therapy in a child with meningitis. The choice of antibiotics will depend on the likely pathogen. A third-generation cephalosporin, e.g. ceftriaxone, is the preferred choice to cover the most common bacterial causes, with the addition of amoxicillin in infants <3 months old to cover Listeria. Although still relatively rare in the UK, pneumococcal resistance to penicillin and cephalosporins is increasing rapidly in certain parts of the world. . The length of the course of antibiotics given depends on the causative organism and clinical response. Beyond the neonatal period, dexamethasone should be administered when antibiotics are started as it reduces the risk of long-term complications such as deafness Complications: *E

Specific bacterial infections Dr.Jamal.M.hussein FIBMS pediatrics Learning objectives:  Understanding the specific pathogens .  Mechanism how they produce diseases.  Clinical presentation of differen pathogen .  Diagnostic tests and ways of prevention and treatment.  Complications and outcome. Meningococcal infection Meningococcal infection is a disease that strikes fear into both parents and doctors, as it can kill previously healthy children within hours. The septicemia is usually accompanied by a purpuric rash, which may start anywhere on the body and then spreads. The rash may or may not be present in cases with meningococcal meningitis. Characteristic lesions are non-blanching on palpation (although they may start as maculo-papular lesions), are irregular in size and outline, and may have a necrotic center. They are typically larger than 5mm, although may start as petechiae and increase in size. The septicemia may progress within hours and the child may require intensive care . Of the main causes of bacterial meningitis, meningococcal infection has the lowest risk of long-term neurological sequelae, with most survivors recovering fully, though limb loss sometimes occurs. summery  Any febrile child with a purpuric rash or who is very unwell should be given intramuscular benzylpenicillin or intravenous third-generation cephalosporin before urgent transfer to hospital. Meningococcal septicaemia can kill children in hours. Optimal outcome requires immediate recognition, prompt resuscitation and antibiotics Pneumococcal infections: S. pneumoniae is often carried in the nasopharynx of healthy children. Asymptomatic carriage is particularly prevalent among young children and may be responsible for the transmission of pneumococcal disease to other individuals by respiratory droplets. The organism may cause pharyngitis, otitis media, conjunctivitis, sinusitis, as well as ‘invasive’ disease (pneumonia, bacterial sepsis, and meningitis). Invasive disease, which carries a high burden of morbidity and mortality, mainly occurs in young infants as their immune system responds poorly to encapsulated pathogens such as pneumococci. With the inclusion into the routine immunization schedule, the incidence of invasive disease has declined. Children at increased risk, e.g. due to hyposplenism or asplenism, should also be given daily prophylactic penicillin to prevent infection by strains not covered by the vaccine. summary Pneumococcal infection Causes not only minor infections such as otitis media but also severe invasive disease. Susceptibility is increased in hyposplenism (e.g. sickle cell disease) and nephrotic syndrome. A 13-valent pneumococcal vaccine is included in the UK standard immunization schedule. H. influenzae infection Haemophilus influenzae type b (Hib) was an important cause of systemic illness in children. including otitis media, pneumonia, epiglottitis, cellulitis, osteomyelitis, and septic arthritis. It was the second most common cause of meningitis in the UK. Immunization has been highly effective and Hib now rarely causes systemic disease. Staphylococcal and group A streptococcal infections Staphylococcal and streptococcal infections are usually caused by direct invasion of the organisms. They may also cause disease by releasing toxins, which act as superantigens. Where as conventional antigens stimulate only a small subset of T cells, which have a specific antigen receptor, superantigens bind to a part of the T-cell receptor which is shared by many T cells and therefore stimulates massive T-cell proliferation and cytokine release. Other diseases following streptococcal infections, such as post- streptococcal glomerulonephritis and rheumatic fever, are immune-mediated. Scarlet fever This occurs in association with an exotoxin from group A streptococcal pharyngitis.  It is a diffuse, erythematous macular-papular rash with a sandpaper texture, which appears shortly after the pharyngitis.  It

10/28/2024 Ass.prof .Dr.Khalid Hama Salih 1 Ass.prof.Dr.Khalid Hama Salih MBChB;D.C.H FIBMSPed Ass,Professor of pediatrics College of Medicine, UoS Endocrine • Understanding the diabetes type 1 in children • Knowing the different types of diabetes • Understanding the clinical presentation and how to diagnose type 1 diabetes o The main treatment of diabetes ,the types of insulin their onset and duration of action.Insulin regimens and its application • How to educate the patient with type1 DM regarding dietary and exercise recommendations o complication of diabetes and follow up, 10/28/2024 Ass.prof .Dr.Khalid Hama Salih 2 Objectives CONTENT •Definition •Types of diabetes and their differences •Action of Insulin •Genetic and Environmental factors contributing to diabetes •Clinical presentation •Diagnosis •Treatment •Patient education 10/28/2024 Ass.prof .Dr.Khalid Hama Salih 3 What is DM type 1 10/28/2024 Ass.prof .Dr.Khalid Hama Salih 4 a prevalence of 2.5 per 1000 . Its incidence has increased over the last 30 years, most likely from changes in environmental risk factors, but this is poorly understood. Almost all (95%) children with DM have type 1 diabetes requiring insulin from the outset, although type 2 diabetes due to insulin resistance is increasing in childhood, as obesity becomes more common. There is an association with other autoimmune disorders hypothyroidism Addison disease coeliac disease rheumatoid arthritis Diabetes is also more common in children with Down and Turner syndrome 10/28/2024 Ass.prof .Dr.Khalid Hama Salih 5 Aetiology : a genetic predisposition to developing type 1 diabetes. Immune activation may trigger an autoimmune response process in susceptible individuals which damages the pancreatic β-cells and leads to increasing insulin deficiency . Recognized auto-antibodies include glutamic acid decarboxylase (GAD), zinc transporter 8 (ZnT8) and islet antigen 2 (IA-2). Identification of more than one of these auto-antibodies has been shown to be associated with the potential to develop clinical diabetes. Inherited susceptibility is demonstrated by twins have a higher risk of developing DM compared to other siblings Classification of diabetes according to aetiology: 1.Type 1 – most childhood diabetes • Destruction of pancreatic β-cells by an autoimmune process. 2.Type 2 – insulin resistance followed later by β-cell failure • Usually older children, obesity-related, positive family history, not as prone to ketosis, more common in some ethnic groups (e.g. Black and Asian children) 3. Monogenic diabetes: • A. Maturity onset diabetes of the young – various types caused by genetic defects in β-cell function. Strong family history. • B.Neonatal diabetes: transient or permanent secondary to defective β-cell function. 4.Drug-induced, e.g. corticosteroids 5.Pancreatic insufficiency, e.g. cystic fibrosis, iron overload in thalassaemia 6 Endocrine disorders, e.g. Cushing syndrome 7. Gestational diabetes 10/28/2024 Ass.prof .Dr.Khalid Hama Salih 6 Symptoms and signs of diabetes 10/28/2024 Ass.prof .Dr.Khalid Hama Salih 7 Early : A.Most common – the ‘classical triad’: • excessive drinking (polydipsia) • excessive urination (polyuria) • weight loss B.Less common: • secondary enuresis • skin infections – e.g. candidiasis Late – diabetic ketoacidosis : • smell of acetone on breath • vomiting • dehydration • abdominal pain • rapid breathing due to acidosis (Kussmaul breathing) • hypovolaemic shock • drowsiness and reduced level of consciousness 10/28/2024 Ass.prof .Dr.Khalid Hama Salih 8 Diagnosis The diagnosis A.random blood glucose (>11.1mmol/L by the current WHO definition), glycosuria, and ketosis. or B.fasting blood glucose (>7mmol/L) or C. glycosylated haemoglobin (HbA1c) 6.5. Children and young people with type 2 diabetes may have similar symptoms to those with type 1. The estimated incidence of chromosomal abnormalities in live-born infants is about 1 in 150. cause multiple congenital anomalies and cognitive difficulties. Acquired chromosomal changes play a significant role in carcinogenesis and tumor progression 10/30/2024 Ass.prof .Dr.Khalid Hama Salih 5 This is the most common autosomal trisomy and the most common genetic cause of severe learning difficulties). The incidence (without antenatal screening) in live-born infants is about 1 in 650. The incidence increases with maternal age, Down syndrome (trisomy 21) 10/30/2024 Ass.prof .Dr.Khalid Hama Salih 6 Typical craniofacial appearance: • round face and flat nasal bridge, short neck • upslanted palpebral fissures • epicanthic folds (a fold of skin running across the inner edge of the palpebral fissure) • Brushfield spots in iris (pigmented spots) • small mouth and protruding tongue • small ears • flat occiput and third fontanelle Clinical features Other anomalies • single palmar creases, incurved and short fifth finger, and wide ‘sandal’ gap between first and second toes • hypotonia • congenital heart defects (in 40%) • duodenal atresia (or other intestinal atresias) • Hirschsprung disease (<1%) Later medical problems • delayed motor milestones • learning difficulties – severity is variable, usually mild to moderate but may be severe • short stature (special growth chart for children with Down Syndrome) • increased susceptibility to infections • hearing impairment from secretory otitis media (75%) • visual impairment from cataracts (15%), squints, myopia (50%) • increased risk of hypothyroidism (15%) • increased risk of leukaemia and solid tumours . 10/30/2024 Ass.prof .Dr.Khalid Hama Salih 7 Cytogenetics Meiotic nondisjunction (94%) In nondisjunction trisomy 21: result from an error at meiosis. • The chromosome 21 pair fails to separate, so that one gamete has two chromosome 21s and one has none. • Fertilization of the gamete with two chromosome 21s gives rise to a zygote with trisomy 21. • Parental chromosomes do not need to be examined. The incidence of trisomy 21 due to nondisjunction is related to maternal age Translocation (5%) When the extra chromosome 21 has fused with another chromosome (usually 14, but occasionally 15, 22, or 21), this is known as a Robertsonian translocation. This may be present in a phenotypically normal carrier with 45 chromosomes (two being ‘joined together’) or in someone with Down syndrome and a set of 46 chromosomes but with three copies of chromosome 21 material. parental chromosome analysis is recommended, • The risk of recurrence is 10%–15% if the mother carrier and about 2.5% if the father is the carrier. • If a parent carries the rare 21:21 translocation, all the offspring will have Down syndrome. • If neither parent carries a translocation (75% of cases), the risk of recurrence is less than 1%. Mosaicism (1%) In mosaicism, some of the cells are normal and some have trisomy 21. This usually arises after the formation of the chromosomally normal zygote by
