Theo.text: Child Health Care paediatric surgical care Mahdi Aziz Kaki MBChB; D.G.S., FIBMS-Ped Surg Assistant Professor of Pediatric Surgery College of Medicine, UoS * Mahdi Aziz Kaki 1 Principles of pediatric surgery Learning outcomes 1.specifications of pediatric patients 2.Differences between adult and pediatric surgical patients 3.Spectrum of surgical conditions in pediatric surgery 4.Common problems of neonates * Mahdi Aziz Kaki 2 Paediatric Surgery it Combines: • technical challenge of performing complex operations in neonate, appeal of solving a wide range of problems in many anatomic locations, And immense satisfaction of caring for children and offering them a long normal life. Pediatric Patients : include Intrauterine fetal 1-28 day neonate 4 weeks – 1year infant 1 – 12 years children * Mahdi Aziz Kaki 3 INTRODUCTION Children are not small adults. The followings are different 1.Anatomy 2.Physiology(vital signs….) 3.Pathologies: They suffer from different disorders: ( A. congenital - B. acquired (trauma, tumour, inflammation, infection) 4.Physical and psychological responses to trauma . 5.Their capacity for adaptation is greater but they must endure any consequences of disease and its management for longer. 6.In contrast to adults they rarely have comorbidity from degenerative diseases or lifestyle problems but they can suffer the unique consequences of congenital malformations. 7. Children must be treated within the context of their families. * Mahdi Aziz Kaki 4 ANATOMY Anatomical differences between adults and children are important in surgery. •Infants and small children have a wider abdomen, a broader costal margin and a shallower pelvis. Thus, the edge of the liver is more easily palpable below the costal margin and the bladder is an intra-abdominal organ. The ribs are more horizontal and respiratory function is more dependent on diaphragmatic movement. •The umbilicus is relatively low lying. •In the small child, transverse supra-umbilical incisions are preferred to vertical midline ones for laparotomy. • Abdominal scars grow with the child and may migrate •Chest. Children have relatively elastic ribs that rarely fracture.Lung contusion is common. A major thoracic injury may exist despite a normal chest radiograph. * Mahdi Aziz Kaki 5 Physiology •Fetal Circulation * Mahdi Aziz Kaki 6 Respiratory Adaptation ▪↑ PaO2 in the alveoli and arterial circulation (causing vasodilatation) ▪↓ Pulmonary vascular resistance ▪↓ Right-to-left shunting (through ductus arteriosus2 ) ▪↑ Pulmonary venous return to the left atrium ▪↑ Left atrial pressure and cessation of right-to-left shunting (through foramen ovale) ▪Establishment of resting functional residual capacity (FRC) ▪Functional closure of the ductus arteriosus (via ↓ prostaglandin E2) to achieve independence of the two circulations (systemic and pulmonary) * Mahdi Aziz Kaki 7 SPECIFIC PHYSIOLOGIC PROBLEMS OF THE NEWBORN 1.Glucose Metabolism ►Newborn especially SGA & Preterms are at high risk to develop hypoglycemia ►After delivery, the newborn depletes his or her hepatic glycogen stores within 2 to 3 hours. ►The newborn is severely limited in his or her ability to use fat and protein as substrates to synthesize glucose. * Mahdi Aziz Kaki 8 Neonatal hypoglycemia ► Neonatal hypoglycemia is generally defined as a glucose level lower than 40 mg/dL. ► Clinical signs of hypoglycemia are nonspecific and may include a weak or high-pitched cry, cyanosis, apnea, jitteriness, apathy, seizures, abnormal eye movements, temperature instability, hypotonia, and weak suck. Some infants, however, exhibit no signs, despite extremely low blood glucose levels. ► Infants who are at high risk for developing hypoglycemia require frequent glucose monitoring. ► Because most newborns who require surgical procedures are at risk to develop hypoglycemia, a 10% glucose infusion is usually started on admission to the hospital and blood glucose levels are measured at the bedside and confirmed by periodic laboratory

Abdominal trauma in children Mahdi Aziz Kaki MBChB; D.G.S., FIBMS-Ped Surg Assistant Professor of Pediatric Surgery College of Medicine, UoS 11/6/2024 Mahdi Aziz Kaki 1 Concepts in management 11/6/2024 Mahdi Aziz Kaki 2 •Trauma is the leading cause of morbidity and mortality in the pediatric population. •may have a mortality as high as 8.5% •The abdomen is the most common site of initially unrecognized fatal injury in traumatized children. •Abdominal trauma accounts for 8-10% of all trauma admissions to pediatric hospitals. 11/6/2024 Mahdi Aziz Kaki 3 Anatomy •A young child’s abdomen is square •The abdominal wall of a child has thinner musculature •The ribs are more flexible in the child •The solid organs are comparatively larger in the child than in the adult •The bladder extends to the level of the umbilicus at birth… •The rapid growth of the spine during adolescence influences its anatomy and biomechanical properties 11/6/2024 Mahdi Aziz Kaki 4 Etiology Blunt • motor vehicle accidents • Falls • direct blows to the abdominal wall (e.g, handlebar injury) Penetrating • firearm injurie • stab wounds • Impalements • dog bites • machinery-related accidents • gunshot wounds 11/6/2024 Mahdi Aziz Kaki 5 History •A pertinent history should be obtained, as for abdominal trauma in adults. • However, obtaining the history is frequently difficult in young children, who may be stressed from the pain and the unfamiliar hospital environment and staff. • The possibility of inflicted abdominal injury should be considered in a child with unexplained or unusual clinical history or physical findings. 11/6/2024 Mahdi Aziz Kaki 6 Physical Examination •A focused primary survey(ABCDE) •This primary survey is followed by a secondary survey, which consists of a head-to-toe physical examination to identify all traumatic injuries • A tertiary survey, usually performed 24 hours after admission, is also recommended and includes a follow-up head-to-toe examination to minimize the risk of missed injuries. 11/6/2024 Mahdi Aziz Kaki 7 Patterns of Abdominal Organ Injury by Mechanism of Injury Frequency of Organ Injury Blunt Penetrating Liver 15% 22% Spleen 27% 9% Pancreas 2% 6% Kidney 27% 9% Stomach 1% 10% Duodenum 3% 4% Small bowel 6% 18% Colon 2% 16% Other 17% 6% 11/6/2024 Mahdi Aziz Kaki 8 Work up •blood specimen for typing and cross-matching should be obtained. • laboratory assessment (e.g, liver function tests [LFTs], amylase level, lipase level, complete blood count [CBC], and urinalysis) •(FAST) Focused assessment with sonography in trauma •diagnostic imaging (e.g., CT) with contrast! • Diagnostic peritoneal lavage (DPL) is not used as frequently as it once was. 11/6/2024 Mahdi Aziz Kaki 9 (FAST) Focused assessment with sonography in trauma •It allows rapid assessment of the peritoneal cavity and can detect free fluid. • it is operator-dependent and lacks specificity. •significant number of false-negative results (e.g, solid-organ injury First peak in the neonatal period and the second peak around puberty (rare in prepubertal boys). •classification •Intravaginal. – Torsion within tunica due to “bell-clapper” deformity, seen in adolescents. •Extravaginal: – Torsion at the level of the spermatic cord due to failure of the fxation of the layers of the scrotum, seen in neonates. •Diagnosis of torsion is clinical. 11/7/2024 Mahdi Aziz Kaki 6 •Investigations •Urine analysis •Color Doppler ultrasound scan •Sonographic shear wave elastography. •Treatment: •Exploration •Contralateral fixation 11/7/2024 Mahdi Aziz Kaki 7 Torsion of Testicular Appendages 11/7/2024 Mahdi Aziz Kaki 8 • Torsion can happen in prepubertal boys in any of these appendices but is most seen in the hydatid of Morgagni. • The onset of pain is insidious. • Examination shows a focal polar testicular tenderness and perhaps a “blue or black dot sign. • ” Torsion of testicular appendages is self-limiting and therefore if the diagnosis is secure, can be treated with nonsteroidal anti-infammatories. • Exploration is indicated if the symptoms are prolonged or the diagnosis is doubtful. Hemostatic Disorders 10/24/2025 Rozhan Nabaz M Sedik 1 Child Health Dr Rozhan Nabaz Assistant Professor of Pediatrics rozhan.sediq@univsul.edu.iq Learning objectives: You should be able to: 1. Assess a child presenting with bleeding based on history and examination 2. Diagnosis and treatment the common types of hemostatic disorders ( ITP, Von Willebrand disease, Hemophilia, Vitamin K deficiency) 10/24/2025 Rozhan Nabaz M Sedik 2 • Hemostasis is the active process that clots blood in areas of blood vessel injury yet simultaneously limits the clot size only to the areas of injury. Over time, the clot is lysed by the fibrinolytic system, and normal blood flow is restored. • The main components of the hemostatic process are the vessel wall, platelets, coagulation proteins, anticoagulant proteins, and fibrinolytic system 10/24/2025 Rozhan Nabaz M Sedik 3 10/24/2025 Rozhan Nabaz M Sedik 4 History • Sites • Severity and duration • Age at onset • Spontaneous, or after trauma? • Symptoms correlate with the degree of injury or trauma? • Previous personal or family history • Previous surgery , was there any increased bleeding? • Delayed or slow healing of superficial injuries 10/24/2025 Rozhan Nabaz M Sedik 5 • In postpubertal females: menstrual history, mothers and family members may have the same mild bleeding disorder • Some medications, such as aspirin and other nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory (NSAID) drugs. 10/24/2025 Rozhan Nabaz M Sedik 6 PHYSICAL EXAMINATION • Focus on whether bleeding symptoms are associated primarily with the mucous membranes or skin (mucocutaneous bleeding) or with the muscles and joints (deep bleeding). -Defects in platelet-blood vessel wall interaction usually have mucocutaneous bleeding, which may include epistaxis, menorrhagia, petechiae, ecchymoses, occasional hematomas, and, less commonly, hematuria and gastrointestinal bleeding. 10/24/2025 Rozhan Nabaz M Sedik 7 -Individuals with a clotting factor deficiency of factor VIII or IX (hemophilia A or B) have symptoms of deep bleeding into muscles and joints, with much more extensive ecchymoses and hematoma formation. • Individuals with disorders of the collagen matrix and vessel wall may have loose joints and lax skin associated with easy bruising (Ehlers- Danlos syndrome). ➢Evidence on examination like hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy or pallor. 10/24/2025 Rozhan Nabaz M Sedik 8 ✓Platelet and small vessel disorders are usually associated with: ✓While coagulation factor defects are characterized by: • Petechiae and echymosis. • Small hematomas. • Mucus membrane bleeding. • Short lived bleeding after trauma. • Large, deep hematomas. • Hemarthrosis, spontaneous or after minor trauma. • Persistent or recurrent oozing after trauma. 10/24/2025 Rozhan Nabaz M Sedik 9 LABORATORY TESTS • Screening laboratory studies for bleeding patients include a CBC and blood film, prothrombin time (PT), partial thromboplastin time (PTT), thrombin time (TT), fibrinogen, and screening test of platelet function. • Many laboratories have adopted the platelet function analyzer (PFA) to replace the bleeding time as a screening test for platelet function abnormalities and VWD. ❖The PTT is the screening test that checks for deficiency of all clotting factors except factors 7 and 13. The PT is especially sensitive to deficiencies of factor 7. *PTT ; Test Intrinsic and common pathway **PT ; Test Extrinsic and common pathway ***TT; Prolonged in fibrinogen deficiency. 10/24/2025 Rozhan Nabaz M Sedik 10 Platelet Disorders 1. Thrombocytopenia: (normal platelet ranges from 150-450 × 109 /L). Thrombocytopenia refers to a reduction in platelet count to <150 × 109 /L ❑Decreased production: ➢Congenital ; -Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome(This X-linked recessive disorder is characterized by immunodeficiency, severely dysfunctional platelets, and thrombocytopenia with microthrombocytes ). -TAR syndrome. -Fanconi anemia -Osteopetrosis ➢Acquired; BM infilteration (Leukemia), Aplastic anemia 10/24/2025 Rozhan

Pediatric Anemia Reference Illustrated Textbook of PAEDIATRICS Child Health Dr Rozhan Nabaz Assistant Professor of Pediatrics rozhan.sediq@univsul.edu.iq Learning Objectives You Should be able to; 1. Define and Categorize anemia based on Physiology and morphology 2. Evaluate a child presenting with anemia. 3. Management of IDA 4. Recognize Megaloblastic anemia Definition of Anemia Anemia is generally defined as a reduction in red cell mass or blood Hb concentration. Typically defined as; Hemoglobin concentration that is 2 standard deviations below the mean for age and gender. LO 1 The World Health Organization (WHO) uses the following hemoglobin thresholds to define anemia; Children 6 months to <5 years – 11 g/dL Children 5 to <12 years – 11.5 g/dL Children 12 to <15 years – 12 g/dL Etiology l. Blood loss: acute or chronic haemorrhage ll. Anemia caused by decreased red cell production 1. Marrow failure:  Diamond-Blackfan anemia (congenital pure red cell aplasia)  Transient erythroblastopenia of childhood  Aplastic anemia  Marrow replacement (eg, leukemia) 2. Impaired erythropoietin:  Chronic renal failure  Chronic inflammatory diseases  Hypothyroidism 3. Specific factor deficiency  Nutritional anemia secondary to iron, folate, or vitamin B-12 deficiency III. Anemia due to increased destruction hemolysis (i.e., adequate reticulocyte count) A. Intrinsic RBC defect . Hemoglobinopathies (Sickle cell anemia, Thalassemia syndromes) . Membrane defects (Hereditary Spherocytosis, Elliptocytosis) . Enzymopathies (G6PD, pyruvate kinase deficiency) B. Extrinsic defects 1. Immune hemolysis (Autoimmune HA, Isoimmune HA) 2. Non immune; . Infection (malaria, clostridium perfringens) . Microangiopathy {disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC), hemolytic uremic syndrome (HUS), thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) } . Thermal injury to erythrocyte. . Toxins C. Combined mechanism  Paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria (PNH) Diagnosis of Anemia Most anemia can be diagnosed with history , physical examination and minimal laboratory testing. HISTORICAL FACTORS OF IMPORTANCE IN EVALUATING PATIENTS WITH ANEMIA AGE Iron deficiency rare in the absence of blood loss before 6 mo in term infants or before doubling of birth weight in preterm infants Sickle cell anemia and β-thalassemia appear as fetal hemoglobin disappears (4-8 mo of age) benign physiologic anemia of infancy (at an age of 6 -10 wk) requires no therapy. GENDER Consider X-linked disorders in males (G6PD deficiency, Pyruvate kinase deficiency). Normal Hb: A, A2& F •HbF fetal blood & first few months of infancy. •HbA2 is present in extremely small quantities through out life. •HbA is the predominant form RACE β-thalassemia more common in Caucasians; α- thalassemia trait most common among African Americans and Asians. ETHNICITY Thalassemia syndromes most common among patients of Mediterranean origin. G6PD deficiency observed more often among Sephardic Jews, Filipinos, Greeks, Sardinians, and Kurds FAMILY HISTORY AND GENETIC CONSIDERATION  Autosomal dominant: spherocytosis.  Family member with history of anemia, jaundice, cholecystectomy (for bilirubin stones) or splenectomy at an early age NEONATAL  A history of hyperbilirubinemia in the newborn period suggests the presence of congenital hemolytic anemia, such as the hereditary spherocytosis or G6PD deficiency. Prematurity predisposes to the early development of iron deficiency. DIET  Cow's milk diet: iron deficiency  Strict vegetarian: vitamin B12 deficiency  Goat's milk diet: folate deficiency  Pica: plumbism, iron deficiency DRUGS  Oxidants induced G6PD (e.g., nitrofurantoin, antimalarials), phenytoin-induced megaloblastic anemia, drug-induced aplastic anemia DIARRHEA  Malabsorption of vitamin B12 or E or iron  Inflammatory bowel disease and anemia of inflammation (chronic disease) with or without blood loss  Milk protein intolerance–induced blood loss  Intestinal resection: vitamin B12 deficiency INFECTION 

Pediatric Anemia (2) Child Health Dr Rozhan Nabaz Assistant Professor of Pediatrics rozhan.sediq@univsul.edu.iq Learning Objectives You should be able to: 1. Relate clinical and laboratory findings in hemolytic anemia 2. Recognize and Manage common types of hemolytic anemia (Hereditary spherocytosis, G6PD deficiency, Thalassemia, Autoimmune hemolytic anemia) Hemolytic Anemia • Hemolysis is defined as the premature destruction of red blood cells (RBCs) (a shortened RBC life span). Classification Cellular Defect Extracellular Defect / Hereditary Spherocytosis (HS) • It usually has an autosomal dominant inheritance. • The basic defect is an abnormality of a membrane protein (spectrin, band 3, ankyrin, or protein 4.2) that allows the RBC membrane to lose its redundancy and the usual biconcave disk shape resulting in a small, dense cell of spheroid configuration. CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS • In the neonatal period → hemolytic disease manifest as anemia and hyperbilirubinemia sufficiently severe to require phototherapy or exchange transfusions. • Majority have mild to moderate hemolysis. • Some patients remain asymptomatic into adulthood • 5 percent of cases have severe anemia • After infancy, splenomegaly is common • Bilirubin gallstone formation is a function of age; they can form as early as age 4-5 yr and are present in the majority of adult patients. • Aplastic crises( parvovirus B19 infection) • Hypoplastic crises associated with various other infections • Megaloblastic crisis Diagnosis • reticulocytosis and indirect hyperbilirubinemia • hemoglobin level usually is 6-10 g/dL, but it can be in the normal range • The MCV usually falls within low normal range. the MCHC often is increased > 35 g|dl. • Blood smear shows • The classic incubated osmotic fragility test can detect the presence of spherocytes in the blood; however, it is not specific to HS. • If the diagnosis is less certain, the recommended tests that have a high predictive value for HS are the flow cytometric EMA (eosin-5-maleimide) binding test. • Others includes; autohemolysis and the glycerol lysis test MCHC often is increased Spherocytes Coombs test –ve Treatment • Slight anemia (Hb > 1 0 gm/dl and reticulocytic count < 1 0%) ~ No treatment. • Folic acid supplementation is recommended in moderate and severe HS • Severe anemia need blood transfusions. • Splenectomy; is recommended for patients with severe HS. It should be considered for patients with moderate HS and frequent hypoplastic or aplastic crises, poor growth, or cardiomegaly. Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) deficiency • X- linked recessive disorder • Being sex linked it occurs mainly in males • May occur in females if homozygous or heterozygous with random inactivation of the normal X chromosome (Lyon hypothesis). Pathogenesis *G6PD enzyme is key enzyme of Hexose Mono Phosphate (HMP) shunt which produce reduced glutathione. *Reduced glutathione protects the red cells against oxidizing agents. In G6PD deficiency→ ↓ reduced glutathione → impaired elimination of oxidants → oxidation of Hb → methemoglobin → precipitate inside RBCs → acute hemolysis. Drugs, chemicals and food which can cause haemolysis in children with G6PD deficiency (British National Formulary for Children 2020). Clinical Presentation • Typically, hemolysis ensues in about 24-48 hr after a patient has ingested a substance with oxidant properties. • In severe cases, hemoglobinuria and jaundice result, and the hemoglobin concentration may fall precipitously. • The degree of hemolysis varies with the inciting agent, amount ingested, and severity of the enzyme deficiency. ✓onset is usually in the first 3 days of life. May have significant hyperbilirubinemia and require phototherapy or exchange transfusion. ✓Extremely rare. ✓Pallor, tinge of jaundice & moderate splenomegaly. Acute Hemolytic Anemia Neonatal jaundice Chronic non spherocytic hemolytic anemia Diagnosis • hemoglobin and hematocrit. • reticulocyte counts • indirect bilirubin and

Upper Respiratory Tract Infections By: Dr. Shwan Kanan Objectives • Learn about: • croup, its presentation and management. • The importance of epiglottitis. • Pertussis: Recognized by stages, Vaccination and early antibiotic treatment are key to preventing severe cases. Croup • laryngotracheobronchitis, is the most common infection of the middle respiratory tract. • The most common causes of croup are parainfluenza viruses and RSV. • small decrease in diameter secondary to mucosal edema and inflammation exponentially increases airway resistance and significantly increases the work of breathing. • walls of the subglottic space are drawn together, aggravating the obstruction and producing the stridor characteristic of croup. EPIDEMIOLOGY • Six months to 3 years old. • peaks in fall and early winter. • Episodes typically follow a common cold. • Symptomatic reinfection is common; • reinfections are usually mild. CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS • cough, hoarseness, inspiratory stridor, low- grade fever, and respiratory distress • develop slowly or quickly • barking cough • Signs of upper airway obstruction, such as labored breathing and marked suprasternal, intercostal, and subcostal retractions • Wheezing may be present if there is associated lower airway involvement. LABORATORY AND IMAGING STUDIES • Anteroposterior radiographs of the neck show the diagnostic subglottic narrowing known as the steeple sign. • Routine laboratory studies are not useful in establishing the diagnosis. • Leukocytosis is uncommon and suggests epiglottitis or bacterial tracheitis. Many rapid tests (PCR or antigen) are available for parainfluenza viruses and RSV . DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS • The diagnosis of croup usually is established by clinical manifestations. Stridor in infants younger than 4 months old or persistence of symptoms for more than 1 week indicates an increased probability of another lesion (subglottic stenosis or hemangioma) and the need for direct laryngoscopy • Epiglottitis • Bacterial tracheitis • Spasmodic croup TREATMENT • Administration of aerosolized racemic (D- and L-) or L-epinephrine reduces subglottic edema by α-adrenergic vasoconstriction, temporarily producing marked clinical improvement • A rebound effect may occur, with worsening of symptoms as the effect of the drug dissipates • Aerosol treatment may need to be repeated every 20 minutes (for no more than 1 to 2 hours) in severe cases. • Oral or IM dexamethasone for children with mild or moderate croup reduces the need for hospitalization and shortens hospital stays. • minimize forceful inspiration • Cool mist administered by tent or facemask may help prevent drying of the secretions around the larynx • Hospitalization is required for children with stridor at rest. • Subsidence of symptoms may indicate improvement . fatigue may give a clue for impending respiratory failure. COMPLICATIONS • The most common complication of croup is viral pneumonia, which occurs in 1% to 2% of children with croup. • Parainfluenza pneumonia and secondary bacterial pneumonia are more common among immuno-compromised persons. PROGNOSIS • The prognosis is excellent. • lasts approximately 5 days. Epiglottitis • typically occurs in children 1 to 5 years old. • It is a medical emergency because of the risk of sudden airway obstruction. • Hib is historically the principal causative agent, but immunization has reduced Hib infections markedly. • Stridor is common • is distinguished from croup by sudden onset and rapid progression, high fever, muffled rather than hoarse voice, dysphagia and drooling of secretions, refusal to eat or drink, refusal to sleep, and preference for sitting, often with the head held forward, the mouth open, and the jaw thrust forward (sniffing position). Imaging and diagnosis • Lateral radiograph reveals thickened and bulging epiglottis (thumb sign). • swelling of the aryepiglottic folds. • The diagnosis is confirmed by direct observation of the inflamed and swollen supraglottic structures

Bronchiolitis By: Dr. Shwan Kanan ETIOLOGY • is the term used for first-time wheezing with a viral respiratory infection. • respiratory tract inflammation with airway obstruction resulting from swelling of small bronchioles, which leads to inadequate expiratory airflow. • Most severe among infants. • Bronchiolitis is potentially life-threatening. • RSV is the primary cause, human metapneumovirus, parainfluenza viruses, influenza viruses, adenoviruses, rhinoviruses, and, infrequently, M. pneumoniae. • Viral bronchiolitis is extremely contagious and is spread by contact with infected respiratory secretions • hand carriage of contaminated secretions is the most frequent mode of transmission. EPIDEMIOLOGY • leading cause of hospitalization of infants. • Approximately 50% of children experience bronchiolitis during the first 2 years of life, with a peak age at 2 to 6 months. • only 10% of healthy children with bronchiolitis and wheezing require hospitalization • Boys are affected more commonly than girls in a ratio of 1.5 : 1. • late winter months CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS • incubation period of 4 to 6 days. • presents as a progressive respiratory illness that is similar to the common cold in its early phase with cough, coryza, and rhinorrhea. • It progresses over 3 to 7 days to noisy breathing and audible wheezing • low-grade fever ,irritability. • In contrast to the classic progression of disease, young infants infected with RSV may not have a prodrome and may have apnea as the first sign of infection. Physical signs • bronchiolar obstruction include prolongation of the expiratory phase of breathing, intercostal retractions with inward drawing of the lower ribs, suprasternal retractions, and air trapping with hyperexpansion of the lungs. During the wheezing phase of the illness, percussion of the chest usually reveals only hyperresonance, but auscultation usually reveals diffuse wheezes and crackles throughout the breathing cycle. With more severe disease, grunting and cyanosis may be present. LABORATORY AND IMAGING STUDIES • mild leukocytosis of 12,000 to 16,000/μL • Pulse oximetry is generally adequate for monitoring oxygen saturation • Rapid viral diagnosis also is performed by PCR and is helpful • hyperexpansion of the lungs, including increased lung radiolucency and flattened or depressed diaphragms. DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS • differentiate other diseases with wheezing • impossible to differentiate asthma from bronchiolitis by physical examination, but age of presentation, presence of fever, and no history (personal or family) of asthma are the major differential factors • gastroesophageal reflux is likely to be chronic or recurrent • Cystic fibrosis is associated with poor growth, chronic diarrhea, and a positive family history. • A focal area on radiography that does not inflate or deflate suggests a foreign body. TREATMENT • supportive therapy, including monitoring, control of fever, good hydration, upper airway suctioning, and oxygen administration. • bronchodilators possibly may improve wheezing and respiratory distress Indications for hospitalization • young age (<6 months old), • moderate to marked respiratory distress (sleeping respiratory rate of >50 to 60 breaths/min), • hypoxemia (oxygen saturation <92% on room air), • apnea, • inability to tolerate oral feeding, • lack of appropriate care available at home • chronic lung disease such as bronchopulmonary dysplasia, hemodynamically significant cyanotic and acyanotic congenital heart disease, • neuromuscular weakness • immunodeficiency are at increased risk of severe, potentially fatal disease. PROGNOSIS • Most cases of bronchiolitis resolve completely • The incidence of asthma seems to be higher for children hospitalized for bronchiolitis as infants, • There is a 1% to 2% mortality rate, which is highest among infants with preexisting cardiopulmonary or immunologic impairment. • palivizumab Pneumonia • an infection of the lower respiratory tract that involves the airwa

Asthma By: Dr. Shwan Kanan Objectives • Realize the: • The increasing incidence of asthma among pediatric age groups. • The impotence of environmental control. • Management plan. ETIOLOGY • Inflammation contributes to airway hyper- responsiveness, which is the tendency for the airways to constrict in response to allergens, irritants, viral infections, and exercise. It also results in edema, increased mucus production in the lungs, influx of inflammatory cells into the airway • Inflammatory cells (mast cells, eosinophils, T lymphocytes, neutrophils), chemical mediators (histamine, leukotrienes, platelet-activating factor, bradykinin), and chemotactic factors (cytokines, eotaxin) EPIDEMIOLOGY • Asthma is the most common chronic disease of childhood • Asthma affects 1 in 13 school-age children . CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS • Coughing • wheezing • shortness of breath or rapid breathing • chest tightness. • Nighttime symptoms are common What to ask? • Frequency • severity • factors that may worsen the child's symptoms • family history of allergy and asthma Exacerbating factors • viral infections • exposure to allergens and irritants (smoke, strong odors, fumes) • Exercise • Emotions • change in weather/humidity. • Rhinosinusitis, gastroesophageal reflux, and sensitivity to nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (especially aspirin) can aggravate asthma. physical examination • Tachypnea • Tachycardia • Cough • Wheezing • prolonged expiratory phase. • Physical findings may be subtle, and classic wheezing may not be prominent if there is minimal air movement. • As the attack progresses, cyanosis, diminished air movement (tight chest), retractions, agitation, inability to speak, tripod sitting position, diaphoresis, and pulsus paradoxus (decrease in blood pressure with inspiration of >15 mm Hg) may be observed. Physical examination may show evidence of other atopic diseases, such as eczema or allergic rhinitis. LABORATORY AND IMAGING STUDIES • Objective measurements of pulmonary function (spirometry) help to establish the diagnosis and treatment of asthma. • Spirometry can help monitor the patient's response to the treatment plan. • assess degree of reversibility with therapeutic intervention • measure the severity of an asthma exacerbation. Generally • children older than 5 years of age can perform spirometry maneuvers. Allergy skin testing • part of the evaluation of all children with persistent asthma • should not be done during an exacerbation of wheezing. • positive skin test results correlate strongly with bronchial allergen provocative challenges chest radiograph • performed with the first episode of asthma or with recurrent episodes of undiagnosed cough or wheeze or both to exclude anatomic abnormalities • Children with a history of asthma do not need repeat chest radiograph with each episode, unless there is fever that suggests pneumonia, or if there are localized findings on physical examination. DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS Wheezing and cough, especially in young infants, suggest other conditions that should be excluded before the diagnosis of asthma is made Mnemonic of Causes of Cough in the First Months of Life • C-Cystic fibrosis • R-Respiratory tract infections • A-Aspiration (swallowing dysfunction, gastroesophageal reflux, tracheoesophageal fistula, foreign body) • D-Dyskinetic cilia • L-Lung and airway malformations (laryngeal webs, laryngotracheomalacia, tracheal stenosis, vascular rings and slings) • E-Edema (heart failure, congenital heart disease) TREATMENT Optimal medical treatment of asthma includes the following key components: • environmental control • pharmacologic therapy • patient education including attainment of self- management skills . Because many children with asthma have coexisting allergies, steps to minimize allergen exposure should be taken. For all children with asthma, exposures to tobacco and wood smoke and exposure to viral infections should be minimized. Asthma medications can be divided into: • long-ter

Cystic fibrosis By: Dr. Shwan Kanan Objectives • Learn about: • Cystic fibrosis presentations and complications • It’s management including physiotherapy • Causes of bronchiectasis • Signs and symptoms of foreign body inhalation. • How to prevent it. cystic fibrosis is an autosomal recessive disease Physiotherapy in a patient with cystic fibrosis ETIOLOGY AND EPIDEMIOLOGY • autosomal recessive disorder • most common life-limiting recessive genetic disease in whites • In the U.S., 1 in 3200 white newborns • long arm of chromosome 7 • cystic fibrosis transmembrane regulator (CFTR). • deletion of phenylalanine at position ΔF508 • The secretory and absorptive characteristics of epithelial cells are affected • The CFTR is a chloride channel and substantially regulates epithelial chloride and possibly sodium transport. • It may reduce the function of airway defenses or promote bacterial adhesion to the airway epithelium. CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS • CF is a chronic, insidiously progressive disease exhibiting multiple complications related to viscous mucus, malabsorption, and infection • marked impermeability to chloride and an excessive reabsorption of sodium • impaired mucociliary transport and airway obstruction • Chronic bronchial infection • colonized with Haemophilus influenzae, Staphylococcus aureus, or Pseudomonas aeruginosa • Chronic bronchial infection leads to cough sputum production, hyperinflation, bronchiectasis, and eventually pulmonary insufficiency and death • Digital clubbing is common in patients with CF • Chronic sinusitis is nearly universal, and nasal polyposis is common. • exocrine pancreatic insufficiency • Maldigestion with secondary malabsorption results in steatorrhea • secondary deficiency states (vitamins A, D, E, and K) in untreated patients • failure to thrive despite a good appetite • intestinal obstruction resulting from inspissated meconium (meconium ileus). • relative insulin deficiency • Secondary amenorrhea is often present as a result of chronic illness and markedly reduced body weight • Males are almost universally azoospermic • atrophy or absence of the vas deferens • heat exhaustion or hypochloremic alkalosis in infants. • Pulmonary infections with certain virulent strains of Burkholderia cepacia are particularly difficult to treat and may be associated with an accelerated clinical deterioration. • Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis may complicate CF lung disease , treatment with steroids and antifungal agents. Complications of Cystic Fibrosis • Respiratory: Bronchiectasis, bronchitis, bronchiolitis, pneumonia, Atelectasis ,Hemoptysis ,Pneumothorax, Nasal polyps, Sinusitis, Reactive airway disease, Cor pulmonale, Respiratory failure. • Gastrointestinal : Meconium ileus, Distal intestinal obstruction syndrome (non-neonatal obstruction) Rectal prolapse, Intussusception, Volvulus, strictures, Appendicitis, Pancreatitis, portal hypertension: esophageal varices, hypersplenism, Neonatal obstructive jaundice, Inguinal hernia, malabsorption, Vitamin deficiency states, Insulin deficiency, symptomatic hyperglycemia,and diabetes. • Other: Infertility, Delayed puberty, Edema- hypoproteinemia, Dehydration-heat exhaustion ,Hypertrophic osteoarthropathy- arthritis, Clubbing and Amyloidosis DIAGNOSTIC STUDIES • considered seriously in any patient with chronic or recurring respiratory or gastrointestinal symptoms • presence of one or more typical phenotypic features of CF (chronic sinopulmonary disease, characteristic gastrointestinal and nutritional abnormalities, salt loss syndromes, and obstructive azoospermia), • a sibling with a history of CF or a positive result from a newborn screening test • a positive result obtained from a sweat test on two or more occasions (positive if >60 mEq/L with adequate sweat collection of at least 75 mg, borderline if 40 to 60 mEq/L, and negative if <40 mEq/L), • characteristic abnormality in ion transport across nasal epithelium shown in vivo. • Although highly specific f

Accidents By:Dr.Shwan Kanaan Accidents • Approximately 2 million children and young people attend an emergency department with an accidental injury each year in the UK. • Fortunately, the majority suffer only minor and temporary damage; however, a small proportion sustain significant injuries with life- changing consequences from disability, disfigurement and psychological harm, and for a few it is fatal Accident prevention • modification of product design, e.g. for road traffic accidents, vehicle child restraint design, and legislation change • alteration of the environment, e.g. reduction in speed limits or road layout design • education of children and their carers, e.g. school safety campaigns.

Poisoning By Dr. Shwan Kanaan • Approximately 6,000,000 children. • Children under 5 years of age account for 60 to 80% • low morbidity and mortality. • plants, household products, cosmetics, and over-the-counter medications . • multiple substances in a suicide gesture or an attempt to "get high." Emergency management • Identification of the poison • 1. Determine the product ingested, the amount taken, the time of ingestion, and the child's present condition. • 2. In determining therapy, assume the largest estimated amount ingested. • 3. Physical examination may reveal evidence for a particular ingestion. When the substance is unknown, consider common signs and symptoms. • 4. Qualitative analysis of blood or urine may confirm clinical suspicion. Gastric fluid analysis has no utility. Quantitative analysis of blood may be useful. Supportive therapy • 1. Respiratory support. • Maintain a patent airway • oxygen. • Endotracheal intubation may be required to prevent aspiration in patients with a depressed or absent gag reflex. • Assisted ventilation is often necessary after exposure to CNS depressants. • 2. Cardiac support • intravenous crystalloid fluid to correct hypotension or shock. • Vasopressors may be required with myocardial depressants (e.g., tricyclic antidepressants). • Cardiac arrhythmias are managed according to advanced cardiac life support algorithms. • 3. Fluid homeostasis . • Replace previous and ongoing fluid losses while correcting electrolyte derangements. • 4. Hematologic. Correct hemolytic anemias with packed cell transfusions or exchange transfusions. • 5. Central nervous system . Toxicity may include CNS depression or seizures. • a. An intravenous benzodiazepine (diazepam or lorazepam), phenytoin, and phenobarbital are the anticonvulsants of choice. Under select circumstances (e.g., isoniazid toxicity), a specific agent such as pyridoxine is indicated. • b. Provide supportive measures for prolonged coma. • 6. Renal insufficiency • can result from use of ethylene glycol and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory agents. • Renal function should be monitored, with hemodialysis instituted as needed. Gastrointestinal decontamination • 1. Gastric evacuation is the first intervention to consider. • efficacy falls dramatically when it is instituted more than 1 hour after ingestion. Chemical method • Ipecac syrup is the method of choice • induces emesis within 15 to 20 minutes • removes approximately 30 to 40% of a substance when administered within 1 hour of ingestion. • contraindicated with caustics, hydrocarbons, or agents that cause rapid coma (relative contraindication) or in those who are comatose or having seizures. Mechanical method • Orogastric lavage is as effective as ipecac • emergency department. • faster and allows prompt administration of adsorbent and cathartic. • For the patient with depressed airway reflexes, intubation should be performed before passage of a lavage tube. Adsorbents • Activated charcoal, • an odorless, tasteless black powder • the most effective means of gastrointestinal decontamination • can be administered without gastric evacuation. • should not be given before ipecac syrup. • It is not effective against metals, alcohols, hydrocarbons, or caustics. • The dose is 1 g/kg PO in 8 oz water or with cathartic . • Cathartics • hasten intestinal transit of gastrointestinal contents • sorbitol (2 ml/kg) and magnesium citrate (4-8 ml/ kg, maximum 300 ml). • Activated charcoal-cathartic mixtures provide a fixed dose of cathartic. • Sorbitol should be used with caution in children less than 2 years old since it can induce excessive fluid losses and dehydration • .Whole bowel irrigation (WBI) employs 5 to 20 liters of an electrolyte-balanced solution (e.g., GoLYTELY) to clear the rectal effluent. • Dilution is a relatively ineffective method of gastrointestinal decontamination, but may ameliorate GI distress from gastric irritants. • Neutralization of ingested corrosives is not advised. • The resulta

Growth and development Dr. Tara Hussein Tayeb Assistant Professor Objectives; • To know the developmental stages • to know what is normal and delay development • to be able to identify appropriate developmental age and compare with chronological age Development • The acquirement of new skills (it is qualitative in nature) • Important developmental skills are called ; developmental milestones • It is sequentially • The main objectives for developmental examination is to confirm normal development or further asses abnormal development. ( screening) Assessment of development; • gross motor • vision and fine motor • hearing, speech and language • social, emotional and behavioral. when assessing development In prematurely born infant, the age should be corrected until 2 years of age. DEVELOPMENT rapid in all domains during the first 5 years of life requires; secure, responsive and loving environment A delay or disorder in any one skill area can have an impact on other areas Neglect or child abuse can affect a child at any age but may have particularly profound young children Development; • Median age; is the age when half of a population of children achieve that skill; if a child does not meet the median age for acquiring a skill, it does not mean that this is an abnormality • Red flag age; is the age by which a developmental milestone should have been achieved. Failure to meet a red flag age is a prompt for more detailed assessment to determine if investigation or intervention is required. walking 25 % at 11 mn median 12 mn 97.5% by 18 mn red flag at 18 months Overall, of children who bottom-shuffle, 50% will walk independently by 18 months and 97.5% by 27 months of age. Cephalocaudal progression of voluntary motor control late walkers; should be assessed to exclude the hyper- or hypotonia , causes could be; • cerebral palsy, • global developmental delay, • primary muscle disorder, • joint hypermobility, • developmental dysplasia of the hip. Infants have a number of primitive reflexes at birth, which gradually disappear, whilst postural reflexes* essential for independent sitting and walking emerge. Persistence of primitive reflexes may interfere with normal motor development. Assessing if development is normal • Start with each domain of development separately. • Ask about the sequence of skills achieved as well as those skills likely to develop in the near future. • Determine the level the child has reached for each developmental domain. • Is the child progressing at a similar rate in each domain, or do some developmental domains lag behind the others? • Relate the developmental age with the chronological age Gross Motor Achieving head control Gross Motor Vision& fine Motor follows abject to 90 degrees Vision& fine Motor Hearing ,speech & Language Hearing ,speech & Language social, emotional and behavioral social, emotional and behavioral • By 25–26 weeks’ gestation, a fetus responds to noises and voice. • At birth, a baby startles to loud sounds, and has a marked preference for voices. Hearing ; Risk factors for hearing loss; •Family history of hereditary hearing loss •Genetic syndromes with hearing loss, e.g. Down syndrome •Craniofacial anomalies of ear (including cleft lip and palate) •Admission to NICU (>48 h) •Congenital infection, e.g. CMV (cytomegalovirus), rubella •Bacterial meningitis Methods of hearing examinations; • Automated Otoacoustic Emission(AOAE). It establishes normal cochlear function • Automated Auditory Brain Response (AABR); It tests both the cochlea and the auditory nerve. screening visual examination birth – for structure of eye and red reflex (cataracts impede a red reflex) 6–8 week check – red reflex for cataracts; fixing and following preschool vision screening – checks visual acuity and eye alignment Case; A 18 month old toddler, can sit and play with out support for extended periods of time, speaks in single words, knows about 3 words, he is unable to walk, parent come to asses if he is normal. Dr. Tara Hussein Tayeb Assistant Professor / Suleimani University Infants and young children are vulnerable for nutritional deficiency; • infants have very large nutritional requirement ( double that of adult relative to their body weight) • A term newborn, after the first week of life, requires an intake of 150 ml/kg of milk per day, The proportion of dietary energy intake used for growth 35% in infancy 5% after 1 yr 2% until mid-adolscence Why are infants so nutritionally vulnerable? • Low storage of fat and protein; specially in premature infants • Very high energy requirement; • The brain accounts for two-thirds of basal metabolic rate in a baby at term, and for about 50% at 1 year of age • children with chronic illness have a higher metabolic demand and some poor appetite • Recurrent acute illnesses can cause a cycle of poor appetite and weight loss • energy requirement after complicated surgery or severe acute illness in an infant ,150–200 kcal/kg per day (compared with normal of 95–115 kcal/kg per day) parenteral nutrition maybe needed Long term outcomes of nutritional deficiency; • Affect on linear growth; the mean height of a population reflects its genetics, nutritional status, and health during childhood • cause immunodeficiency and increase infection • Micro and macro nutrient deficiency and their specific manifestations • neonates with intrauterine growth restriction due to placental insufficiency are at latter risk of metabolic syndrome and coronary artery disease. Feeding ; • The WHO recommend exclusive breastfeeding for 6 months, to prevent gastroenteritis and other infections. • Complementary feeds are usually introduced at 4–6 months. Advantages of breast feeding ; • Reduced risk of developing gastroenteritis and otitis media, and lower respiratory tract infections • Protective effect against necrotizing enterocolitis in extremely preterm infants • increase in IQ score by about 3 points • Reduction in risk of SIDS (sudden infant death syndrome) • Lower incidence of obesity, diabetes mellitus and hypertension in later life. • Dramatically improves survival during infancy In low-income countries, Infection protection in breast feeding; • Secretory immunoglobulin A (SLgA) prevents intestinal infection • Human milk oligosaccharides, function as prebiotics, preventing pathogenic bacteria from attaching to mucosal surfaces • white blood cells, can kill micro-organisms • whey proteins (lysozyme and lactoferrin), can kill bacteria, viruses and fungi. Advantages of breast feeding for the mother ; • Promotes close attachment between mother and baby • Delays return of menstruation, allowing iron stores to replenish following pregnancy and childbirth; this also helps with birth spacing • Burns energy stores, helping a return to pre- pregnancy weight • Lowers the risk of developing postnatal depression • reduces postpartum blood loss, by stimulating the secretion of oxytocin • Reduces risk of breast and probably ovarian cancer • Is economical Supplementations; • Vitamin K supplementation at birth is recommended as there is insufficient vitamin K in breast milk to reliably prevent bleeding from vitamin K deficiency (hemorrhagic disease of the newborn). • It is recommended that all infants (and children up to 5 years) in the UK should be given vitamin D supplements. Barriers and complications of breast feeding ; • Dislike of idea of breastfeeding or embarrassment • Other family members cannot help or take part with feeding, • If the mother returns to work, it may make continuing breastfeeding for the recommended length of time more difficult. • Physical breast problems – sore, cracked nipples, thrush, mastitis, inverted nipples, inadequate milk supply. • Breast milk jaundice – mild, self-limiting, unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia; advise to continue breastfeeding; assess infant if prolonged (>2 weeks). • Transmission of drugs – most are transmitted in very low concentration Formula feeding • Most are based on cow’s milk. • E

Obesity in children Dr. Tara Hussein Tayeb Assistant Professor Objectives • Understanding what obesity is • Understanding the physiology of obesity • How to use and interpret BMI charts • Causes of obesity • History and examination • associated features • complications • Management Obesity; 3 • obesity; refers to an excess of fat • BMI; Weight in kilograms ÷ (height in meters)2 • weight-for-height or length (which is particularly useful for the child younger than two years) • measures of regional fat distribution (eg, waist circumference and waist-to-hip ratio) Copyrights apply UK; BMI> 91 % Over weight and > 98% as obese 8 Bioelectrical Impedance Analysis (BIA) Skinfold Calipers Dual-Energy X-ray Absorptiometry (DXA) Other methods of assessing fat body content skin fold thickness percentiles Copyrights apply The term "morbid obesity" is sometimes used to identify individuals with obesity-related comorbidities. However, this term is often inappropriately used as a synonym for severe obesity The prevalence of overweight and obesity would be lower if adjusted to pubertal status, but this adjustment is not routinely performed in clinical care or research ARC NPY AGRP POMC Hypothalamus Appetite stimulating neurons Appetite suppressing neurons Hunger satiety PVN Food intake Energy Expenditure brain stem ghrelin GLP1, PPY, cck Short term regulation Long term stimulation ARC NPY AGRP POMC Hypothalamus Appetite stimulating neurons Appetite suppressing neurons Hunger satiety PVN Food intake Energy Expenditure Low energy expenditure appetite stimulation stop feeding increase energy spending Low leptin High leptin insulin Leptin; secr by adipose brain stem Reward, NA, VTA Causes of obesity Life style Genetic (very rare) High fat, energy-dense diet inflammatory response in the hypothalamic response Leptin unresponsiveness in hypothalamus( resistance) Appetite stimulation ( interpreted as starvation state) Mutated leptin gen Mutated downstream gene Leptin deficiency Leptin resistance Childhood obesity Dysregulation of feeding behavior and energy expenditure most common; low leptin signaling Factors causing obesity; Environmental; • Sugar-sweetened beverages • screen and media time • shortened sleep duration or irregular sleep schedules • Decreased physical activity • Drugs; steroids, antidepressants , antipsychotics, medroxyprogesterone… • Gut microbiota? • Toxins; BPA (common contaminant of foods sold in cans and plastic packaging) • Viruses; Adeno? Genetic • Polygenic factors ; responsible for 40-85% of variation in adiposity, evidence from twin, adoption, and family studies suggests that genetic factors also play a considerable role in the development of childhood obesity • Syndromic obesity; Prader-Willi,, Down syndrome • Monogenic Obesity; leptin deficiency, leptin receptor deficiency, leptin receptor disfunction, Pro-opiomelanocortin deficiency Assessment of an obese child; History; • family history of obesity • dietary habits • Lifestyle; sedentary or active • sleep habits; ( duration and quality) Examination; • BMI • BMI in parents • The trend of BMI ( steady or rapid increase) • assessing for comorbidities; Blood pressure, acanthosis nigricans • Syndromic features Finding and associations with obesity; • Cushing • syndromic • Hypothyroid short stature head aches, vomiting , decreased vision, papilledema, cranial nerve palsies pseudotumor cerebre snoring, inattentiveness, daytime sleepiness, nocturnal enuresis obstructive sleep apnea abdominal right upper quadrant pain, hepatomegaly gall bladder disease Hip and knee pain and limp slipped capital femoral epiphysis, Blount disease ( tibia Vara) Oligomenorrhea, amenorrhea Polycystic ovaries Prader Willi Syndrome Polydipsia, Polyurea, Nocturia Type 2 diabetes Mellitus Binge eating and Purging Eating disorder Insomnia, moodiness Depression Hypertension, Hyperlipidemia Metabolic syndrome acanthosis nigricans Type 2 diabetes Micropenis embedded in suprapubic fat Undescended testes Prader

Student name: ____________________________________________​ ​ ​ Group ​ ​ ​ ​ ​ ​ ​ ​ Component Criteria Max Mark s Marks Awarde d Comments History Taking Chief complaint, present illness, past/family/personal history, review of systems, summarization & provisional diagnosis 30 Physical Examination General and systemic examination, accuracy, correlation with history 25 Case Presentation Organization, clarity, fluency, use of medical terminology, ability to answer questions 15 Discussion & Interpretation Problem identification, differential diagnosis, management plan, evidence-based reasoning 20 Professionalis m & Communicatio n Rapport, ethics, teamwork, punctuality, respect, appearance 10 Total Score: _ ____ / 100 Teacher name: __________________________________________ Date: / /202

1.​ You have just confirmed the diagnosis of cystic fibrosis in a 3-year-old child. The parents are concerned about future pregnancies. You explain to them that the pattern of genetic transmission of cystic fibrosis is: (A) autosomal dominant (B) autosomal recessive (C) X-linked recessive (D) X-linked dominant (E) autosomal recessive in some families and X-linked in others. 2.​ A 12-year-old boy just returned from Boy Scout Camp in Wisconsin. He now has fever, myalgia, and a 10-cm skin lesion which looks like a target. You suspect Lyme disease, most likely contracted by which of the following? (A) ingestion of unripe fruit (B) ingestion of spoiled fruit (C) drinking of contaminated water (D) the bite of a tick (E) the bite of a mosquito. 3.​ A 2-year-old boy presents with extremity swelling and proteinuria and is found on urine analysis. Minimal-change disease is suspected, and you explain to the child’s parents that this diagnosis: (A) is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in childhood (B) has a peak incidence in children between 10 and 15 years of age (C) usually results in end-stage renal disease in 5–10 years (D) is characterized by normal serum lipids and cholesterol (E) typically has a poor response to corticosteroid treatment. 4.​ The following conditions are haemaglobinopathies: A. G6PD deficiency. ​ B. von Willebrand disease. ​ C. Spherocytosis. ​ D. Iron deficiency anemia.​ E. Sickle cell disease. 5.​ Regarding Kawasaki's disease: A. Splenomegaly is a recognized feature.​ B. It is caused by a spirochaete organism.​ C. It is a cause of coronary artery aneurysms.​ D. Fever is not a typical feature.​ E. Is associated with profound thrombocytopaenia. 6. A normal two-year-old child would be expected to: A. Drink from a cup.​ B. Build a tower of 5 blocks.​ C. Copy a circle.​ D. Kick a ball.​ E. Knows 50-200 words. 7. You have just prescribed phenytoin for a 12- year-old boy with new onset of epilepsy. Of the following side effects, which is most likely to occur in this patient? (A) lymphoma syndrome (B) Raynaud phenomenon (C) acute hepatic failure (D) gingival hyperplasia (E) optic atrophy. 8. A term infant is born to a mother who has been using crack cocaine. This infant is at increased risk for which of the following? (A) anemia (B) intrauterine growth retardation (C) hypercalcemia (D) macrosomia (E) postmaturity 9. You examine a 16-year-old male student with a 5-day history of fever, sore throat, and fatigue. Physical examination reveals an exudative tonsillitis and bilateral enlarged and slightly tender posterior cervical lymph nodes. The spleen is palpable 3 cm below the rib cage. Which agent is most likely responsible for this patient’s illness? (A) Group A β-hemolytic streptococcus (B) Adenovirus (C) Toxoplasma gondii (D) Epstein-Barr virus (E) Corynebacterium diphtheriae 10. A term infant requires intubation in the delivery room after aspiration of thick meconium and is brought to the neonatal intensive care unit. Which of the following is the most likely risk factor for meconium aspiration syndrome in this infant? (A) chromosomal anomaly. (B) congenital heart disease (C) cystic fibrosis (D) fetal distress (E) tracheoesophageal fistula 11. A newborn infant with stigmata of Down syndrome has a heart murmur. Which of the following cardiac lesions is most likely in this baby? (A) hypoplastic left heart syndrome (B) total anamolous venous return (C) coarctation of the aorta (D) anamolous coronary artery (E) atrioventricular defect 12. A mother of a 2-month-old wants more information about immunizations. Which of the following statements regarding immunization against Haemophilus influenzae type b (Hib) is correct? (A) It is indicated for high-risk children only. (B) Hib vaccine can be administered effectively as early as 2 months of age. (C) Hib vaccine should not be given to children who have had allergic reactions to eggs. (D) Hib vaccine should not be administered to children with a history of reaction to D

Course name Child Care Course 2. Course leader Assist. Prof. Alan Abdullah Abdulrahman 3. Branch Branch Of Clinical Sciences 4. Year Year 5 undergraduate learners 5. Number of Learners 68 learners 6. Time (in hours) per week 30 hours 7. Venue University campus hall, Dr. Jamal Ahmed Rashid Paediatric Teaching Hospital, Post-call meeting hall and Maternity Hospital NICU. 8. Duration 6 weeks 9. Credit 9 10. Activities 1. Large group sessions (3 hours/day, 5 days/week for 6 weeks. 2. Clinical Sessions including small group teaching, team base learning and examinations (3 hours/day, 5 days/week for 6 weeks. 11. Pre-requisites Successful completion of Year 4. 12. Course leader academic profile https://sites.google.com/a/univsul.edu.iq/alan-abdullah/academic-profile Subject Course book – 2023-2024, University of Sulaimani​ Page 2 University of Sulaimani College Of Medicine Department Of Clinical Sciences 13. Office hour 10 hr 14. Course overview: The curriculum identifies knowledge, skills, and attitudes in child health that should be covered for undergraduate medical course. Many areas will be covered in a specific child health placement. The curriculum is deliberately brief, defining only the core components of child health for medical undergraduates. The course covers topics including health promotion, common health issues, positive parenting, development and behaviour, language, and safeguarding. The learning content is highly engaging, with animations, videos, case studies and questions to help stimulate reflective thinking and support your learning. There are also links to further recommended reading resources. 15. Aim Of the Course: The aim of this course is to teach each candidate how to take history, examine paediatric age group and how to deal with common problems in children including effective communication skills with parents and children. We are enabling them to provide quality and respectful childhood and newborn care services. 16. Course academic staffs: 1 professor, 6 Assistant professors, 3 lecturers and 9 external lecturers. 17. Learning Objectives: By the end of this course, student should be able to: Subject Course book – 2023-2024, University of Sulaimani​ Page 3 University of Sulaimani College Of Medicine Department Of Clinical Sciences 1. Describe how to diagnose correctly the common conditions and diseases affecting children; 2. Describe how to manage common conditions and diseases affecting children. 3. Explain how to measure and monitor growth and development in a child. 4. Explain how to develop and give relevant health messages to individuals, families, and communities. 5. Discuss the principles of counselling. 6. Describe how to effectively organize and carry out immunization activities. 7. Explain how to minimize the common accidents and injuries occurring to children in your catchment area. 8. Describe rehabilitative activities that are needed for children with physical or mental challenges. 18. WHY TAKE THIS COURSE? This course is designed to equip you with the knowledge, skills, and attitudes necessary to enable you provide effective and efficient promotive, preventive, and curative child health services. It is intended to provide you with the following: 1.​ Well prepared self-guided materials on child health. 2.​ A quick reference for use so that you can effectively and promptly diagnose, treat and refer children with common childhood diseases. 3.​ Simple diagnostic and treatment guidelines on common childhood diseases which you can internalize through self-guided learning. 19. Without her warm-­heartedness, I would not have been able to pass through the journey of life. – My sisters and brothers, especially Ayman, my teacher and mentor. • To my dear relatives, in particular, my uncles Ali Saadoon and the great teacher Muhammad Abdallah. I will never forget their support. • To all my friends, especially my soulmates, Marwan Abdulrahman and Samer Jawad. • To the respectable teachers and wonderful students at Basra Medical College. I am proud to be a part of them. • To those people who still live in my heart and will stay there forever. vii Foreword Any book that is written should solve a problem. And if the book solves that prob- lem it is worthy of publication and purchase. Such is the case of Essential Clinical Skills in Pediatrics by Dr. Anwar Qais. As a young medical student, Dr. Qais found that he needed a reference that would assist him in history taking and physical exam skills. That was the problem—no such reference existed. In subsequent years, and with additional training in pediatrics, he prepared a volume that solved that prob- lem. He has done so in a fashion that is both useful and elegant. The book, although brief, covers all the important issues in pediatrics. There are two parts: one on history-taking skills, with specific direction based on symptoms being assessed.
